Question:
9. What is the term for a metastatic cancer in which no primary site can be identified by core (thick-needle) biopsy?
a. Cancer of unknown primary (CUP)
b. Primary mediastinal carcinoma
c. Occult carcinoma
d. Pancancer syndrome
e. Embryonal carcinoma
Reference: Strojan, P., & Hočevar, M. (Eds.). (2018). Onkologija: Učbenik za študente medicine [Oncology: Textbook for medical students] (1st ed.). Onkološki inštitut Ljubljana.
Context: Cancer of unknown primary origin is a histologically confirmed metastatic cancer, in which the diagnosis of malignancy is made from the metastasis, and despite today’s available diagnostic methods, the site of the primary tumor cannot be found. This represents a heterogeneous group of diseases that are generally incurable and have a poor prognosis. Good collaboration between the clinician and pathologist is very important in the search for the primary site.

Question:
10. At what age does the SVIT colorectal-cancer screening program begin for men and women in Slovenia?
a. 40 years
b. 45 years
c. 50 years
d. 55 years
e. 60 years
Reference: Strojan, P., & Hočevar, M. (Eds.). (2018). Onkologija: Učbenik za študente medicine [Oncology: Textbook for medical students] (1st ed.). Onkološki inštitut Ljubljana.
Context:
Hidden blood in stool can be an early sign of potentially serious changes in the colon and rectum. Numerous studies have shown that regular stool screening between the ages of 50–74, followed by colonoscopy and treatment of any detected lesions in those with positive results, reduces mortality from this cancer.
The SVIT program, led and coordinated by the National Institute of Public Health, is organized in accordance with European guidelines for quality assurance in colorectal cancer screening. Every two years, men and women aged 50–74 are invited to participate by taking two stool samples at home and sending them by mail to the central laboratory of the SVIT program. There, they perform an immunochemical test for hidden blood. All those with positive results undergo colonoscopy and, if needed, are directed to treatment.

Question:
11. Which of the following is one of the two most common nonmelanoma skin cancers?
a. Merkel cell carcinoma
b. Basal cell carcinoma
c. Kaposi sarcoma
d. Dermatofibrosarcoma protuberans
e. Sebaceous carcinoma
Reference: Strojan, P., & Hočevar, M. (Eds.). (2018). Onkologija: Učbenik za študente medicine [Oncology: Textbook for medical students] (1st ed.). Onkološki inštitut Ljubljana.
Context:
Non-melanoma skin cancer (NMSC) is a much more common form of skin cancer than cutaneous melanoma. About 80 percent of non-melanoma skin cancers are basal cell carcinoma (BCC), while squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) is four to five times less common.
These are usually slow-growing tumors in light-skinned populations that rarely metastasize, yet they still cause significant morbidity. They grow in a locally destructive manner and often recur, which can leave undesirable cosmetic consequences.

Question:
12. Among solid tumors in children, which is most frequently diagnosed?
a. Wilms tumor
b. Osteosarcoma
c. Brain tumors 
d. Neuroblastoma
e. Rhabdomyosarcoma
Reference: Strojan, P., & Hočevar, M. (Eds.). (2018). Onkologija: Učbenik za študente medicine [Oncology: Textbook for medical students] (1st ed.). Onkološki inštitut Ljubljana.
Context: The most common type of cancer in children is leukemia (30%); among solid tumors, central nervous system tumors are the most common (21%), followed by lymphomas (12%). Other solid tumors are rarer, such as neuroblastoma (7%), soft tissue sarcoma (7%), bone sarcoma (7%), nephroblastoma (7%), retinoblastoma (3%), and germ cell tumors (3%).

Question:
13. Which staging system is used for Hodgkin lymphoma?
a. Dukes staging
b. Ann Arbor staging
c. TNM staging
d. FIGO staging
e. Ranson’s criteria
Reference: Strojan, P., & Hočevar, M. (Eds.). (2018). Onkologija: Učbenik za študente medicine [Oncology: Textbook for medical students] (1st ed.). Onkološki inštitut Ljubljana.
Context: The diagnosis is established with a fine-needle aspiration biopsy of the enlarged lymph node, but to determine the histological subtype, a histological examination of the excised lymph node is required. The stage of the disease according to the Ann Arbor classification is determined with diagnostic tests: abdominal and peripheral lymph node ultrasound, chest X-ray, PET-CT, chest CT (for assessment of the lung parenchyma), CT or MRI of the neck, abdomen and pelvis, and examination of the upper aerodigestive tract (ENT examination). In higher stages of the disease, a bone marrow biopsy is required.

Question:
14. Which growth factor is administered subcutaneously to reduce febrile neutropenia?
a. Erythropoietin
b. Interleukin-2 (IL-2)
c. Granulocyte-colony stimulating factor (G-CSF)
d. Granulocyte–macrophage colony-stimulating factor (GM-CSF)
e. Thrombopoietin analogs
Reference: Strojan, P., & Hočevar, M. (Eds.). (2018). Onkologija: Učbenik za študente medicine [Oncology: Textbook for medical students] (1st ed.). Onkološki inštitut Ljubljana.
Context: Supportive drugs that can prevent the development of severe neutropenia and consequently febrile neutropenia are growth factors that stimulate the proliferation and differentiation of granulocytes and macrophages (GM-CSF) or granulocytes (G-CSF). Preventive use of granulocyte growth factors (filgrastim, pegfilgrastim) is recommended during chemotherapy if the risk of febrile neutropenia exceeds 20%, or in patients in whom continuation of treatment with effective doses of chemotherapy would otherwise not be possible (e.g., patients who have already experienced febrile neutropenia, since its recurrence can lead to severe, life-threatening complications, or those in whom, due to prolonged neutropenia, treatment cannot continue at regular time intervals).

Question:
15. Which effect of whole-body irradiation is dose-dependent but has no threshold (i.e., stochastic)?
a. Hair loss
b. Radiation-induced fibrosis
c. Radiation-induced second malignancy
d. Acute radiation dermatitis
e. Radiation pneumonitis
Reference: Strojan, P., & Hočevar, M. (Eds.). (2018). Onkologija: Učbenik za študente medicine [Oncology: Textbook for medical students] (1st ed.). Onkološki inštitut Ljubljana.
Context:
Stochastic effects of radiation are the result of mutations in the DNA molecule in cells. These cells retain the ability to divide and do not die through mitotic death. Over time, these mutations can accumulate and lead to the malignant transformation of cells and thus the development of cancer. These events are random, and the probability of mutations increases with the received dose. In any case, this dose must be low enough not to kill the cells. In principle, there is no dose so low that it could not potentially cause mutations. Therefore, the relationship between the probability of mutation occurrence and the dose of radiation is linear (see figure 23). The consequences of mutations are malignant cell transformations, which means that this effect is not dependent on the dose of radiation; only the probability of the effect’s occurrence is dose-dependent. As the received dose of radiation increases, the probability of stochastic effects also increases.

It is also characteristic of stochastic effects that they occur with a significant time delay; for example, the interval until the appearance of blood cancers is about seven years and for solid tumors about ten years. When a mutation occurs in germ cells, this mutation is passed on to offspring, and in this case, we refer to it as hereditary radiation effects. English: Stochastic Effects of Radiation Are The Result of Mutations in the DNA Molecule and Cells.Thes Cells Retain the Ability to Divide and Do Not Die Through Mitotic Death.Over Time, these Mutations Can Accumulate and Lead to the Malignant Transformation of Cells and Thus the Development of Cancer.Thesis Are Random, and the Probability of Mutations Increases with the Received Port.And Any Case, this gains must be low enough not to kill the cells.And principle, there is no reaches are low that it could not potentially caus mutations.Therefore, The Relationship Between the Probability of Mutation Occurrence and the Radiation is Linear (See Figure 23).The Consequence of Mutations Are Malignant Cell Transformations, which Means that this effect is not depending on the radiation;Only The Probability of the Effect's Occurrence is achieving.AS The Received Reaches of Radiation Increases, The Probability of Stochastic Effects Also Increases.

It is also characteristic of stochastic effects that they occur with a significant time delay;for Example, The Interval Until the Appearance of Blood Cancers is About Seven Years and For Solid Tumors About Ten Years.When a Mutation Occurs and Germ Cells, this mutation is passed on to offspring, and and this time, we refer to it as hereditary radiation effects.

Question:
16. What is the principal purpose of tumor lysis syndrome prophylaxis in hematologic malignancies?
a. Prevent venous thromboembolism
b. Prevent infection from neutropenia
c. Prevent metabolic complications from rapid cell lysis
d. Prevent mucositis
e. Prevent cardiotoxicity
Text Answer: Prevent metabolic complications from rapid cell lysis
Reference: Strojan, P., & Hočevar, M. (Eds.). (2018). Onkologija: Učbenik za študente medicine [Oncology: Textbook for medical students] (1st ed.). Onkološki inštitut Ljubljana.
Context: Tumor lysis syndrome or rapid tumor cell breakdown syndrome can cause severe metabolic disturbances. It arises from the accelerated breakdown of tumor cells and the release of intracellular contents into the bloodstream. It most commonly occurs at the start of treatment for rapidly growing and chemotherapy-sensitive cancers, such as acute leukemias and highly malignant lymphomas, especially Burkitt lymphoma; it is rare in solid tumors.

Question:
17. What is the most common site of distant metastasis for breast cancer?
a. Brain
b. Liver
c. Kidney
d. Skin
e. Bone
Reference: Strojan, P., & Hočevar, M. (Eds.). (2018). Onkologija: Učbenik za študente medicine [Oncology: Textbook for medical students] (1st ed.). Onkološki inštitut Ljubljana.
Context:
Invasive cancers are characterized by local growth and the ability to metastasize.
Breast cancer spreads via the lymphatic system to regional lymph nodes (axillary, internal mammary, and supraclavicular nodes). Hematogenous spread most commonly occurs to the lungs, liver, bones, and brain.
The clinical presentation depends on the size of the primary tumor, lymph node involvement, invasion into surrounding structures, and metastases in distant organs.

Question:
18. What does “adjuvant” systemic therapy mean in oncology?
a. Therapy given before any local treatment
b. Palliative care only
c. Therapy given after primary local treatment to eradicate micrometastases
d. Therapy given to treat metastatic disease only
e. Therapy given only for symptom control
Text Answer: Therapy given after primary local treatment to eradicate micrometastases
Reference: Strojan, P., & Hočevar, M. (Eds.). (2018). Onkologija: Učbenik za študente medicine [Oncology: Textbook for medical students] (1st ed.). Onkološki inštitut Ljubljana.
Context:
Adjuvant (complementary) systemic (postoperative, post-radiation) therapy is used after radical surgical removal of the cancer or radical radiation therapy.
We must recognize that cancer is a systemic disease and that cancer cells always enter the bloodstream. Adjuvant systemic therapy is intended to destroy individual cells in the body, so-called micrometastases. In adjuvant systemic therapy, cytostatics, hormonal drugs, and targeted therapies are used.

Question:
19. Which psychiatric symptom is most commonly seen immediately after an adult cancer diagnosis?
a. Mania
b. Anxiety
c. Obsessive-compulsive behaviors
d. Schizophrenia
e. Dementia
Reference: Strojan, P., & Hočevar, M. (Eds.). (2018). Onkologija: Učbenik za študente medicine [Oncology: Textbook for medical students] (1st ed.). Onkološki inštitut Ljubljana.
Context: Psychological problems that patients face can include fear, anxiety, and depression, which must be properly identified and managed in each individual case. If necessary, we advise involving additional experts (psychiatrist, psychologist).

Question:
20. Which cytokine is secreted by many tumor cells to suppress local immune activity?
a. IL-2
b. IFN-γ
c. TNF-α
d. IL-10
e. GM-CSF
Reference: Ouyang, W., Rutz, S., Crellin, N. K., Valdez, P. A., & Hymowitz, S. G. (2011). Regulation and functions of the IL-10 family of cytokines in inflammation and disease. Annual Review of Immunology, 29, 71–109.
Context: "IL-10 is a potent anti-inflammatory cytokine produced by various immune cells and tumor cells. It inhibits the function of antigen-presenting cells and suppresses the expression of pro-inflammatory cytokines, thereby facilitating tumor immune evasion."

Question:
21. Name two typical acute hematologic toxicities of adjuvant breast cancer chemotherapy.
a. Thrombocytosis and polycythemia
b. Neutropenia and anemia
c. Eosinophilia and basophilia
d. Leukemoid reaction and thrombocytopenia
e. Pancytopenia and hemolytic anemia
Reference: Strojan, P., & Hočevar, M. (Eds.). (2018). Onkologija: Učbenik za študente medicine [Oncology: Textbook for medical students] (1st ed.). Onkološki inštitut Ljubljana.
Context:
BONE MARROW SUPPRESSION
The action of cytostatics on rapidly dividing bone marrow cells results in bone marrow suppression (myelosuppression). Bone marrow suppression occurs in 80 to 100 percent of all patients treated with chemotherapy. The suppression of blood cell maturation is evident in the peripheral blood as leukopenia or neutropenia, anemia, and thrombocytopenia. The maturation of all types of blood cells in the bone marrow can be affected, but neutropenia is the most common during treatment with standard doses of cytostatics. Bone marrow suppression usually occurs 7 to 14 days after chemotherapy, but with some cytostatics (carmustine, lomustine, melphalan) it is most pronounced later—between the 28th and 35th day after starting treatment.

Question:
22. Which thyroid cancer subtype is most frequently diagnosed in children exposed to Chernobyl on neck ultrasound?
a. Follicular thyroid carcinoma
b. Medullary thyroid carcinoma
c. Papillary thyroid carcinoma
d. Anaplastic thyroid carcinoma
e. Hurthle cell carcinoma
Reference: Stezhko, V. A., Buglova, E. E., Danilova, L. I., Drozd, V. M., Krysenko, N. A., Lesnikova, N. R., Minenko, V. F., Ostapenko, V. A., Tronko, N. D., Tuttle, R. M., & Williams, D. (2004). A cohort study of thyroid cancer and other thyroid diseases after the Chornobyl accident: Objectives, design and methods. Radiation Research, 161(4), 481–492.
Context: "The thyroid tumours that were discovered after the Chernobyl nuclear accident were virtually all (95%) of the papillary carcinoma type."

Question:
23. Which of the following is the single most important risk factor for hepatocellular carcinoma worldwide?
a. Chronic hepatitis C virus (HCV) infection
b. Chronic hepatitis B virus (HBV) infection
c. Nonalcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD)
d. Excessive alcohol consumption
e. Aflatoxin exposure
Reference: Strojan, P., & Hočevar, M. (Eds.). (2018). Onkologija: Učbenik za študente medicine [Oncology: Textbook for medical students] (1st ed.). Onkološki inštitut Ljubljana.
Context:
The precise mechanisms of carcinogenesis in HCC at the molecular level are not yet fully understood. Venous obstruction, steroids, and others are considered to play a role in the development of HCC.
Known risk factors for the development of HCC are:
Cirrhotic liver changes
Hemochromatosis
Wilson's disease
Infection with hepatitis B (HBV) and C (HCV) viruses
Alcoholism
The highest risk for developing primary HCC is associated with HBV infection at birth and in early childhood
Exposure to environmental carcinogens (vinyl chloride, contraceptives, anabolic steroids)

Question:
24. What is the standard combined modality for treating localized glioblastoma (e.g., T1N0M0)?
a. Surgical resection alone
b. Radiotherapy alone
c. Resection + radiotherapy with concurrent and adjuvant temozolomide
d. Chemotherapy alone (lomustine-based)
e. Stereotactic radiosurgery alone
Text Answer: Resection + radiotherapy with concurrent and adjuvant temozolomide
Reference: Strojan, P., & Hočevar, M. (Eds.). (2018). Onkologija: Učbenik za študente medicine [Oncology: Textbook for medical students] (1st ed.). Onkološki inštitut Ljubljana.
Context: Temelj zdravljenja visoko malignih gliomov je kirurška resekcija, ki ji sledita radioterapija in kemoterapija. Kirurško zdravljenje ima tri cilje: postavitev končne diagnoze, citoredukcija in izboljšanje bolnikovega stanja. Zaradi heterogenosti tumorja obstaja nevarnost napačnega vzorčenja, predvsem pri biopsiji. English: The foundation of high -malignant gliomas is surgical resection, followed by radiotherapy and chemotherapy.Surgical treatment has three goals: final diagnosis, cytoreduction and improving the patient's condition.Due to the heterogeneity of the tumor, there is a risk of wrong sampling, especially in biopsy.

Question:
1. Which cranial nerve is most commonly affected first during uncal herniation?
a) CN IV
b) CN III
c) CN V
d) CN II
e) CN VI
Reference: Uncal Herniation - StatPearls - NCBI
Context: As the uncus of the temporal lobe herniates medially, it compresses the ipsilateral oculomotor nerve (cranial nerve III) against the tentorium cerebelli. This compression typically results in early signs of oculomotor nerve palsy, characterized by ipsilateral pupillary dilation due to compression of the superficial parasympathetic fibers, followed by impairment of extraocular movements (e.g., eye deviation down and out).

Question:
What structure is typically damaged in Weber's syndrome?
a) Medulla oblongata
b) Pons
c) Midbrain (mesencephalon)
d) Subthalamic nucleus
e) Basal ganglia
Reference: Weber Syndrome - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf
Context: Weber syndrome is a midbrain stroke characterized by crossed hemiplegia along with oculomotor nerve deficits.

Question:
A patient has difficulty closing the eye, mouth asymmetry, and cannot wrinkle the forehead on one side. Where is the facial nerve lesion?
a) In the cerebellopontine angle
b) In the brainstem nucleus
c) After exiting the stylomastoid foramen
d) In the internal acoustic meatus
e) In the contralateral motor cortex
Reference: Facial Nerve Palsy - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf
Context: The distinction between central and peripheral facial palsy is crucial. In a peripheral facial nerve lesion (e.g., Bell's palsy, lesion after the stylomastoid foramen), the entire ipsilateral half of the face is affected, resulting in inability to wrinkle the forehead, difficulty closing the eye (lagophthalmos), and mouth deviation. In contrast, a central facial lesion (e.g., stroke affecting the motor cortex) typically spares the forehead as the upper face receives bilateral cortical innervation

Question:
Which clinical feature is typical of Wallenberg syndrome (lateral medullary syndrome)?
a) Hemiplegia and facial droop
b) Quadriplegia
c) Bilateral vision loss
d) Ipsilateral Horner's syndrome and contralateral pain and temperature loss
e) Bilateral Babinski sign
Text Answer: Ipsilateral Horner's syndrome and contralateral pain and temperature loss
Reference: Wallenberg Syndrome - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf
Context: Wallenberg syndrome is a complex neurological disorder that typically results from a stroke affecting the lateral medulla. Key clinical features include ipsilateral Horner's syndrome (miosis, ptosis, anhidrosis), ipsilateral ataxia, ipsilateral dysphagia and dysarthria, and contralateral loss of pain and temperature sensation over the body

Question:
What cranial nerve nuclei are located in the pons?
a) CN I, II, III
b) CN V, VI, VII (and part of VIII)
c) CN IX, X, XI
d) CN III, IV
e) CN II, IV, VI
Text Answer: CN V, VI, VII (and part of VIII)
Reference: Waxman, S. G. (2020). Clinical Neuroanatomy (29th ed.). McGraw-Hill Education.
Context: The pons contains the nuclei for cranial nerves V (trigeminal), VI (abducens), VII (facial), and VIII (vestibulocochlear, specifically the vestibular nuclei and the cochlear nuclei).

Question:
Which of the following is not part of the Papez circuit?
a) Hippocampus
b) Fornix
c) Mammillary bodies
d) Cingulum
e) All listed structures are part of the Papez circuit
Text Answer: All listed structures are part of the Papez circuit
Reference: Lanting, C. L., O'Keefe, J., & Koning, P. (2020). The Papez circuit: A computational neuroanatomical view. Frontiers in Systems Neuroscience, 14,
Context: The Papez circuit, traditionally described as a major limbic pathway for processing emotion and memory, includes the following key structures: the hippocampus (via the subiculum), the fornix, the mammillary bodies of the hypothalamus, the mammillothalamic tract, the anterior thalamic nuclei, and the cingulate gyrus (which contains the cingulum as a major fiber bundle connecting it).

Question:
What gait pattern is most likely in a patient with cerebellar ataxia?
a) Festinating gait
b) Steppage gait
c) Waddling gait
d) Wide-based, unsteady gait
e) Spastic scissoring gait
Reference: Cerebellar Ataxia - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf
Context: The gait in cerebellar ataxia is typically wide-based, irregular, and unsteady, often described as 'drunken' or 'reeling.' Patients often struggle with tandem gait and may deviate to either side.

Question:
Which part of the brain is affected if a patient has constructional apraxia and cannot draw a clock?
a) Dominant temporal lobe
b) Non-dominant parietal lobe
c) Dominant frontal lobe
d) Cerebellum
e) Occipital lobe
Reference: Constructional Apraxia - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf
Context: Constructional apraxia is the inability or difficulty to build, assemble, or draw complex figures, despite having intact motor and sensory function. It is most commonly associated with lesions of the non-dominant (typically right) parietal lobe.

Question:
A patient presents with ptosis, a dilated pupil, and an eye that looks "down and out." Which nerve is most likely affected?
a) CN VI
b) CN IV
c) CN III
d) CN V1
e) CN II
Reference: Oculomotor Nerve Palsy - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf
Context: Complete oculomotor nerve (CN III) palsy is characterized by ptosis (due to paralysis of the levator palpebrae superioris), a dilated and fixed pupil (due to paralysis of the constrictor pupillae), and the eye resting in a position of 'down and out' (due to paralysis of most extraocular muscles, leaving only the lateral rectus and superior oblique muscles unopposed).

Question:
What is the likely diagnosis if a patient has bilateral pale optic discs and paresthesia in one leg, with brisk reflexes and an extensor plantar response?
a) Single spinal lesion
b) Diabetic polyneuropathy
c) Diffuse central nervous system disorder
d) Brainstem stroke
e) Optic neuritis
Reference: Multiple Sclerosis - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf
Context: Multiple sclerosis (MS) is a chronic inflammatory demyelinating disease of the central nervous system that typically presents with a range of neurological deficits, often disseminated in space and time. Common presenting symptoms include optic neuritis (which can lead to optic atrophy and pale optic discs), sensory disturbances such as paresthesia (which can be unilateral), and pyramidal tract signs (e.g., brisk reflexes, spasticity, and extensor plantar responses), indicating upper motor neuron involvement

Question:
What neurological condition typically causes a "claw hand"?
a) Radial nerve lesion
b) Ulnar nerve lesion
c) Median nerve lesion
d) Cervical radiculopathy
e) Musculocutaneous nerve lesion
Reference: Claw Hand(Archived) - PubMed
Context: Ulnar nerve palsy... can result in severe complications such as claw hand deformities. When the ulnar nerve is injured, the muscles innervated by the nerve begin to weaken. This leads to an imbalance between the strong extrinsic muscles (i.e., extensor digitorum communis) and the weakened intrinsic muscles (i.e., interossei and lumbricals). This imbalance is characterized clinically by metacarpophalangeal (MCP) hyperextension and proximal interphalangeal (PIP), and distal interphalangeal (DIP) flexion.

Question:
Which sensory fibers transmit vibration and proprioception?
a) Thin, unmyelinated fibers
b) Thin, myelinated fibers
c) Thick, myelinated sensory fibers
d) Thick, unmyelinated fibers
e) Autonomic fibers
Reference: Purves, D., Augustine, G. J., Fitzpatrick, D., Katz, L. C., LaMantia, A. S., McNamara, J. O., & Williams, S. M. (Eds.). (2001). Neuroscience (2nd ed.). Sinauer Associates
Context: Large, myelinated afferents (Aβ fibers), which are thick, myelinated sensory fibers, are responsible for transmitting information about touch, pressure, vibration, and proprioception. Their large diameter and myelination allow for rapid conduction of these precise sensory modalities.

Question:
What muscle is tested by the Froment’s sign?
a) Opponens pollicis
b) Flexor pollicis longus
c) Adductor pollicis (ulnar nerve)
d) Abductor pollicis brevis
e) First dorsal interosseous
Reference: Pidgeon, T. S., & Pidgeon, J. (2018). Clinical assessment of ulnar neuropathy. Journal of Clinical Neurophysiology, 35(2),
Context: Froment’s sign is positive when the patient compensates for a weak or paralyzed adductor pollicis muscle (ulnar nerve innervated) by flexing the interphalangeal joint of the thumb using the median nerve innervated flexor pollicis longus to maintain a pinch grip, as the examiner attempts to pull a piece of paper from between the thumb and index finger.

Question:
Which cranial nerve is responsible for lateral eye movement?
a) CN III
b) CN IV
c) CN VI
d) CN II
e) CN V1
Reference: Abducens Nerve Palsy - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf
Context: The abducens nerve (cranial nerve VI) is a motor nerve that innervates the lateral rectus muscle, which is responsible for abduction (lateral eye movement) of the eye.

Question:
Which type of memory is tested by asking a patient to repeat a series of digits backward?
a) Semantic memory
b) Working memory (attention)
c) Episodic memory
d) Long-term memory
e) Prospective memory
Reference: Lezak, M. D., Howieson, D. B., Bigler, D., & Tranel, D. (2012). Neuropsychological Assessment (5th ed.)
Context: The digit span backward task is a common neuropsychological test primarily used to assess working memory capacity and attention. It requires not only the temporary storage of information (as in digit span forward) but also the manipulation and reordering of that information in the mind.

Question:
What does a positive Romberg test suggest?
a) Cerebellar lesion
b) Impaired proprioception or dorsal column dysfunction
c) Vestibular disorder
d) Cortical stroke
e) Visual agnosia
Text Answer: Impaired proprioception or dorsal column dysfunction
Reference: Romberg Test - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf
Context: A positive Romberg sign indicates sensory ataxia, meaning the patient loses balance when their eyes are closed because they are unable to use visual input to compensate for a deficit in proprioception (the sense of position). This specifically suggests a lesion in the dorsal columns of the spinal cord or in the peripheral nerves that convey proprioceptive information

Question:
Which spinal level corresponds to the dermatome over the nipples?
a) C6
b) T6
c) T4
d) T2
e) T10
Reference: Anatomy, Skin, Dermatomes - StatPearls
Context: T4: Nipple

Question:
What is the main function of the basal ganglia?
a) Sensory processing
b) Coordination of movement and muscle tone
c) Memory formation
d) Visual tracking
e) Language comprehension
Text Answer: Coordination of movement and muscle tone
Reference: Basal Ganglia - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf
Context: The basal ganglia are a group of subcortical nuclei that play a crucial role in the initiation and coordination of voluntary movements, as well as the regulation of muscle tone.

Question:
What is the primary neurotransmitter used in the parasympathetic nervous system?
a) Dopamine
b) Noradrenaline
c) Acetylcholine
d) Serotonin
e) Glutamate
Reference: Neuroanatomy, Parasympathetic Nervous System - StatPearls
Context: The parasympathetic nervous system (PNS) uses acetylcholine as its neurotransmitter for pre- and postganglionic neurons activating muscarinic receptors.

Question:
What muscle is innervated by the long thoracic nerve?
a) Rhomboid major
b) Latissimus dorsi
c) Trapezius
d) Serratus anterior
e) Levator scapulae
Reference: Long Thoracic Nerve - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf
Context: The long thoracic nerve is a motor nerve that arises from the brachial plexus (C5, C6, C7 nerve roots) and exclusively innervates the serratus anterior muscle.

Question:
1. Which of the following is a common symptom of acromegaly?
a) Galactorrhea
b) Hypotension
c) Sleep apnea
d) Alopecia
e) Dry skin
Reference: Interna medicina (6. izd., str. XXXVIII, 1693). (2022). Medicinska fakulteta; Buča.
Context:
Pljuča. Med razvojem bolezni se povečajo tudi pljuča. Zožijo se male dihalne poti, lahko se pojavijo pljučna hipertenzija, dodatne dihalne motnje lahko povzroča kifoza. Pogosta spremljajoča pojav sta hudo smrčanje in destruktivna spalna apneja, ki sta posledica zožitve dihalnih poti zaradi velikega jezika, prognatije, zadebelitve mehkega dela zgornjih dihalnih poti, pri nekaterih pa prevladuje centralni vzrok. Motnjo v veliki meri odpravi uspešno zdravljenje akromegalije, zelo učinkovita pa je tudi uporaba naprave, ki omogoča stalni pozitiven tlak v dihalnih poteh (CPAP). 

English: Lungs. As the disease progresses, the lungs also enlarge. The small airways become narrowed, pulmonary hypertension can develop, and kyphosis may cause additional respiratory problems. A common accompanying finding is loud snoring and obstructive sleep apnea, which result from narrowing of the airways due to a large tongue, prognathism, and thickening of the soft tissues of the upper airway; in some cases, a central cause predominates. Successful treatment of acromegaly largely resolves these disturbances, and the use of a device that provides continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) is also very effective.

Question:

a) Visual field examination
b) Serum calcium measurement
c) MRI with gadolinium contrast

Reference: Interna medicina (6. izd., str. XXXVIII, 1693). (2022). Medicinska fakulteta; Buča.
Context:
Prikaz z magnetno resonanco (MR) je metoda izbora pri 
utemeljenem sumu na ekspanzivni proces v hipotalamič­
no-hipofiznem območju. Prikaže mehke strukture, ki iz_xFFFE_polnjujejo turško sedlo in supraselarni predel. Prikaže tu_xFFFE_di morebitno lateralno širjenje in način rasti tumorja. Mož­
ganske strukture vključno s hipofizo so vidne bolj natančno 
kakor na posnetkih z rentgensko računalniško tomografijo 
(CT). Preiskava bolnika ne obremenjuje z žarčenjem. Kost_xFFFE_ne strukture pa se z magnetno resonanco slabše prikažejo 
kot s CT. Slikanje poteka v koronarnih in sagitalnih ravni_xFFFE_nah, kontrastno sredstvo je gadolinij. 

English: 
Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is the method of choice for
reasonable suspicion of an expansive process in the hypothalamic-pituitary region. It shows the soft structures that fill the sella turcica and the suprasellar region. It also shows any lateral spread and the way the tumor grows. Brain structures, including the pituitary gland, are visible more precisely than on X-ray computed tomography (CT) images. The examination does not expose the patient to radiation. However, bone structures are less clearly visible with magnetic resonance than with CT. Imaging is performed in the coronal and sagittal planes, and the contrast agent is gadolinium.

English:

Question:
8. What is a common complication of long-term glucocorticoid therapy that can be prevented or mitigated?
a) Growth retardation in children
b) Peptic ulcer
c) Osteoporosis
d) Suppression of the HPA axis
e) Diabetes mellitus
Reference: Harrison’s Principles of Internal Medicine, 20th ed.
Context:
“Osteoporosis is the most common long-term complication of systemic glucocorticoid therapy and is both preventable and partially reversible. Glucocorticoid-induced bone loss occurs rapidly within the first 6–12 months of therapy. Prophylaxis should include adequate intake of calcium (1,200–1,500 mg/day) and vitamin D (800–1,000 IU/day), as well as initiation of bisphosphonate therapy (e.g., alendronate 5–10 mg/day or risedronate 5 mg/day orally) in adults expected to receive the equivalent of ≥ 5 mg/day of prednisone for ≥ 3 months. Weight-bearing exercise and minimization of glucocorticoid dose to the lowest effective amount are also recommended.”
“Although peptic ulceration, growth retardation (in children), hypothalamic–pituitary–adrenal (HPA) axis suppression, and hyperglycemia are recognized adverse effects of glucocorticoids, only glucocorticoid-induced osteoporosis has an established prophylactic regimen that reliably reduces fracture risk.”

Reference: Interna medicina (6. izd., str. XXXVIII, 1693). (2022). Medicinska fakulteta; Buča.
Context:
Če raste tumor supraselarno, potisne diafragmo sedla navzgor, nateg dure in večjih žil pa povzroča glavobol. 
Tumor pritiska na križišče vidnih živcev. Najprej prizadene 
osrednja prekrižana vlakna vidnih živcev, kar povzroči izpad zgornjih temporalnih kvadrantov vidnega polja. Nadaljnja rast tumorja okvari vsa prekrižana vlakna v kiazmi, 
razvije se popolna bitemporalna hemianopsija. Včasih raste tumor asimetrično, zato prizadene en vidni živec bolj 
kot drugega. Počasi se pojavijo v vidnem polju še dodatni 
skotomi, vidni živci atrofirajo, sledi popolna slepota. Zgodi 
se, da bolnik obišče zdravnika šele v tem stadiju. 
Rast tumorja v hipotalamus lahko povzroči diabetes insipidus, kar pogosto vidimo pri kraniofaringeomih. Če vrašča tumor v hipotalamus in proti tretjemu ventriklu, lahko povzro­či motnje v odtoku likvorja in hidrocefalus s povečanim intrakranialnim tlakom, kar je redko. Včasih je moteno izločanju 
hipotalamičnih sproščevalnih in zaviralnih hormonov, lahko 
se pojavijo velik apetit s polifagijo in debelost, včasih anoreksija, motnje v regulaciji telesne temperature, nespečnost ali 
čezmerna zaspanost. Razvijejo se lahko tudi motnje v regulaciji vegetativnega živčevja s čezmernim znojenjem, akrocianozo, tahikardijami itn. Nekateri bolniki se psihično spremenijo. Tumor se lahko širi lateralno v kavernozni sinus, v katerem potekajo tretji, četrti in šesti možganski živec, zato nastanejo motnje pri premikanju očesnih zrkel in dvojni vid. Obsežno širjenje tumorja lahko povzroči temporalno epilepsijo. 
Kadar tumor razžre dno turškega sedla in vrašča v obnosne 
votline, se lahko pojavita likvoreja in meningitis.

English:

If the tumor grows suprasellar, it pushes the diaphragm of the sella upward, and the tension of the dura and major vessels causes headache.
The tumor presses on the optic nerve junction. First, the central crossed fibers of the optic nerves are affected, which causes loss of the upper temporal quadrants of the visual field. Further growth of the tumor damages all crossed fibers in the chiasm,
complete bitemporal hemianopia develops. Sometimes the tumor grows asymmetrically, so one optic nerve is affected more
than the other. Slowly, additional
scotomas appear in the visual field, the optic nerves atrophy, and complete blindness follows.
Sometimes, the patient visits a doctor only at this stage.
Tumor growth into the hypothalamus can cause diabetes insipidus, which is often seen in craniopharyngiomas. If the tumor grows into the hypothalamus and towards the third ventricle, it can cause disorders in the outflow of cerebrospinal fluid and hydrocephalus with increased intracranial pressure, which is rare. Sometimes the secretion of hypothalamic releasing and inhibiting hormones is disturbed, a large appetite with polyphagia and obesity may occur, sometimes anorexia, disturbances in body temperature regulation, insomnia or excessive sleepiness. Disturbances in the regulation of the autonomic nervous system may also develop with excessive sweating, acrocyanosis, tachycardia, etc. Some patients experience mental changes. The tumor may spread laterally into the cavernous sinus, in which the third, fourth and sixth cranial nerves pass, causing disturbances in the movement of the eyeballs and double vision. Extensive tumor spread may cause temporal epilepsy.
When the tumor erodes the floor of the Turkish saddle and grows into the paranasal sinuses, cerebrospinal fluid and meningitis may occur.

English:

Treatment. Once the cause of hyperprolactinemia has been identified,
we choose a treatment that eliminates the cause: we discontinue
or replace the drug that increases PRL concentration, we replace levothyroxine in hypothyroidism, we surgically remove the expansive process that presses on the pituitary stalk,
or a pituitary adenoma that secretes PRL in addition to other hormones. If we confirm a prolactinoma, we treat it with medication.
Treatment with dopamine agonists. Prolactin and idiopathic hyperprolactinemia are treated with dopamine agonists, unlike the treatment of other pituitary adenomas,
in which the first priority is the earliest possible surgical removal of the tumor.

V embrionalnem razvoju nastane zadnji reženj 

English:

Pituitary hormones are stored in secretory granules.
Immunocytochemical staining, which demonstrates individual hormones in cells, distinguishes five types of pituitary cells,
which secrete six pituitary hormones:
• somatotropic (secrete growth hormone – STH),
• lactotropic (secrete prolactin – PRL),
• corticotrophic (secrete adrenocorticotropin – ACTH and
related peptides),
• gonadotrophic (secrete luteinizing hormone – LH and
follitropin – FSH),
• thyrotropic (secrete thyrotropin – TSH).
In the posterior lobe of the pituitary gland (neurohypophysis), in addition to vessels and pituitary cells, there are nerve fibers that contain neurosecretory
granules, which contain the hormones oxytocin and AVP.
During embryonic development, the posterior lobe is formed

Text Answer: 100 mg hydrocortisone IV followed by 200–300 mg in continuous infusion over 24h
Reference: Interna medicina (6. izd., str. XXXVIII, 1693). (2022). Medicinska fakulteta; Buča.
Context:
Adisonska kriza. Pri adisonski krizi je treba ukrepati takoj. 
Zdravimo hipotenzijo z agresivnim nadomeščanjem teko­
čine v obliki 5-odstotne glukoze v fiziološki raztopini, pri 
čemer naj bolnik sprva dobi 1000 ml v dveh do štirih urah, 
lahko tudi več, če je potrebno in varno glede na njegovo 
srčno rezervo. Ključno zdravilo je hidrokortizon, ki ga damo čim prej, in sicer najprej 100 mg v bolusu po možnosti 
iv., na terenu lahko tudi im., nato pa zaradi kratke razpo_xFFFE_lovne dobe v prvih 24 urah še 200 mg v kontinuirani infuziji. Takšni stresni odmerki hidrokortizona in infuzija začasno odpravijo potrebo po nadomeščanju mineralokortikoidov, ki manjkajo pri bolnikih s primarno odpovedjo. Intenzivno iščemo in zdravimo sprožilni dejavnik, ki je običaj_xFFFE_no okužba. 

English:

Addisonian crisis. Immediate action is required in the event of an Addisonian crisis.
Treat hypotension with aggressive fluid replacement in the form of 5% glucose in saline, with the patient initially receiving 1000 ml over two to four hours, and more if necessary and safe based on their cardiac reserve. The key drug is hydrocortisone, which is given as soon as possible, initially 100 mg as a bolus, preferably iv., but in the field it can also be given im., and then, due to its short half-life, 200 mg as a continuous infusion in the first 24 hours. Such stress doses of hydrocortisone and the infusion temporarily eliminate the need for mineralocorticoid replacement, which is lacking in patients with primary failure. We intensively search for and treat the triggering factor, which is usually infection.

lahko prevladujejo simptomi s strani centralnega živčevja, 
kot so zaspanost, zmedenost ali koma. Če gre za bolnika s 
kronično, neprepoznano primarno odpovedjo, opazimo na 
tipičnih mestih (obraz, vrat, hrbtišča dlani, komolci, kolena, 
členki, dlančne gube, prsne bradavice, perinej, popek, notranja stran ustnic in bukalna sluznica) značilne rjavkaste 
hiperpigmentacije, ki nastanejo zaradi odlaganja melani_xFFFE_na ob kronično povišanem ACTH

English:

Patients in Addisonian crisis are severely affected, severely dehydrated, hypotensive, and shocked. They also report nonspecific symptoms such as loss of appetite, nausea, and vomiting. Diffuse abdominal pain of unknown cause is often present, which worsens with deep palpation and may mimic an acute abdomen. Elevated body temperature is a consequence of infection or the underlying disease itself. Later in the course, central nervous system symptoms such as drowsiness, confusion, or coma may predominate. In patients with chronic, unrecognized primary failure, characteristic brownish hyperpigmentation is observed in typical locations (face, neck, backs of the hands, elbows, knees, wrists, palmar folds, nipples, perineum, umbilicus, inner lips, and buccal mucosa) due to melanin deposition due to chronically elevated ACTH.

Question:
26. Which of the following is a true pituitary neoplasm?
a) Glioma
b) Germinoma
c) Adenoma
d) Cyst of Rathke's pouch
e) Neuroblastoma
Reference: Interna medicina (6. izd., str. XXXVIII, 1693). (2022). Medicinska fakulteta; Buča.
Context: Epidemiology. The most common pituitary tumors are pituitary neuroendocrine tumors (PitNET), as they have been recently renamed. In this chapter, we will, in accordance with current clinical practice, use the traditional terminology. They are found in up to 15 percent of deceased individuals during autopsy. Half of them stain positive for prolactin. In most cases, the adenomas found during life did not cause any clear clinical symptoms. The incidence of clinically significant pituitary adenomas is 2 per 100,000 per year, and the prevalence is 20 per 100,000 inhabitants. Pituitary adenomas that cause clinical symptoms account for up to 15 percent of all intracranial neoplasms.

Question:
27. Which of the following CT features is most specific for identifying a lipid-rich (benign) adrenal adenoma?
a) Contrast washout delay
b) Low attenuation (<10 Hounsfield units)
c) Size above 6 cm
d) High iodine uptake
e) Cystic degeneration
Reference: Interna medicina (6. izd., str. XXXVIII, 1693). (2022). Medicinska fakulteta; Buča.
Context: When assessing the risk of malignancy, the key feature of an incidentaloma is its appearance on a non–contrast CT scan. Benign tumors are homogeneous, usually smaller than 4 cm, and rich in fat (≤ 10 Hounsfield units). If the nature of the incidentaloma cannot be conclusively determined and it has no clear hormonal activity, a contrast‐enhanced adrenal CT protocol can be performed. This means that the patient is first scanned without contrast, then contrast is injected, followed by repeat scans at 1 and 15 minutes to calculate washout. A relative washout greater than 40 percent and an absolute washout greater than 60 percent indicate a benign lesion. Suspicious tumors are often, and always, larger than 4 cm, have irregular margins, are nonhomogeneous, may contain calcifications, are well vascularized, and have a noncontrast attenuation above 20 HU. In patients with known malignancy, up to 70 percent of incidentalomas represent metastases. For primary characterization of an incidentaloma, MRI with chemical‐shift imaging is used instead of CT as the first study in younger patients and pregnant women. FDG PET is also available, but it can be positive in benign lesions and negative in malignant ones (for example, a metastatic hypernephroma or a low‐grade lymphoma). Ultrasound cannot determine the nature of an incidentaloma. A fine‐needle biopsy under ultrasound (or CT) guidance is considered only when there is a history of malignancy outside the adrenal glands, if the tumor is hormonally inactive, and if the histology will alter management. Biopsy can distinguish adrenal from nonadrenal tissue (e.g., metastasis, infection, infiltration) but cannot differentiate between cortical adenomas and carcinomas.

Question:
32. Pituitary tumors are generally treated surgically first, except:
a) TSH adenomas
b) Craniopharyngiomas (sometimes only palliative aspiration)
c) Prolactinomas (treated with bromocriptine, cabergoline)
d) Somatotrophic (growth‐hormone–secreting) adenomas
e) Meningiomas
Reference: Interna medicina (6. izd., str. XXXVIII, 1693). (2022). Medicinska fakulteta; Buča.
Context: Operativno zdravljenje. Večje hormonsko neaktivne tumorje, ki povzročajo klinične simptome (glavobol, motnje vida, hipopituitarizem itd.), operativno odstrani nevrokirurg na transsfenoidalni poti. Hormonsko aktivne mikroadenome in makroadenome se kirurg še posebej potrudi operativno odstraniti v celoti. Ta doktrina ne velja za prolaktinome, ki jih najprej zdravimo z zdravili. English: Surgical Treatment. Larger hormonally inactive tumors that cause clinical symptoms (headache, visual disturbances, hypopituitarism, etc.) are removed surgically by a neurosurgeon via the transsphenoidal approach. For hormonally active microadenomas and macroadenomas, the surgeon makes a special effort to remove them completely. This approach does not apply to prolactinomas, which are treated first with medication.

Question:
1. Which functional class according to the NYHA classification does a patient belong to who does not experience difficulty breathing with daily activities (washing, walking on level ground), but problems occur with moderate exertion (walking up one flight of stairs)?
a. I
b. II
c. III
d. IV
e. V
Reference: Interna medicina (6. izd., str. XXXVIII, 1693). (2022). Medicinska fakulteta; Buča.
Context:
Glede na pojav prsne bolečine ali občutka težkega dihanja razdelimo bolnike v štiri funkcijske razrede:
a. NYHA I. razred – ni omejitve aktivnosti. V to skupino spadajo bolniki z objektivno ugotovljeno srčno boleznijo, ki so brez simptomov (ali pa jih imajo le ob hudih naporih).
b. NYHA II. razred – simptomi ne omejujejo vsakodnevnih opravil (umivanje, kuhanje, zmerna hoja po ravnem). Težave se pojavijo pri srednjih naporih (npr. hoja po stopnicah – več kot eno nadstropje). Med mirovanjem ponekod jo v nekaj minutah brez zdravil.
c. NYHA III. razred – simptomi omejujejo vsakodnevna opravila. Počitek jih pomiri.
d. NYHA IV. razred – vsa telesna dejavnost je zelo omejena, bolnik ima simptome tudi med mirovanjem.
English: Based on the occurrence of chest pain or the sensation of difficult breathing, patients are divided into four functional classes:

a. NYHA Class I – No limitation of physical activity. Patients with objectively confirmed heart disease who have no symptoms (or only with strenuous exercise).
b. NYHA Class II – Slight limitation of physical activity. Comfortable at rest; ordinary physical activity (washing, cooking, walking on level ground) does not cause undue fatigue or dyspnea. Symptoms occur with moderate exertion (e.g., climbing more than one flight of stairs). Comfort returns within a few minutes at rest, without medication.
c. NYHA Class III – Marked limitation of physical activity. Comfortable at rest; less than ordinary activity causes symptoms. Relief comes with rest.
d. NYHA Class IV – Unable to carry on any physical activity without discomfort; symptoms of heart failure are present even at rest.

Question:
2. What is Brugada syndrome?
a. A genetic disease, more common in males, associated with a high risk of sudden cardiac death
b. A genetic disease affecting calcium channels, resulting in impaired heart muscle contraction
c. The occurrence of atrial fibrillation in a structurally normal heart
d. Altered conduction of electrical impulses through cardiomyocytes, manifested by a widened QRS complex
e. None of the above
Text Answer: A genetic disease, more common in males, associated with a high risk of sudden cardiac death
Reference: Interna medicina (6. izd., str. XXXVIII, 1693). (2022). Medicinska fakulteta; Buča.
Context:
Sindrom Brugada je genetska bolezen, pri kateri je značilen nenormalen elektrokardiogram in povečano tveganje za nenadno srčno smrt.
Sindrom opredelimo z značilno elektrokardiografsko sliko pri osebah s strukturno normalnim srcem, sinkopo ali srčnim zastojem.
Pri mladih zdravih moških iz jugovzhodne Azije je sindrom Brugada pogost vzrok nenadne smrti zaradi prekatne fibrilacije. Smrt nastopi praviloma ponoči, med spanjem.

English:
Brugada syndrome is a genetic disorder characterized by an abnormal electrocardiogram and an increased risk of sudden cardiac death.
The syndrome is diagnosed by its distinctive ECG pattern in individuals with structurally normal hearts, presenting with syncope or cardiac arrest.
In young healthy men from Southeast Asia, Brugada syndrome is a common cause of sudden death due to ventricular fibrillation. Death typically occurs at night during sleep.

Question:
3. What is Syndrome X?
a. An X-linked heart disease
b. Microvascular ischemic heart disease without involvement of the major coronary arteries
c. Unspecified chest pain
d. Neurovegetative dystonia
e. Imaginary chest pain
Text Answer: Microvascular ischemic heart disease without involvement of the major coronary arteries
Reference: Interna medicina (6. izd., str. XXXVIII, 1693). (2022). Medicinska fakulteta; Buča.
Context: Mikrovaskularna angina (sindrom X) je posledica disfunkcije koronarne mikrocirkulacije. Lahko gre za strukturno remodeliranje mikrožilja, kar vodi do fiksne obstrukcije pretoka. Možna je tudi motnja vazomotorike na nivoju mikrožilja, ki povzroča dinamično obstrukcijo. English: Microvascular angina (Syndrome X) is the result of dysfunction of the coronary microcirculation. It may involve structural remodeling of the microvessels, leading to a fixed obstruction of flow. There can also be a vasomotor disturbance at the level of the microvasculature, causing dynamic obstruction.

Question:
4. Which type of myocarditis is treated with a combination of cyclosporine and glucocorticoids?
a. Giant cell myocarditis
b. Any viral myocarditis
c. Only fulminant viral myocarditis
d. Patients with chronic myocarditis and dilated cardiomyopathy
e. Patients with toxic myocarditis after cyclophosphamide treatment
Reference: Interna medicina (6. izd., str. XXXVIII, 1693). (2022). Medicinska fakulteta; Buča.
Context: Imunosupresivna zdravila. Zdravljenje z imunosupresivnimi zdravili (glukokortikoidi v monoterapiji ali v kombinaciji z azatioprinom in/ali ciklosporinom) je indicirano pri gigantoceličnem miokarditisu, srčni sarkoidozi, miokarditisu v sklopu sistemskih vezivnotkivnih bolezni, eozinofilnem miokarditisu, toksičnem miokarditisu in kroničnem limfocitnem miokarditisu, ki je neodziven na simptomatično zdravljenje srčnega popuščanja. English: Immunosuppressive drugs. Treatment with immunosuppressive drugs (glucocorticoids as monotherapy or in combination with azathioprine and/or cyclosporine) is indicated for giant cell myocarditis, cardiac sarcoidosis, myocarditis as part of systemic connective tissue diseases, eosinophilic myocarditis, toxic myocarditis, and chronic lymphocytic myocarditis that is unresponsive to symptomatic treatment of heart failure.

Question:
5. What is the target LDL-cholesterol level in secondary prevention?
a. ≤ 1.3 mmol/L
b. ≤ 1.5 mmol/L
c. ≤ 1.8 mmol/L
d. ≤ 2.0 mmol/L
e. ≤ 2.5 mmol/L
Reference: Harrison’s Principles of Internal Medicine, 20th ed.
Context: For patients with established atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease (ASCVD), intensive lipid-lowering therapy is recommended to achieve an LDL-C level of less than 70 mg/dL (1.8 mmol/L). In very high-risk individuals, such as those with multiple major ASCVD events or one major ASCVD event and multiple high-risk conditions, an LDL-C target of less than 55 mg/dL (1.4 mmol/L) may be considered.

Question:
6. In a patient with heart failure and a chronic dry cough:
a. We change the ACE inhibitor
b. We do not start an ARB
c. We immediately refer them to a pulmonologist
d. We discontinue the beta‐blocker
e. We replace the ACE inhibitor with an ARB
Text Answer: We replace the ACE inhibitor with an ARB
Reference: Interna medicina (6. izd., str. XXXVIII, 1693). (2022). Medicinska fakulteta; Buča.
Context: Stranski učinki zdravljenja z zaviralci konvertaze angiotenzina (kašelj, angioedemi, hipotenzija, hiperkaliemija, poslabšanje ledvične funkcije) so redki (do 10 odstotkov), običajno se pojavijo ob uvedbi zdravljenja. Ob pojavu stranskih učinkov sprva zmanjšamo odmerek, če tudi takrat stranski učinki ne izzvenijo, ukinemo zdravljenje z zaviralci ACE in te nadomestimo z antagonisti angiotenzinskih receptorjev. English: Side effects of treatment with angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitors (cough, angioedema, hypotension, hyperkalemia, worsening of renal function) are rare (up to 10 percent) and usually appear at the initiation of treatment. If side effects occur, we initially reduce the dose; if the side effects still do not resolve, we discontinue ACE inhibitor treatment and replace it with angiotensin receptor antagonists.

Reference: Interna medicina (6. izd., str. XXXVIII, 1693). (2022). Medicinska fakulteta; Buča.
Context: Rentgenogram prsnih organov. Pomemben je pri izključevanju drugih vzrokov boleznih simptomov in znakov. Pri bolnikih s pljučnim infarktom običajno vidimo zasenčenja, ki so trikotaste oblike (vrh trikotnika v hilusu pljuč). Zastrt bo lahko frenikokostalni sinus zaradi plevralnega izliva na isti strani.
