Question:
6. Which radiolabeled agent is recommended for treating medullary thyroid carcinoma that overexpresses somatostatin receptors?
a. Iodine-131 (radioiodine)
b. I-131 MIBG (metaiodobenzylguanidine)
c. Y-90 DOTATATE
d. Tc-99m sestamibi
e. Ga-68 DOTATOC
Reference: Salavati, A., Puranik, A., Kulkarni, H. R., Budiawan, H., & Baum, R. P. (2016). Peptide receptor radionuclide therapy (PRRT) of medullary and nonmedullary thyroid cancer using radiolabeled somatostatin analogues. Seminars in Nuclear Medicine, 46(3), 215–224.
Context: “Peptide receptor radionuclide therapy using 177Lu-labeled or 90Y-labeled somatostatin analogues may have a significant role in the management of medullary and nonmedullary thyroid cancers in those patients where PET/CT with 68Ga-labeled somatostatin analogues demonstrates significant SSTR expression.”

Question:
7. What is the standard management for localized, muscle-invasive bladder cancer?
a. Radical cystectomy with pelvic lymph node dissection (with or without neoadjuvant cisplatin-based chemotherapy)
b. Partial cystectomy alone
c. External-beam radiotherapy alone
d. Transurethral resection plus intravesical BCG only
e. Systemic immunotherapy alone
Text Answer: Radical cystectomy with pelvic lymph node dissection (with or without neoadjuvant cisplatin-based chemotherapy)
Reference: Strojan, P., & Hočevar, M. (Eds.). (2018). Onkologija: Učbenik za študente medicine [Oncology: Textbook for medical students] (1st ed.). Onkološki inštitut Ljubljana.
Context:
Radical cystectomy with removal of regional lymph nodes is the standard treatment for muscle-invasive bladder cancer. Analyses of treatment outcomes for T2 tumors have shown that patients who undergo cystectomy within ninety days of the cancer diagnosis have significantly better survival than those in whom radical treatment is delayed.
In this surgery, in women, in addition to the bladder and regional lymph nodes, the uterus, anterior vaginal wall, ovaries, and urethra are also removed. In men, the bladder is removed along with the prostate and seminal vesicles, and regional lymph nodes are removed.
Radical cystectomy can be performed as an open surgery, laparoscopically, or laparoscopically with robotic assistance.

Question:
8. In oropharyngeal squamous-cell carcinoma, which proteomic feature is typically associated with HPV positivity and better prognosis?
a. Elevated EGFR expression
b. High TP53 mutation burden
c. High PD-L1 expression alone
d. Low TP53 mutation burden and functional p53
e. Overexpression of HER2/neu
Text Answer: Low TP53 mutation burden and functional p53
Reference: Wikipedia contributors. (2025, February 28). HPV-positive oropharyngeal cancer. In Wikipedia, The Free Encyclopedia.
Context:
“The gene encoding p53 is found as the wild type in HPV⁺OPC but mutated in HPV-OPC. In HPV⁺OPC p53 protein undergoes accelerated degradation by E6, drastically reducing its levels, while in HPV-OPC it undergoes genetic mutation, which may result in synthesis of an abnormal p53 protein…
Although p53 mutations occur in HPV⁺OPC, they are far less common than in HPV-OPC (26% vs 48%), and do not appear to affect clinical outcome.”

Question:
9. If a thyroid fine-needle aspiration (FNA) is reported as “follicular neoplasm,” what is the next optimal step?
a. Start levothyroxine suppression therapy
b. Repeat ultrasound-guided FNA in six weeks
c. Molecular testing for BRAF mutation only
d. Diagnostic lobectomy to evaluate capsular/vascular invasion
e. Radioiodine ablation
Text Answer: Diagnostic lobectomy to evaluate capsular/vascular invasion
Reference: Strojan, P., & Hočevar, M. (Eds.). (2018). Onkologija: Učbenik za študente medicine [Oncology: Textbook for medical students] (1st ed.). Onkološki inštitut Ljubljana.
Context: The smallest procedure we can perform is a lobectomy with isthmusectomy, during which the entire suspicious thyroid lobe is removed along with the associated isthmus. This procedure is appropriate for patients with a suspected follicular thyroid tumor when we want a definitive histological diagnosis, or for patients with a small, lobe-confined differentiated thyroid carcinoma in whom we do not expect postoperative radioiodine therapy (Table 6). In the case of a suspicious cytological appearance of a thyroid tumor intraoperatively, we rarely use the frozen section method, since a diagnosis made this way is reliable in only 30 percent of cases. Lobectomy is a safe procedure with a low risk of postoperative complications.

Question:
10. Which therapy is indicated for a gastrointestinal stromal tumor (GIST) with a primary KIT exon 11 mutation?
a. Sunitinib
b. Sorafenib
c. Bevacizumab
d. Everolimus
e. Imatinib
Reference: Strojan, P., & Hočevar, M. (Eds.). (2018). Onkologija: Učbenik za študente medicine [Oncology: Textbook for medical students] (1st ed.). Onkološki inštitut Ljubljana.
Context:
The discovery of the drug imatinib represented a true revolution in the treatment of GIST.
Imatinib is a targeted therapy that selectively inhibits the tyrosine kinases c-KIT and PDGFR-α. We use it as neoadjuvant treatment in patients with locally advanced disease (unresectable tumors or resectable tumors that would require extensive mutilation) in order to shrink the tumor before surgery to achieve resectability or to allow for a less mutilating operation.

Question:
11. Which two advantages does brachytherapy have over external-beam radiotherapy (teletherapy)?
a. Lower cost and shorter patient lifespan
b. Higher localized dose to tumor with rapid dose falloff in nearby tissue, and shorter overall treatment time
c. Less need for immobilization devices, and no risk of radiation exposure to staff
d. No acute toxicity, and no late toxicity
e. Eliminates the need for concurrent chemotherapy, and is always outpatient
Text Answer: Higher localized dose to tumor with rapid dose falloff in nearby tissue, and shorter overall treatment time
Reference: Strojan, P., & Hočevar, M. (Eds.). (2018). Onkologija: Učbenik za študente medicine [Oncology: Textbook for medical students] (1st ed.). Onkološki inštitut Ljubljana.
Context: Brachytherapy is characterized by complex logistics and requires access to the tumor. The rapid falloff of dose with distance from the radiation source allows the target volume to be treated with very high doses while keeping the required dose constraints on surrounding healthy tissues within limits. The execution of the brachytherapy procedure is based on the prior insertion of applicator tubes, followed by placement of the radioactive source, which is pushed into them by an afterloading device. Today, the most commonly used radiation source is iridium-192; the position and dwell time of the source within the applicators are determined by a computer-generated treatment plan.

Question:
12. Which late radiation complication is most characteristic of hierarchical (rapidly dividing) tissues?
a. Necrosis
b. Xerostomia
c. Mucositis
d. Fibrosis
e. Osteoradionecrosis
Reference: Straub, J. M., New, J., Hamilton, C. D., Lominska, C., Shnayder, Y., & Thomas, S. M. (2015). Radiation‐induced fibrosis: Mechanisms and implications for therapy. Journal of Cancer Research and Clinical Oncology, 141(11), 1985–1994.
Context: “Comparatively, the majority of the late effects of radiation vary in severity depending on the radiation dose, fraction size, and volume treated. One important late effect that is a significant contributor to patient morbidity is radiation-induced fibrosis (RIF), which may occur in the skin and subcutaneous tissue, lungs, gastrointestinal and genitourinary tracts, as well as any other organs in the treatment field.”

Question:
13. Which immunophenotypic marker is relatively specific for diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL)?
a. CD3
b. CD15
c. CD19/CD20
d. CD30
e. CD10 only
Reference: Strojan, P., & Hočevar, M. (Eds.). (2018). Onkologija: Učbenik za študente medicine [Oncology: Textbook for medical students] (1st ed.). Onkološki inštitut Ljubljana.
Context: Since 1998, especially in the treatment of patients with indolent and aggressive B-cell non-Hodgkin lymphomas, a particular form of biological therapy has become increasingly established. In these cases, we use monoclonal antibodies capable of binding to characteristic structures on the membranes of B-cell (and some T-cell) lymphomas: rituximab, obinutuzumab, and ofatumumab—which target the CD20 determinant; alemtuzumab—which targets the CD52 determinant; inotuzumab ozogamicin—a CD22-targeted antibody conjugated to a cytotoxic agent; and brentuximab vedotin—a CD30-targeted antibody conjugated to a cytotoxic agent.

Question:
14. What is the principal difference in acute hematologic toxicity timing between whole-body irradiation (WBI) and localized radiotherapy?
a. WBI causes rapid pancytopenia; localized causes focal marrow suppression that appears later
b. Localized radiotherapy causes immediate pancytopenia; WBI does not affect blood counts
c. Both WBI and localized radiotherapy cause identical timing of marrow aplasia
d. WBI causes lymphopenia only; localized causes neutropenia only
e. There is no hematologic effect from localized radiotherapy
Text Answer: WBI causes rapid pancytopenia; localized causes focal marrow suppression that appears later
Reference: Strojan, P., & Hočevar, M. (Eds.). (2018). Onkologija: Učbenik za študente medicine [Oncology: Textbook for medical students] (1st ed.). Onkološki inštitut Ljubljana.
Context:
With whole-body irradiation, irreversible cell damage occurs, as well as mutations in some cells. Depending on the dose of radiation received, at the lowest doses the most sensitive organs (e.g., bone marrow) are damaged; at higher doses, the small intestine is affected; and at still higher doses, other organ systems with an even higher threshold for deterministic radiation effects are damaged. At extremely high doses (gamma rays, 100 Gy), death ensues within 24 to 48 hours due to the so-called cerebrovascular syndrome (blood–brain barrier failure). Given its greatest sensitivity to radiation, at low doses (3 to 5 Gy) the bone marrow syndrome appears with a latency of about 21 days, when the consequences of blood cell depletion develop.

Less frequently and especially later on (due to the longer lifespan of mature blood cells), bone marrow irradiation can cause changes in the blood count. Acute adverse effects, after completion of irradiation, generally heal with proper care. The exception is the most severe forms of such injuries, which do not fully heal and progress to chronic impairment (consequential late adverse effects) (Figure 25). English: With Whole-Body Irradiation, Irreversible Cell Damage Occurs, AS Well As Mutatations and Some Cells.DEPENDING ON THE AGRAPTION OF RADIATION RECEIVIDED, AT THE LOWEST ACCEPTION (E.G., Bone Marrow) Are Damaged;at Higher achievement, The Small Intestine is affected;and at Still Higher achievement, Other Organ Systems with an even higher threshold for determinist radiation effects are damaged.At Extremely High Achievement (Gamma Rays, 100 Gy), Death Ensues Within 24 to 48 Hours due to the co-Cerelled Cerebrovascular Syndrome (Blood-Brain Barrier Failure).Given Its Greatest Sensitivity to Radiation, at Low COMPLETE (3 to 5 GY) The Bone Marrow Syndrome Appears with A Latency of About 21 Days, When the Consequences of Blood Cell Deplational Develop.

Less Frequently and Especialilly Later On (due to the Longer Lifespan of Mature Blood Cells), Bone Marrow Irradiation Can Caus Changes in the Blood Count.Acute Adverse Effects, After Completion of Irradiation, Generally Heal with Proper Care.The Excection is the Most Severe Forms of Such Injuries, which do not fully heal and prosaire (Consequential Late Adverse Effects) (Figure 25).

Question:
15. In small cell lung carcinoma (SCLC) with syndrome of inappropriate ADH secretion (SIADH), which laboratory abnormality is most typical?
a. Hypernatremia
b. Hyponatremia with inappropriately concentrated urine
c. Hypokalemia with polyuria
d. Hypercalcemia
e. Hypophosphatemia
Reference: Strojan, P., & Hočevar, M. (Eds.). (2018). Onkologija: Učbenik za študente medicine [Oncology: Textbook for medical students] (1st ed.). Onkološki inštitut Ljubljana.
Context:
Diagnostics: In a patient with cancer, this is usually normovolemic hyponatremia. Laboratory results show a decreased serum sodium level (< 135 mmol/L) and a reduced plasma osmolality (< 280 mOsm/kg), while the urine sodium concentration is elevated (> 20 mmol/L) and urine osmolality is increased (> 500 mOsm/kg). Fluid‐balance assessment reveals fluid retention.

In the differential diagnosis, one must consider hypothyroidism and adrenal insufficiency. Hyponatremia can also be caused by certain medications (carbamazepine, ifosfamide, vincristine, narcotics).

Question:
16. Which of the following is NOT part of the initial workup for carcinoma of unknown primary (CUP)?
a. Thorough history and physical examination
b. Contrast-enhanced CT of chest/abdomen/pelvis
c. Core‐needle biopsy with immunohistochemistry
d. PET-CT in all cases regardless of initial CT findings
e. Targeted molecular profiling if initial tests are inconclusive
Text Answer: PET-CT in all cases regardless of initial CT findings
Reference: Strojan, P., & Hočevar, M. (Eds.). (2018). Onkologija: Učbenik za študente medicine [Oncology: Textbook for medical students] (1st ed.). Onkološki inštitut Ljubljana.
Context:
The minimal set of procedures for identifying the anatomical origin and defining the extent of disease includes:
Thorough medical history
Clinical examination, including:
Breast examination
Rectal examination
Gynecological examination
Examination of all lymph node regions
Skin inspection
Basic laboratory tests:
Complete blood count with differential
Biochemical panel (liver function tests, blood urea nitrogen)
C-reactive protein (CRP)
Tumor markers (see Table 2)
Basic urinalysis
Fecal occult blood test
Cross-sectional imaging:
CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis
(If the patient is allergic to iodine-based contrast or has renal insufficiency, perform PET-CT instead of CT.)
Mammography (in women)
Additional studies are performed in a directed manner, for example:
Endoscopic evaluation of the gastrointestinal tract (if a GI origin is suspected)
Breast ultrasound and/or MRI (if a breast origin is suspected)
Octreoscan (if a neuroendocrine tumor is suspected)
PET-CT (for enlarged lymph nodes in the neck or supraclavicular region when a head / neck primary is suspected, or for a solitary metastasis anywhere in the body)

Question:
17. Which CT feature suggests that a pancreatic adenocarcinoma is unresectable?
a. Tumor confined to the head of the pancreas only
b. No vascular involvement and no metastases
c. ≥ 180° encasement of the superior mesenteric artery (SMA)
d. Solitary 1 cm lesion in the tail of the pancreas
e. Dilated pancreatic duct without a visible mass
Text Answer: ≥ 180° encasement of the superior mesenteric artery (SMA)
Reference: Strojan, P., & Hočevar, M. (Eds.). (2018). Onkologija: Učbenik za študente medicine [Oncology: Textbook for medical students] (1st ed.). Onkološki inštitut Ljubljana.
Context: The only treatment that offers the patient a potential chance for long‐term survival is surgical resection. This is feasible if the disease is still resectable (absence of retroperitoneal invasion, no involvement of the hepatic artery, celiac trunk, or superior mesenteric artery, and no distant metastases). Invasion of the superior mesenteric vein and portal vein by itself does not constitute a contraindication to surgery.

Question:
18. What are the most frequent metastatic sites for malignant melanoma?
a. Colon, small intestine, kidney
b. Lung, liver, brain, bone
c. Prostate, bladder, ovary
d. Spleen, pancreas, thyroid
e. Esophagus, stomach, rectum
Reference: O’Neill, R. S., Leaver, P., Ryan, C., Liang, S., Sanagapalli, S., & Cosman, R. (2024). Metastatic melanoma: an unexpected cause of acute liver failure. Clinical Journal of Gastroenterology, 17(6), 1125–1129.
Context: “Malignant melanoma (MM) is an aggressive malignancy with an unpredictable metastatic pattern, with the most common organs involved being the lungs, bone, lymph nodes, liver and brain.”

Question:
19. In head‐and‐neck squamous‐cell carcinoma, what does “field cancerization” refer to?
a. A new primary arising outside the head‐and‐neck region
b. Genetic alterations confined to a single tumor focus
c. Metastasis from a remote organ to the head‐and‐neck region
d. Dysplasia limited to the nasal cavity only
e. Multiple primary tumors arising in a wide mucosal field after carcinogen exposure
Text Answer: Multiple primary tumors arising in a wide mucosal field after carcinogen exposure
Reference: Strojan, P., & Hočevar, M. (Eds.). (2018). Onkologija: Učbenik za študente medicine [Oncology: Textbook for medical students] (1st ed.). Onkološki inštitut Ljubljana.
Context: In 1953, Slaughter already drew attention to the occurrence of multiple invasive squamous cell carcinomas in the oral cavity that arise alongside the primary tumor, as well as to the presence of atypical epithelium around these tumors. He explained the multicentric origin of invasive squamous cell carcinoma by the process of “field cancerization,” in which the entire mucosal field is under the influence of carcinogenic factors. Subsequent advances in molecular genetics have confirmed and expanded the fundamental theory of this process.

Question:
20. Which lung-cancer subtype most commonly secretes ectopic ADH, leading to SIADH?
a. Non-small-cell lung cancer – adenocarcinoma
b. Non-small-cell lung cancer – squamous cell carcinoma
c. Small cell lung carcinoma (SCLC)
d. Large cell lung carcinoma
e. Bronchoalveolar carcinoma
Reference: Strojan, P., & Hočevar, M. (Eds.). (2018). Onkologija: Učbenik za študente medicine [Oncology: Textbook for medical students] (1st ed.). Onkološki inštitut Ljubljana.
Context: Hyponatremia is a relatively common electrolyte disturbance in patients with cancer, and one of the most frequent causes is the syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone secretion (SIADH). This is a paraneoplastic syndrome resulting from ectopic secretion of antidiuretic hormone (ADH) or an ADH‐like protein. In the kidneys, this leads to excessive water reabsorption, plasma osmolality decreases, and the patient excretes inappropriately concentrated urine. In reality, there is no true sodium deficiency: hyponatremia is secondary to hypoosmolality of the plasma due to water excess. SIADH occurs most often in small‐cell lung cancer (75 % of all SIADH cases in malignancy), but it is also common in pancreatic cancer, mesothelioma, primary brain tumors, and brain metastases.

Question:
21. Which of the following is a common clinical feature of hypercalcemia in a patient with squamous-cell lung cancer?
a. Polycythemia
b. Polyuria and polydipsia
c. Bradycardia and hypertension
d. Weight gain from fluid retention
e. Hypokalemia
Reference: Strojan, P., & Hočevar, M. (Eds.). (2018). Onkologija: Učbenik za študente medicine [Oncology: Textbook for medical students] (1st ed.). Onkološki inštitut Ljubljana.
Context: Symptoms and Signs: The first signs are thirst and polyuria, constipation, and fatigue; later, nausea, loss of appetite, and vomiting may develop, all of which lead to patient dehydration. When serum calcium levels exceed 3.0 mmol/L, signs of central nervous system involvement appear: confusion and somnolence, which can progress to coma and death. Cardiac arrhythmias may also occur. Prolonged hypercalcemia impairs kidney function and can lead to renal failure.

Question:
22. Which of the following laboratory changes is NOT a Criterion for tumor lysis syndrome (TLS)?
a. Uric acid ≥ 476 µmol/L or 25 %↑ above baseline
b. Potassium ≥ 6.0 mmol/L or 25 %↑ above baseline
c. Phosphate ≥ 2.1 mmol/L or 25 %↑ above baseline
d. Calcium ≥ 3.0 mmol/L or 25 %↑ above baseline
e. Calcium ≤ 1.75 mmol/L or 25 %↓ below baseline
Reference: Strojan, P., & Hočevar, M. (Eds.). (2018). Onkologija: Učbenik za študente medicine [Oncology: Textbook for medical students] (1st ed.). Onkološki inštitut Ljubljana.
Context: Symptoms and Signs: Metabolic disturbances such as hyperuricemia, hyperkalemia, hyperphosphatemia, and hypocalcemia occur. These lead to further complications, including cardiac arrhythmias (sudden cardiac death) and acute renal failure. Signs of developing tumor lysis syndrome include nausea, vomiting, and general weakness. Patients become oliguric and hypertensive, with weight gain due to fluid retention; in more severe cases, seizures may occur.

Question:
23. What is the indication for preoperative chemoradiotherapy in rectal adenocarcinoma?
a. T3–T4 or node-positive (N+) tumors within 12 cm from the anal verge
b. All rectal tumors regardless of stage
c. T1N0M0 tumors only
d. Tumors confined to the anal canal only
e. Metastatic rectal cancer
Text Answer: T3–T4 or node-positive (N+) tumors within 12 cm from the anal verge
Reference: Strojan, P., & Hočevar, M. (Eds.). (2018). Onkologija: Učbenik za študente medicine [Oncology: Textbook for medical students] (1st ed.). Onkološki inštitut Ljubljana.
Context: For early T3 tumors (minimal extension into the perirectal fat), we use a short-course preoperative radiation regimen in which the tumor and regional lymph nodes are treated with only five daily fractions of 5 Gy, for a total dose of 25 Gy. This achieves the same local control as a longer preoperative radiation course, but does not provide the same degree of tumor volume reduction (or downstaging). Surgery follows immediately after completion of radiation. Older patients with multiple comorbidities do not receive chemotherapy during radiation—because the toxicity of combined treatment is too great for them—so these patients receive only the short-course radiation, with surgery delayed by 6–8 weeks. English: For Early T3 Tumors (Minimal Extension Into Perirectal Fat), We Use Short-Course Preoperative Radiation Regimen in which tumor and regional lymph nodes are treated with only five daily fractions of 5 gy, for a total reach of the 25 gy.This Achieves the Sam Local Control as a Loner preoperative radio, but do they do the degre of tumor Volume Reduction (or downstaging).Surgery Follow Immedially After Completion of Radiation.Older Patients with Multiple Comorbidies to Not Receive Chemotherapy During Radiation-Becauses The Toxicity of Combined Treatment Is Too Great for Them-Ses Thsee Patients Receive Only The Short-Course Radiation, With Surgery Delayed BY 6-8 WEEKS.

Question:
24. Which two immunomodulatory mechanisms allow tumors to escape host immune response?
a. Upregulation of MHC class I and secretion of IL-2
b. Downregulation of PD-L1 and increased CD8+ T-cell infiltration
c. Overexpression of Fas ligand alone
d. Increased tumor antigenicity and activation of NK cells
e. Secretion of immunosuppressive cytokines (e.g., IL-10, TGF-β) and downregulation of antigen presentation (MHC I loss)
Text Answer: Secretion of immunosuppressive cytokines (e.g., IL-10, TGF-β) and downregulation of antigen presentation (MHC I loss)
Reference: Strojan, P., & Hočevar, M. (Eds.). (2018). Onkologija: Učbenik za študente medicine [Oncology: Textbook for medical students] (1st ed.). Onkološki inštitut Ljubljana.
Context: Tumor cells can decrease recognition of tumor antigens by altering their antigenic profile or by reducing antigen expression through MHC I molecules, by completely losing MHC I from the cell surface, by expressing nonclassical MHC molecules such as HLA-G and HLA-E, by downregulating adhesion molecules, or by inducing changes in effector cells through secretion of immunoregulatory cytokines. Tumor cells also adapt to the immune response by fully or partly blocking the function of intracellular transport proteins (TAP proteins) and by altering expression of adhesion molecules.

Question:
25. Which two high-penetrance genes are most commonly associated with hereditary nonpolyposis colorectal cancer (HNPCC/Lynch syndrome)?
a. BRCA1 and BRCA2
b. MLH1 and MSH2
c. APC and TP53
d. PTEN and STK11
e. RB1 and VHL
Reference: Strojan, P., & Hočevar, M. (Eds.). (2018). Onkologija: Učbenik za študente medicine [Oncology: Textbook for medical students] (1st ed.). Onkološki inštitut Ljubljana.
Context:
Inherited genetic factors are all changes that parents pass on to their children. For cancer development, important changes include:
Monogenic alterations in genes that directly cause specific cancer types (e.g., mutations in APC in familial adenomatous polyposis—FAP).
Genetic or epigenetic changes in individual genes that substantially increase cancer risk (e.g., BRCA1/2 for breast and/or ovarian cancer; TP53 in Li–Fraumeni syndrome; MLH1, MSH2, MSH6 in Lynch syndrome).
Various polygenic alterations, which often interact with additional environmental factors.

Context: In the Western world—and also in our country—the most common primary malignant neoplasms of lymph nodes are mature B-cell lymphomas, particularly diffuse large B-cell lymphoma and small B-cell lymphomas (follicular lymphoma; lymphocytic lymphoma/chronic lymphocytic leukemia B; mantle cell lymphoma; marginal zone lymphoma; lymphoplasmacytic lymphoma). Question:
28. How does intensity-modulated radiotherapy (IMRT) improve the therapeutic ratio in head-and-neck cancer?
a. By delivering uniform dose to a larger volume
b. By increasing overall treatment time
c. By delivering conformal high doses to tumor while sparing adjacent critical structures (e.g., salivary glands)
d. By using lower photon energy only
e. By eliminating the need for image guidance
Text Answer: By delivering conformal high doses to tumor while sparing adjacent critical structures (e.g., salivary glands)
Reference: Strojan, P., & Hočevar, M. (Eds.). (2018). Onkologija: Učbenik za študente medicine [Oncology: Textbook for medical students] (1st ed.). Onkološki inštitut Ljubljana.
Context: In the IMRT technique, the treatment plan also includes data on varying the dose within each individual beam or field; by defining the spatial and temporal pattern of movement of the multileaf collimator system leaves—either stepwise (“step-and-shoot IMRT”) or continuously (“sliding windows IMRT”)—we can achieve that different portions within the field are irradiated with different doses (Figure 10). The purpose of this type of intensity modulation of the beam is to “bend” the isodose lines more effectively, thereby achieving a higher degree of conformity. A second beneficial effect is the increased dose gradient at the edge of the high-dose region, which likewise contributes to greater safety of irradiation by protecting surrounding healthy tissues adjacent to the target from (excessively) high doses.

To improve sensitivity, collection should be done in the morning, before defecation and washing, by pressing transparent cellulose tape (“Scotch test”, cellulose tape slide test) on the perianal skin and then examining the tape placed on a microscope slide.

Question:
11. What is the most common cause of fever in children?
a) Otitis media
b) Viral respiratory infection
c) Urinary tract infection
d) Streptococcal angina
e) Gastroenteritis
Reference: El-Radhi, A. S. (2012). Fever in Children: Diagnosis and Management. Springer.
Context: The most common cause of fever in children is infection. Among infections, viral infections, especially of the respiratory tract, are by far the most frequent cause of fever.

Question:
12. What is the most reliable enzyme for diagnosing myocardial infarction?
a) LDH
b) CK
c) Troponin
d) AST
e) ALT
Reference: Thygesen, K., Alpert, J. S., Jaffe, A. S., Chaitman, B. R., Bax, J. J., Morrow, D. A., ... & White, H. D. (2018). Fourth universal definition of myocardial infarction (2018). Journal of the American College of Cardiology, 72(18),
Context: Cardiac troponins (cTnI and cTnT) are the most sensitive and specific biomarkers for myocardial necrosis, and are currently the preferred biomarkers for the diagnosis of myocardial infarction.

Question:
15. What are the key symptoms of acute limb ischemia?
a) Red, swollen, warm limb
b) Numbness with no pain
c) Pale, cold, painful limb
d) Only tingling sensation
e) Elevated skin temperature
Reference: Olin, J. W., & White, C. J. (2010). Acute limb ischemia. Circulation, 122(16),
Context: Acute limb ischemia is characterized by the sudden decrease in limb perfusion resulting in potential threat to limb viability. The classic clinical presentation includes the six 'Ps': pain, pallor, pulselessness, paresthesia, paralysis, and poikilothermia (coldness).

Question:
What is the most typical symptom of scabies (Sarcoptes scabiei)?
a) Fever
b) Painful rash
c) Intense itching, especially at night
d) Joint pain
e) Watery blisters on palms
Reference: Scabies - Symptoms & causes - Mayo Clinic
Context: The most common symptom of scabies is intense itching, which is typically worse at night.

Question:
What condition is associated with a smooth, red tongue?
a) Sjögren’s syndrome
b) Pernicious anemia
c) Candidiasis
d) Hypothyroidism
e) Iron deficiency without anemia
Reference: Plemons, J. M., & Al-Hashimi, I. (2014). Oral manifestations of systemic diseases. In Burket's Oral Medicine
Context: The classic oral manifestation of pernicious anemia is atrophic glossitis, which presents as a smooth, beefy red tongue that is often painful or burning.

Question:
What is the target HbA1c level in most diabetic patients?
a) Below 8.5%
b) As low as possible
c) Between 7–8%
d) Below 7%
e) Exactly 7.5%
Reference: Understanding A1C Test | ADA - American Diabetes Association.
Context: .The goal for most adults with diabetes is an A1C that is less than 7%.

Question:
Which medication should not be combined with sildenafil (Viagra)?

a) Nitrates

b) Beta blockers

c) Calcium channel blockers

d) ACE inhibitors

e) Statins
Reference: Sildenafil - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf
Context: Nitrates are absolutely contraindicated with sildenafil, due to the risk of severe and potentially fatal hypotension.

Question:
What is the initial treatment for a hypoglycemic coma?
a) Oral glucose
b) 5% glucose IV
c) 50% glucose IV or intramuscular glucagon
d) Insulin
e) Fluids only
Reference: Hypoglycemia. In Williams Textbook of Endocrinology
Context: If the person is unconscious or unable to swallow, intravenous glucose (25 g of 50% dextrose solution) should be administered. If intravenous access cannot be readily established, glucagon (1 mg intramuscularly) can be administered.

Question:
What is the most common complication of streptococcal tonsillitis?
a) Otitis media
b) Acute glomerulonephritis
c) Peritonsillar abscess
d) Meningitis
e) Rheumatic fever
Reference: Strep throat - Symptoms & causes - Mayo Clinic
Context: If untreated, strep throat can cause complications, such as kidney inflammation or rheumatic fever. Rheumatic fever can lead to painful and inflamed joints, a specific type of rash, or heart valve damage.

Question:
What is the best initial diagnostic method when a smoker presents with hemoptysis?
a) Bronchoscopy
b) Chest X-ray
c) Lung biopsy
d) Sputum culture
e) CT scan
Reference: Hemoptysis: Diagnosis and Management - American Family Physician.
Context: A CT scan of the chest is generally the initial diagnostic test for evaluating hemoptysis once the airway has been stabilized and life-threatening bleeding has been excluded.

Question:
Which condition most commonly leads to erectile dysfunction in diabetic men?
a) Obesity
b) Low testosterone
c) Diabetic neuropathy
d) High cholesterol
e) Hypertension
Reference: Feldman, H. A., Goldstein, I., Hatzichristou, D. G., Krane, A. B., & McKinlay, J. B. (1998). Impotence and its medical and psychosocial correlates: results of the Massachusetts Male Aging Study. Journal of Urology, 160(1)
Context: The most common cause of erectile dysfunction in men with diabetes is diabetic neuropathy, which damages the nerves that control penile erection

Question:
What is the main clinical feature of erythema migrans in Lyme disease?
a) High fever
b) Neurological symptoms
c) Expanding red skin rash with central clearing
d) Joint pain
e) Swollen lymph nodes
Text Answer: Expanding red skin rash with central clearing
Reference: Wormser, G. P., Dattwyler, R. J., Shapiro, E. D., Halperin, J. J., Steere, A. C., Klempner, M. S., ... & American Academy of Neurology. (2006). The clinical assessment, treatment, and prevention of Lyme disease, human granulocytic anaplasmosis, and babesiosis: clinical practice guidelines by the Infectious Diseases Society of America. Clinical Infectious Diseases, 43(9), 1089-1134
Context: The most distinctive clinical feature of Lyme disease is the expanding skin lesion known as erythema migrans (EM). The EM lesion typically begins as a red macule or papule at the site of the tick bite and expands slowly over days to weeks to form a large, annular (ring-shaped) lesion with central clearing.

Question:
What is the most typical symptom of a peritonsillar abscess?
a) Dry cough
b) Difficulty swallowing
c) Trismus and uvula deviation
d) Persistent fever
e) Enlarged tonsils without pain
Reference: Peritonsillar Abscess - StatPearls - NCBI
Context: The classic symptoms and signs of peritonsillar abscess are sore throat, odynophagia [painful swallowing], trismus (spasm of the jaw muscles causing the mouth to remain tightly closed), and a 'hot potato' voice. On physical examination, the uvula is deviated away from the involved side, and there is unilateral bulging of the soft palate and anterior tonsillar pillar.

Question:
What is the main non-pharmacological recommendation for a hypertensive patient during hot weather with leg swelling?
a) Start a diuretic
b) Reduce water intake
c) Elevate the legs and move to a cooler environment
d) Add salt to the diet
e) Increase physical activity outdoors
Text Answer: Elevate the legs and move to a cooler environment
Reference: Kenny, G. P., Flouris, A. D., & Seifert, T. (2010). Physiological adjustments to exercise in the heat. In Sports Medicine and Science: An Encyclopedia of Sports Medicine and Science
Context: Strategies to manage heat-related peripheral edema include elevation of the affected limbs and staying in a cool environment

Question:
What is the most common cause of sudden worsening in heart failure patients?
a) Hyperlipidemia
b) Infection
c) Regular medication intake
d) Sleeping pattern changes
e) Depression
Reference: Ponikowski, P., Voors, A. A., Anker, S. D., Bueno, H., Cleland, J. G. F., Coats, A. J. S., ... & ESC Scientific Document Group. (2016). 2016 ESC Guidelines for the diagnosis and treatment of acute and chronic heart failure. European Heart Journal, 37(27)
Context: The most common precipitants of ADHF [Acute Decompensated Heart Failure] are non-adherence to diet or medications and infection.

Question:
What is the next step in a patient with atrial fibrillation, palpitations, and unknown thyroid status?
a) Prescribe beta-blocker immediately
b) Measure TSH levels
c) Start anticoagulants
d) Refer for cardioversion
e) Do coronary angiography
Reference: Kirchhof, P., Camm, A. J., Goette, N., Brandes, L. F., Eckardt, M., & Esc Group. (2016). 2016 ESC Guidelines for the management of atrial fibrillation developed in collaboration with EACTS. European Heart Journal
Context: Screening for underlying causes of atrial fibrillation (AF) is recommended for all patients, including measurement of thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) levels to exclude hyperthyroidism, and a thorough history and physical examination to exclude structural heart disease."

Question:
What is the target LDL cholesterol level in patients with cardiovascular disease?
a) Below 2.5 mmol/L
b) Below 2.0 mmol/L
c) Below 1.5 mmol/L
d) Above 3.0 mmol/L
e) Exactly 2.0 mmol/L
Reference: LDL-cholesterol targets as Achilles' heel of 2020 ISH guidelines - PMC - PubMed
Context: The 2019 ESC/EAS Guidelines for the management of dyslipidaemias introduced even the concept of “extreme high risk group”, stating, that in some patients who experience a second vascular event within 2 years (not necessarily of the same type as the first event) while taking maximally tolerated statin-based therapy, an LDL-cholesterol goal of <1.0 mmol/L (40 mg/dL) is recommended. For the broader "very high risk" category (which includes all patients with established atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease), the LDL-cholesterol target is <1.4 mmol/L (55 mg/dL).

Question:
What is the most common cause of chest pain not related to ischemia?
a) Angina pectoris
b) Pericarditis
c) Musculoskeletal chest wall pain
d) Pulmonary embolism
e) Aortic dissection
Reference: Chest Pain - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf
Context: Musculoskeletal chest pain, including costochondritis and Tietze's syndrome, is the most common cause of non-cardiac chest pain.

Question:
What is the first step in managing a patient with a suspected pulmonary edema?
a) Oral antibiotics
b) Oxygen therapy and diuretics
c) Corticosteroids
d) Inhalers
e) Bronchoscopy
Reference: Acute Pulmonary Edema - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf
Context: The initial management of acute pulmonary edema should focus on stabilization of the patient's condition. This includes oxygen therapy and the administration of diuretics

Question:
What is the diagnostic hallmark of atrial fibrillation on ECG?
a) ST elevation
b) Absence of P waves and irregular R-R intervals
c) Long QT interval
d) Fixed AV block
e) Normal sinus rhythm
Text Answer: Absence of P waves and irregular R-R intervals
Reference: Kirchhof, P., Camm, A. J., Goette, N., Brandes, L. F., Eckardt, L., & Endrich, B. (2016). 2016 ESC Guidelines for the management of atrial fibrillation developed in collaboration with EACTS. European Heart Journal, 37(38)
Context: he diagnostic hallmark of atrial fibrillation (AF) on the electrocardiogram (ECG) is irregularly irregular R-R intervals and the absence of discernible P waves, with the presence of fibrillatory waves varying in morphology and amplitude.

Question:
Which antihypertensive class is also used to treat benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH)?
a) Beta blockers
b) ACE inhibitors
c) Alpha blockers
d) Calcium channel blockers
e) Diuretics
Reference: Roehrborn, C. G., & Schwinn, D. A. (2004). Alpha1-adrenergic receptors and their antagonists in the treatment of lower urinary tract symptoms and benign prostatic hyperplasia. Journal of Urology, 171(3),
Context: Alpha-blockers are a class of medications that are effective in treating both hypertension and symptoms of benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH) due to their ability to relax smooth muscle in the prostate and bladder neck, as well as in peripheral blood vessels.

Question:
What is the most common pathogen causing traveler’s diarrhea?
a) Rotavirus
b) Salmonella
c) Escherichia coli (E. coli)
d) Giardia lamblia
e) Shigella
Reference: Travelers Diarrhea - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf
Context: The most common bacterial cause is enterotoxigenic Escherichia coli (ETEC), with estimates that the bacteria is responsible for nearly 30% of cases.

Question:
What is the most effective non-drug intervention for allergic rhinitis?
a) Corticosteroids
b) Allergen avoidance
c) Antihistamines
d) Decongestants
e) Saline nasal rinses
Reference: Bousquet, J., Khaltaev, N., Cruz, A. A., Denburg, J., Fokkens, W. J., Togias, C., ... & White, P. (2008). Allergic Rhinitis and its Impact on Asthma (ARIA) 2008 update (in collaboration with the World Health Organization, GA(2)LEN and AllerGen). Allergy, 63(Suppl. 86),
Context: The primary and most effective non-pharmacological intervention for allergic rhinitis is allergen avoidance. This involves reducing exposure to identified allergens (e.g., dust mites, pet dander, pollen) in the environment.

Question:
What is the correct action for a nosebleed at home?
a) Tilt head back and lie down
b) Sit upright, pinch the soft part of the nose, and lean forward
c) Apply warm compress to the neck
d) Drink warm fluids
e) Use antihistamines
Text Answer: Sit upright, pinch the soft part of the nose, and lean forward
Reference: Nosebleed - Mayo Clinic.
Context:
.To stop a nosebleed:

Sit upright and lean forward slightly. This will prevent blood from running down the back of your throat.
Pinch the soft part of your nose (just above the nostrils) firmly and continuously for at least 10 to 15 minutes.

Question:
A patient presents with vertical gaze palsy. Which brain structure is most likely affected?
a) Pons
b) Dorsal midbrain (mesencephalon)
c) Internal capsule
d) Basal ganglia
e) Thalamus
Reference: Vertical Gaze Palsy - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf.
Context: Disorders of vertical gaze commonly result from lesions of the dorsal midbrain (also known as the pretectal area), where the vertical gaze centers and the interstitial nucleus of Cajal are located. Parinaud syndrome, a classic example of dorsal midbrain syndrome, typically includes vertical gaze palsy.

Question:
A lesion of the left medial longitudinal fasciculus (MLF) will result in:
a) Impaired abduction of the right eye during leftward gaze
b) Impaired adduction of the left eye during rightward gaze, with nystagmus of the right eye
c) Complete bilateral ophthalmoplegia
d) Vertical gaze palsy
e) Impaired convergence
Text Answer: Impaired adduction of the left eye during rightward gaze, with nystagmus of the right eye
Reference: Internuclear Ophthalmoplegia - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf
Context: A lesion of the medial longitudinal fasciculus (MLF) on one side, such as the left MLF, results in an internuclear ophthalmoplegia (INO). This is characterized by impaired adduction of the ipsilateral eye (e.g., the left eye fails to adduct when looking to the right) and nystagmus of the contralateral abducting eye (e.g., nystagmus of the right eye when looking to the right).

Question:
A man with ataxia on one side of the body, dysarthria, ipsilateral Horner’s syndrome, and contralateral body sensory loss most likely has:
a) Medial medullary syndrome
b) Weber syndrome
c) Wallenberg syndrome (PICA infarct)
d) Benedikt syndrome
e) Locked-in syndrome
Reference: Wallenberg Syndrome - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf.
Context: Wallenberg syndrome, also known as lateral medullary syndrome, typically results from occlusion of the posterior inferior cerebellar artery (PICA) or its branches. Its characteristic features include ipsilateral cerebellar signs (ataxia), dysarthria, ipsilateral Horner's syndrome (miosis, ptosis, anhidrosis), and contralateral loss of pain and temperature sensation over the body

Question:
24. A patient is unable to repeat spoken words but speaks fluently and understands language. What type of aphasia is this?
a) Broca's aphasia
b) Wernicke’s aphasia
c) Conduction aphasia
d) Transcortical sensory aphasia
e) Global aphasia
Reference: Wallenberg Syndrome - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf
Context: Wallenberg syndrome, also known as lateral medullary syndrome, typically results from occlusion of the posterior inferior cerebellar artery (PICA) or its branches. Its characteristic features include ipsilateral cerebellar signs (ataxia), dysarthria, ipsilateral Horner's syndrome (miosis, ptosis, anhidrosis), and contralateral loss of pain and temperature sensation over the body.

Question:
What is the first imaging method in a patient who presents with sudden onset hemiparesis and gaze deviation?
a) Non-contrast head CT
b) Brain MRI with contrast
c) Spinal MRI
d) EEG
e) Angiography
Reference: Powers, W. J., Rabinstein, A. A., Telegrafo, T. H., et al. (2019). Guidelines for the Early Management of Patients With Acute Ischemic Stroke: 2019 Update to the 2018 Guidelines for the Early Management of Acute Ischemic Stroke. Stroke, 50(12)
Context: For patients presenting with acute neurological deficits suggestive of stroke (such as sudden onset hemiparesis and gaze deviation), non-contrast head CT (NCCT) is the recommended first-line imaging modality. Its primary purpose is to rapidly differentiate between ischemic stroke and hemorrhagic stroke, which guides acute management decisions.

Question:
What is the hallmark of internuclear ophthalmoplegia (INO)?
a) Bilateral abduction deficit
b) Vertical gaze limitation with nystagmus
c) Impaired adduction of one eye with nystagmus of the abducting eye
d) Bilateral ptosis and mydriasis
e) Downward gaze paralysis
Text Answer: Impaired adduction of one eye with nystagmus of the abducting eye
Reference: Internuclear Ophthalmoplegia - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf
Context: Internuclear ophthalmoplegia (INO) is a disorder of conjugate horizontal gaze resulting from a lesion in the medial longitudinal fasciculus (MLF). The hallmark features are impaired adduction of the ipsilateral eye (on attempted lateral gaze) and nystagmus of the contralateral abducting eye."

Question:
Which neurological condition is characterized by constructional apraxia and dressing apraxia due to damage in the non-dominant parietal lobe?
a) Wernicke’s aphasia
b) Hemispatial neglect syndrome
c) Broca’s aphasia
d) Parkinson’s disease
e) Optic ataxia
Reference: Hemispatial Neglect - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf
Context: Lesions of the non-dominant (typically right) parietal lobe can lead to a constellation of deficits known as hemispatial neglect syndrome. This syndrome is characterized by a failure to attend to stimuli on the contralateral side of space, and it often includes constructional apraxia (difficulty drawing or building) and dressing apraxia (difficulty organizing and performing the motor actions necessary for dressing).

Question:
A patient presents with tongue deviation to the left and atrophy on that side. Where is the lesion?
a) Right cerebral cortex
b) Left facial nerve nucleus
c) Left hypoglossal nerve
d) Right corticospinal tract
e) Right vagus nerve
Reference: Hypoglossal Nerve Palsy - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf.
Context: A lesion of the hypoglossal nerve (cranial nerve XII) leads to weakness, atrophy, and fasciculations of the ipsilateral half of the tongue. When the patient is asked to protrude the tongue, it will deviate towards the side of the lesion due to the unopposed action of the healthy genioglossus muscle on the contralateral side.

Question:
What is the characteristic gait of a patient with normotensive hydrocephalus?
a) Steppage gait
b) Waddling gait
c) Ataxic gait
d) Magnetic (marche à petits pas) gait
e) Festinating gait
Reference: Normal Pressure Hydrocephalus - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf.
Context: The gait disturbance in normal pressure hydrocephalus (NPH) is often characterized by a 'magnetic' gait, also known as 'marche à petits pas' (gait of small steps). Patients appear as though their feet are glued to the floor, with difficulty lifting their feet, a wide base, and impaired balance, despite a lack of significant weakness.

Question:
Which cranial nerve is not located in the cavernous sinus?
a) CN III
b) CN IV
c) CN V1
d) CN VI
e) CN II
Reference: Cavernous Sinus Anatomy - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf
Context: The cavernous sinus contains several important neurovascular structures. The cranial nerves passing through the cavernous sinus are the oculomotor nerve (CN III), the trochlear nerve (CN IV), the ophthalmic division of the trigeminal nerve (CN V1), the maxillary division of the trigeminal nerve (CN V2), and the abducens nerve (CN VI). The optic nerve (CN II) does not pass through the cavernous sinus; it travels directly from the orbit to the optic chiasm.

Question:
What neurological sign is seen in a patient who climbs up their thighs with their hands to stand up from the floor?
a) Lhermitte’s sign
b) Gowers' sign
c) Romberg sign
d) Froment sign
e) Babinski sign
Reference: Gowers Sign - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf.
Context: Gowers' sign is a classic clinical finding observed in patients with proximal muscle weakness, particularly in Duchenne muscular dystrophy. It is characterized by the patient's difficulty rising from a sitting or supine position on the floor, requiring them to "climb up" their own legs with their hands to achieve an upright posture.

Question:
What syndrome results from a lesion affecting the corticospinal tract and CN III in the midbrain?
a) Wallenberg syndrome
b) Benedikt syndrome
c) Weber syndrome
d) Locked-in syndrome
e) Medial medullary syndrome
Reference: Weber Syndrome - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf
Context: Weber syndrome is a midbrain stroke syndrome resulting from a lesion affecting the cerebral peduncle (containing the corticospinal and corticobulbar tracts) and the exiting fibers of the oculomotor nerve (CN III). Clinically, it presents as ipsilateral oculomotor nerve palsy (ptosis, dilated pupil, eye deviated down and out) and contralateral hemiplegia (weakness) due to damage to the corticospinal tract

Question:
Which structure is primarily involved in recognizing faces?
a) Angular gyrus
b) Broca's area
c) Fusiform gyrus
d) Prefrontal cortex
e) Parahippocampal gyrus
Reference: Prosopagnosia - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf.
Context: The fusiform gyrus (located in the inferior temporal lobe) contains the fusiform face area (FFA), a region that shows particularly strong activation when individuals view faces. It is widely considered to be a key region involved in the processing and recognition of faces. Damage to this area can lead to prosopagnosia (face blindness)

Question:
Which gait is associated with peroneal nerve palsy?
a) Festinating gait
b) Steppage (high stepping) gait
c) Ataxic gait
d) Waddling gait
e) Spastic scissoring gait
Reference: Peroneal Nerve Injury - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf
Context: The most common presentation of common peroneal nerve injury is a weakness of ankle dorsiflexion leading to the classic foot drop, which often causes patients to catch their toes on the ground while ambulating or ambulate with a high steppage gait to prevent dragging their toes on the ground.

Question:
What is the most appropriate imaging for suspected spinal cord compression with sensory level at the nipples (T4)?
a) Brain MRI
b) Lumbar puncture
c) Cervical spine X-ray
d) Thoracic spine MRI
e) Thoracic CT without contrast
Reference: Spinal Cord Compression - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf
Context: When spinal cord compression is suspected, particularly with a localized sensory level, magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the relevant spinal region is the imaging modality of choice. MRI provides superior visualization of the spinal cord, nerve roots, intervertebral discs, and surrounding soft tissues, allowing for precise localization and characterization of the compressive lesion. Given a sensory level at the nipples, the thoracic spine is the targeted region

Question:
What neurological disorder is characterized by increased muscle tone, brisk reflexes, and extensor plantar response?
a) Lower motor neuron lesion
b) Upper motor neuron lesion
c) Neuropathy
d) Myopathy
e) Cerebellar syndrome
Reference: Upper Motor Neuron Lesion - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf
Context: Lesions of the upper motor neuron (UMN) pathway typically result in a characteristic constellation of clinical signs including increased muscle tone (spasticity), brisk deep tendon reflexes, and the presence of pathological reflexes such as the extensor plantar response (Babinski sign).

Question:
Which type of aphasia is most likely if the patient speaks fluently, uses neologisms, cannot understand language, but can repeat?
a) Broca’s aphasia
b) Transcortical sensory aphasia
c) Conduction aphasia
d) Global aphasia
e) Wernicke’s aphasia
Reference: Aphasia - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf.
Context: Transcortical sensory aphasia is characterized by fluent speech, often with paraphasias and neologisms, and severe impairment of auditory comprehension. The distinguishing feature, however, is the preserved ability to repeat words and phrases, despite the comprehension deficit

Question:
A patient has right hemiparesis and conjugate eye deviation to the left. Where is the lesion?
a) Left frontal eye field
b) Left parietal lobe
c) Right brainstem
d) Right occipital lobe
e) Left cerebellar hemisphere
Text Answer: patient has right hemiparesis and conjugate eye deviation to the left. Where is the lesion?
Reference: Gaze Palsies - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf
Context: Lesions affecting the frontal eye fields (located in the frontal lobe) cause a deviation of the eyes toward the side of the lesion and away from the side of the hemiparesis. Thus, a lesion in the left frontal eye field would lead to conjugate gaze deviation to the left and right-sided hemiparesis due to the involvement of the adjacent motor cortex.

Question:
What is a classic symptom of Friedreich’s ataxia outside the nervous system?
a) Hepatic failure
b) Cardiomyopathy
c) Cataract
d) Retinopathy
e) Cushingoid face
Reference: Clinical Features of Friedreich Ataxia - PMC - PubMed Central.
Context: Friedreich's ataxia is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder, but it is also associated with significant non-neurological manifestations. The most common and clinically relevant extra-neurological finding is cardiomyopathy (abnormal thickening of the heart muscle), which often leads to heart failure and is a leading cause of mortality in affected individuals. Other common non-neurological issues include skeletal deformities (e.g., scoliosis, pes cavus) and diabetes mellitus..

Question:
What is the mechanism of Wallerian degeneration?
a) Retrograde axonal degeneration
b) Segmental demyelination
c) Anterograde axonal degeneration distal to the injury
d) Neuronal apoptosis in the cortex
e) Mitochondrial toxicity
Text Answer: Anterograde axonal degeneration distal to the injury
Reference: Wallerian Degeneration - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf
Context: Wallerian degeneration is an active process of anterograde axonal degeneration that occurs in the portion of an axon distal to a site of injury. It involves the breakdown of the axon and its myelin sheath after the axon has been severed or crushed, leading to the disintegration of the distal segment of the nerve fiber

Question:
What symptom combination is typical for transtentorial uncal herniation?
a) Bilateral miosis and aphasia
b) Ipsilateral fixed dilated pupil and contralateral hemiparesis
c) Bilateral vision loss and normal consciousness
d) Diplopia and central scotoma
e) Hypotension and coma
Text Answer: Ipsilateral fixed dilated pupil and contralateral hemiparesis
Reference: Brain Herniation - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf
Context: Uncal herniation, a type of transtentorial herniation, typically results in compression of the ipsilateral oculomotor nerve (CN III) and the contralateral cerebral peduncle. This leads to a classic clinical triad of ipsilateral (same side) pupillary dilation and unresponsiveness to light (fixed and dilated pupil) due to parasympathetic fiber compression, and contralateral (opposite side) hemiparesis or hemiplegia from the compression of the corticospinal tract

Question:
A patient presents with anosognosia and hemispatial neglect. Where is the lesion?
a) Dominant parietal lobe
b) Dominant frontal lobe
c) Occipital cortex
d) Non-dominant parietal lobe
e) Basal ganglia
Reference: Hemispatial Neglect - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf
Context: Hemispatial neglect (or unilateral spatial neglect) is a disorder of attention characterized by a failure to report or respond to stimuli presented on the side opposite to the brain lesion. It is most frequently caused by damage to the non-dominant (typically right) parietal lobe. This region's involvement often also leads to anosognosia, which is the patient's unawareness or denial of their own neurological deficit, such as their hemiparesis or neglect.

Question:
1. Which of the following enzymes is directly involved in the iodination of tyrosyl residues in thyroglobulin within the thyroid follicle lumen?
a) Iodotyrosine deiodinase
b) Thyroid peroxidase
c) 5’-deiodinase type 2
d) Tyrosine hydroxylase
Reference: de Vijlder, J. J. M., & Bikker, R. (2000). Biochemistry and physiology of thyroid peroxidase. In D. P. Nauseef (Ed.), The Peroxidase Multigene Family of Enzymes (pp. 117–132). Springer.
Context: “Thyroglobulin is the protein in which iodine, bound to tyrosine residues, and thyroid hormone, formed from these iodinated tyrosine residues, is stored until needed. Iodination and coupling of iodo-tyrosine residues is catalyzed by the enzyme thyroid peroxidase (TPO) within the colloid of the thyroid follicle.”

Question:
2. Parathyroid hormone (PTH) exerts its effects via which intracellular second messenger pathway in renal tubular cells?
a) IP3/DAG cascade only
b) cAMP-dependent protein kinase A
c) Direct G-protein coupled activation of phospholipase C
d) Activation of nuclear steroid receptors
Reference: Sutkeviciute, I., Jean-Alphonse, F. G., & Vilardaga, J.-P. (2017). Endosomal PTH Receptor Signaling Through cAMP and Its Consequence for Human Medicine. In Structure and Function of GPCRs (pp. 181–193). Springer.
Context: "The parathyroid hormone (PTH) type 1 receptor (PTHR) is a medically important G protein-coupled receptor (GPCR) that triggers the cAMP/PKA signaling pathway in kidney and bone cells to regulate calcium ion homeostasis and bone turnover."

Question:
3. The MEN1 tumor suppressor gene product, menin, interacts directly with which nuclear complex?
a) Retinoblastoma (Rb) complex
b) mTOR complex 1
c) MLL (Mixed Lineage Leukemia) histone methyltransferase complex
d) APC complex
Text Answer: MLL (Mixed Lineage Leukemia) histone methyltransferase complex
Reference: Yokoyama, A., Wang, Z., Wysocka, J., Sanyal, M., Aufiero, D. J., Kitabayashi, I., Herr, W., & Cleary, M. L. (2004). Leukemia proto‐oncoprotein MLL forms a SET1‐like histone methyltransferase complex with menin to regulate Hox gene expression. Molecular and Cellular Biology, 24(13), 5639–5649.
Context: “Menin, a product of the MEN1 tumor suppressor gene, is also a component of the 1‐MDa MLL complex. Abrogation of menin expression phenocopies loss of MLL and reveals a critical role for menin in the maintenance of Hox gene expression. Oncogenic mutant forms of MLL retain an ability to interact with menin but not other identified complex components. These studies link the menin tumor suppressor protein with the MLL histone methyltransferase machinery, with implications for Hox gene expression in development and leukemia pathogenesis.”

Question:
4. In patients with sight-threatening thyroid eye disease who are unresponsive to glucocorticoids, the next step in therapy is:
a) Rituximab
b) Mycophenolate mofetil
c) Orbital radiotherapy
d) Teprotumumab (IGF-1R inhibitor monoclonal antibody)
Reference: Ugradar, S., Malkhasyan, E., & Douglas, R. S. (2024). Teprotumumab for the Treatment of Thyroid Eye Disease. Endocrine Reviews, 45(3), bnae018.
Context: "Teprotumumab, a fully human IgG1 monoclonal antibody that inhibits the IGF-1R, recently gained approval in the United States for the treatment of TED. In phase 2 and phase 3 clinical studies, teprotumumab showed efficacy in reducing inflammation, proptosis, diplopia, and burden on quality of life in patients who were treated. Postintroduction studies have confirmed the results of the phase 2 and phase 3 studies. Since 2020, more than 5800 patients have been treated with teprotumumab, and it appears to be well tolerated. The American Thyroid Association and the European Thyroid Association have recommended it as first-line therapy for patients with moderate to severe TED who display features of proptosis and diplopia."

Question:
5. A mutation in which gene is most commonly associated with generalized glucocorticoid resistance (Chrousos syndrome)?
a) NR3C1 (glucocorticoid receptor gene)
b) POMC
c) HSD11B1
d) CYP11B1
Reference: Charmandari, E., Kino, T., Ichijo, T., & Chrousos, G. P. (2008). Generalized glucocorticoid resistance: Clinical aspects, molecular mechanisms, and implications of a rare genetic disorder. The Journal of Clinical Endocrinology & Metabolism, 93(5), 1563–1572.
Context: “Primary generalized glucocorticoid resistance (Chrousos syndrome) is a rare genetic disorder characterized by impaired sensitivity of all target tissues to glucocorticoids. This syndrome results from heterozygous, loss‐of‐function mutations in the human glucocorticoid receptor (hGR) gene (NR3C1) located on chromosome 5q31–32. To date, over 30 different NR3C1 mutations—most of which disrupt ligand binding or DNA‐binding functions of the receptor—have been identified in affected patients. These inactivating NR3C1 mutations reduce feedback inhibition at the level of the hypothalamus and pituitary, leading to compensatory increases in ACTH secretion and resultant elevations in adrenal steroid production.”

Question:
6. Metyrapone inhibits the synthesis of cortisol by blocking which enzyme?
a) 21-hydroxylase
b) 11β-hydroxylase
c) 17α-hydroxylase
d) Aldosterone synthase
Reference: Pivonello, R., De Leo, M., Cozzolino, A., & Colao, A. (2021). Metyrapone in Cushing’s syndrome: a profile of its use. Drugs & Therapy Perspectives, 37(3), 109–117.
Context: "Metyrapone and its active metabolite, metyrapol, dose-dependently inhibit the two highly homologous isoforms of 11-hydroxylase, 11β-hydroxylase (CYP11B1) and 18-hydroxylase (CYP11B2), and consequently inhibit cortisol and aldosterone production."

Question:
7. Which subunit of the insulin receptor has intrinsic tyrosine kinase activity?
a) α-subunit
b) β-subunit
c) IRS-1
d) PI3K
Reference: Ullrich, A., Bell, G. I., Chen, E. Y., Herrera, R., Petruzzelli, L. M., Dull, T. J., ... & Taylor, S. I. (1985). Human insulin receptor and its relationship to the receptor for insulin‐like growth factor I. Nature, 313(6005), 756–761.
Context: “The insulin receptor is synthesized as a single polypeptide that is proteolytically cleaved into two disulfide-linked subunits: an extracellular α-subunit and a transmembrane β-subunit. The β-subunit contains an intrinsic tyrosine kinase domain responsible for the receptor’s autophosphorylation and downstream signalling activity.”

Question:
8. In 17α-hydroxylase deficiency, the following profile is typical:
a) Low cortisol, low mineralocorticoids, low androgens
b) Low cortisol, high mineralocorticoids, low androgens
c) High cortisol, high mineralocorticoids, high androgens
d) High cortisol, low mineralocorticoids, high androgens
Text Answer: Low cortisol, high mineralocorticoids, low androgens
Reference: Medscape. (n.d.). 17-Hydroxylase Deficiency Syndrome.
Context: "17-hydroxylase (17-OH) deficiency syndrome is a rare genetic disorder of steroid biosynthesis that causes decreased production of glucocorticoids and sex steroids by the adrenal glands and increased synthesis of mineralocorticoid precursors."

Question:
9. Octreotide’s mechanism in treating acromegaly primarily involves:
a) Directly binding to GH-receptors
b) Inhibiting GHRH secretion
c) Mimicking somatostatin to suppress GH secretion
d) Increasing GH receptor degradation
Text Answer: Mimicking somatostatin to suppress GH secretion
Reference: Melmed, S. (2005). Medical progress: Acromegaly. The New England Journal of Medicine, 355(24), 2558–2573.
Context: “Somatostatin analogs such as octreotide bind primarily to somatostatin receptor subtype 2 (SSTR2) on pituitary somatotrophs, leading to inhibition of adenylyl cyclase, reduced intracellular cAMP, and consequent suppression of growth‐hormone release.”

Question:
10. The defect in pseudohypoparathyroidism type 1a involves which protein?
a) α-subunit of Gs-protein (GNAS1 mutation)
b) PTH receptor (PTHR1)
c) Calcium-sensing receptor (CaSR)
d) Vitamin D receptor
Reference: Kotanidou, E. P., Tsinopoulou, V.-R., Serbis, A., Litou, E., & Galli-Tsinopoulou, A. (2021). Pseudohypoparathyroidism Type 1A with Normocalcaemia, due to the Novel c.389A>G (p.Tyr130Cys) Mutation in the GNAS Gene. Journal of Bone Metabolism, 28(1), 85–89.
Context: "Pseudohypoparathyroidism type 1A (PHP1A) is a rare disease caused by molecular defects in the maternally-inherited allele of the guanine nucleotide-binding protein, α-stimulating (GNAS) gene. The GNAS gene encodes the stimulatory G-protein α-subunit that regulates production of the second messenger cyclic adenosine monophosphate."

Question:
1. The use of digitalis and verapamil is absolutely contraindicated in:
a. Atrial fibrillation
b. Atrial flutter
c. Atrial tachycardia
d. Atrial extrasystoles
e. Atrial fibrillation in a patient with WPW‐type preexcitation
Text Answer: Atrial fibrillation in a patient with WPW‐type preexcitation
Reference: Interna medicina (6. izd., str. XXXVIII, 1693). (2022). Medicinska fakulteta; Buča.
Context: Če je refraktarna doba zelo kratka ali če je bolnik dobil preparat digitalisa ali verapamil, lahko atrijska fibrilacija preide v prekatno fibrilacijo in bolnik umre (če ni pravočasne pomoči). Refraktarna doba akcesorne poti se med naporom krajša; tako je opisanih nekaj smrtnih primerov pri osebah z Wolff-Parkinson-Whitovim sindromom med športnim udejstvovanjem. English: If the refractory period is very short or if the patient has received digitalis or verapamil, atrial fibrillation can transition to ventricular fibrillation, and the patient may die (if prompt help is not available). The refractory period of the accessory pathway shortens during exertion; thus, some fatal cases have been reported in individuals with Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome during sports participation.

Question:
2. An 80-year-old woman is brought to the internal medicine emergency department because for the first time in her life, she began to experience difficulty breathing and chest tightness when rapidly climbing stairs. She did not stop immediately, but then felt dizzy, collapsed, and briefly lost consciousness. Otherwise, she has been healthy until now, with only arterial hypertension, for which she is not receiving treatment. The ECG shows a sinus rhythm with signs of left ventricular hypertrophy and left atrial overload. On auscultation of the lungs, inspiratory crackles are heard bilaterally at the bases; over the heart there is a loud systolic murmur heard across the entire precordium, radiating into the carotid arteries.
What is the most likely diagnosis?
a. Stenosis of both internal carotid arteries
b. Symptomatic aortic stenosis
c. Acute heart failure due to left ventricular pump failure
d. Atrial fibrillation with a tachycardic ventricular response
e. Asymptomatic aortic stenosis
Reference: Harrison’s Principles of Internal Medicine, 20th ed.
Page: Chapter 264: Valvular Heart Disease – Aortic Stenosis
Context: The classic triad of severe aortic stenosis includes angina, syncope, and dyspnea (heart failure). Syncope often occurs during exertion when systemic vascular resistance falls due to exercise-induced vasodilation, but cardiac output cannot rise appropriately.

Question:
3. Choose the characteristic effect of altered heart rate on valvular heart lesions (two correct answers):
a. Sinus tachycardia worsens symptoms of mitral stenosis
b. Sinus tachycardia improves symptoms of mitral stenosis
c. Sinus tachycardia does not affect symptoms of mitral stenosis
d. Sinus bradycardia improves symptoms of aortic regurgitation
e. Sinus bradycardia increases symptoms of aortic regurgitation
f. Sinus bradycardia does not affect symptoms of aortic regurgitation

1. a + e 
2. a + b
3. b + d
4. c + f
5. d + f
Reference:
1.Interna medicina (6. izd., str. XXXVIII, 1693). (2022). Medicinska fakulteta; Buča.
2.Braunwald’s Heart Disease: A Textbook of Cardiovascular Medicine, 11th Edition
Context: 1. Klinična slika. Težave se pojavijo, ko se mitralno ustje zoži na manj kot 2 cm². Glavni simptom je težka sapa pri naporu. Zaradi pljučne hipertenzije se lahko pojavi krvavo obarvan izpljunek, včasih tudi prave hemoptize. Pri popuščanju desnega prekata so bolniki utrujeni, slabo zmogljivi, otekajo v noge in v trebuh. Dilatacija levega preddvora pospešuje nastanek fibrilacije preddvorov. Ob pojavu fibrilacije preddvorov imajo bolniki tahiaritmijo, kar njihovo hemodinamično stanje še dodatno poslabša. English: Clinical presentation. Symptoms appear once the mitral orifice narrows to less than 2 cm². The main symptom is severe dyspnea on exertion. Pulmonary hypertension may lead to blood-tinged sputum and occasionally frank hemoptysis. In right ventricular failure, patients experience fatigue, poor exercise tolerance, and edema of the legs and abdomen. Left atrial dilation promotes the development of atrial fibrillation. When atrial fibrillation occurs, patients develop tachyarrhythmia, which further worsens their hemodynamic status.

Question:
4. Which is the best investigative method for assessing the dynamic behavior of a mitral valve lesion?
a. Ventriculography
b. Invasive measurement of pulmonary artery pressure
c. Stress echocardiography on a semi-recumbent cycle ergometer
d. Cardiac magnetic resonance imaging
e. Cardiac CT
Text Answer: Stress echocardiography on a semi-recumbent cycle ergometer
Reference: Interna medicina (6. izd., str. XXXVIII, 1693). (2022). Medicinska fakulteta; Buča.
Context:
Pridobljene napake srčnih zaklopk. Z dvodimenzionalno in tridimenzionalno ehokardiografijo prikažemo morfološke spremembe zaklopk, kot so zadebelitve, vegetacije, prolaps listov zaklopk, zraslost komisur in spremembe na subvalvularnem aparatu. Z ehokardiografijo opredelimo tudi stopnjo povečanja srčnih votlin, stopnjo hipertrofije sten, sistolično in diastolično funkcijo prekatov, ki so lahko posledica teh napak. Stopnjo stenoze zaklopke ocenimo glede na hitrost toka krvi skozi ustje znotraj zaklopke – čim večja je ta hitrost, tem hujša je zožitev. Z enačbo kontinuitete izračunamo tudi preostalo površino stenotične zaklopke. Z doplersko metodo zlahka odkrijemo regurgitacijske tokove. Z barvno doplersko preiskavo prikažemo smer regurgitacijskih tokov, kar omogoča semikvantitativno oceno stopnje regurgitacije. English: Acquired valvular lesions. Using two- and three-dimensional echocardiography, we demonstrate morphological valve changes—such as leaflet thickening, vegetations, leaflet prolapse, commissural fusion, and subvalvular apparatus abnormalities. Echocardiography also defines the degree of cardiac chamber enlargement, the extent of wall hypertrophy, and both systolic and diastolic ventricular function that may result from these lesions. The severity of valve stenosis is assessed by measuring blood-flow velocity across the valve orifice: the higher the velocity, the more severe the narrowing. The continuity equation is then used to calculate the remaining effective valve area. Doppler techniques readily detect regurgitant jets, and color Doppler imaging shows their direction, allowing a semi-quantitative assessment of regurgitation severity.

The severity of valve stenosis is assessed by measuring blood-flow velocity across the valve orifice: the higher the velocity, the more severe the narrowing. The continuity equation is then used to calculate the remaining effective valve area. Doppler techniques readily detect regurgitant jets, and color Doppler imaging shows their direction, allowing a semi-quantitative assessment of regurgitation severity. In tricuspid regurgitation, Doppler measurement of the pressure gradient between the ventricle and atrium permits estimation of systolic pulmonary artery pressure.

Question:
5. Characteristics of central venous pressure (CVP):
a. In a healthy person it is between 10 and 20 mmHg
b. It is decreased in pulmonary embolism
c. The v-wave on the CVP tracing is increased in tricuspid insufficiency
d. A cannon a-wave is a sign of atrioventricular dissociation
e. In atrial fibrillation the a-wave on the CVP tracing is accentuated

1.a + b
2.a + c
3.b + d
4.c + d 
5.c + e"
Reference: Interna medicina (6. izd., str. XXXVIII, 1693). (2022). Medicinska fakulteta; Buča.
Context:
Kateterizacija srca zaradi izolirane trikuspidalne insuficience ni potrebna. Izmerimo povišan tlak v desnem preddvoru in v krivulji tlakov je visok val v (slika 2.164). Koronografija je indicirana v enakih primerih kot pri drugih boleznih zaklopk. English: Cardiac catheterization is not required for isolated tricuspid insufficiency. One finds elevated pressure in the right atrium, with a prominent v-wave on the pressure tracing (Fig. 2.164). Coronary angiography is indicated under the same circumstances as for other valvular heart diseases.

Pri neusklajenem delovanju preddvorov in prekatov (atrioventrikularni disociaciji) vidimo le valove a, ki so neodvisni od arterijskih pulzacij in imajo drugačno (višjo) frekvenco. Občasno preddvorna in prekatna sistola sovpadata in ta preddvor iztisne kri proti zaprti trikuspidalni zaklopki, kar vidimo kot nenavadno visok in močan val v (na sliki 2.4c). English : In atrioventricular dissociation, we observe only the a-waves, which occur independently of arterial pulsations and have a different (higher) amplitude. Occasionally, atrial and ventricular systole coincide, and the atrium then ejects blood against a closed tricuspid valve, seen as an unusually tall and forceful v-wave (illustrated in Fig. 2.4c).
