Question:
1. In GIST patients, why is imatinib preferred for a primary KIT exon 11 mutation, whereas sunitinib is chosen for a PDGFR-α D842V mutation?
a. Imatinib binds PDGFR-α only; sunitinib binds KIT only.
b. The exon 11 KIT mutation is resistant to sunitinib, while PDGFR-α D842V is sensitive to imatinib.
c. KIT exon 11 mutations respond well to imatinib’s high-affinity binding; PDGFR-α D842V has steric hindrance to imatinib, but sunitinib can inhibit that mutant.
d. Both mutations respond equally to imatinib; sunitinib is used for cost reasons.
e. Imatinib is indicated for wild-type GIST only; sunitinib for all KIT-mutated GIST.
Text Answer: KIT exon 11 mutations respond well to imatinib’s high-affinity binding; PDGFR-α D842V has steric hindrance to imatinib, but sunitinib can inhibit that mutant.
Reference:
1. Heinrich, M. C., Corless, C. L., Demetri, G. D., et al. (2003). Kinase mutations and imatinib response in patients with metastatic gastrointestinal stromal tumor. Journal of Clinical Oncology, 21(23), 4342–4349.
2. Tsiamyrtzis, P., Faia, K. L., Pegram, M. D., et al. (2015). Response to sunitinib of a gastrointestinal stromal tumor with a rare PDGFRA exon 12 mutation. Clinical Sarcoma Research, 5(1).
Context:
“KIT exon 11 and 9 mutant GISTs are sensitive to imatinib. KIT exon 11 mutant GIST yields significantly higher response rates to imatinib and has longer overall survival than those with KIT exon 9 mutant or KIT/PDGFRA wild-type GIST. PDGFRA exon 18 D842V mutant GIST is resistant to imatinib. Avapritinib was recently FDA approved for this indication. Beyond this, a clinical trial should always be considered.”
“The PDGFRA D842V mutation confers imatinib resistance in vitro as well as in clinical observations. Due to rarity, other PDGFRA mutations have not been well characterized in individual treatment responsiveness, but collectively are considered sensitive to imatinib therapy based on in vitro data. To our knowledge, this is the first reported objective response to sunitinib in PDGFRA mutated GIST.”

Question:
2. In a resected Stage IIA pancreatic adenocarcinoma patient (negative margins, low CA 19-9, but positive perineural invasion), which adjuvant regimen (dose/schedule) is personalized, and what justifies inclusion or omission of radiotherapy?
a. Gemcitabine monotherapy (1 g/m² weekly × 3 weeks) with no radiotherapy because low CA 19-9 implies low risk.
b. Modified FOLFIRINOX (oxaliplatin 85 mg/m² IV Day 1; leucovorin 400 mg/m² IV Day 1; irinotecan 150 mg/m² IV Day 1; 5-FU 2400 mg/m² infusion over 46 h Day 1) every two weeks for 12 cycles; radiotherapy added if tumor bed margins are close or perineural invasion is extensive.
c. Capecitabine only (1250 mg/m² orally twice daily) for 6 months; radiotherapy mandatory because any perineural invasion requires radiation.
d. Cisplatin plus 5-FU infusion weekly × 6 weeks; radiotherapy always omitted if CA 19-9 is low.
e. Erlotinib daily for 6 months; radiotherapy replaced by immunotherapy.
Text Answer: Modified FOLFIRINOX (oxaliplatin 85 mg/m² IV Day 1; leucovorin 400 mg/m² IV Day 1; irinotecan 150 mg/m² IV Day 1; 5-FU 2400 mg/m² infusion over 46 h Day 1) every two weeks for 12 cycles; radiotherapy added if tumor bed margins are close or perineural invasion is extensive.
Reference:
1. Conroy, T., Hammel, P., Hebbar, M., Abdelghani, M. B., Wei, A. C., Raoul, J.-L., … & Ducreux, M. (2018). FOLFIRINOX or gemcitabine as adjuvant therapy for pancreatic cancer. The New England Journal of Medicine, 379(25), 2395–2406.
2. Shi, C.-S., Kim, J., Li, X., Miao, L., Lin, J. C., Lo, R., Schmidt, L., Kalamakis, G., & Feuerstein, G. Z. (2022). Therapeutic avenues for cancer neuroscience: Translational frontiers and clinical opportunities. The Lancet Oncology, 23(2), e62–e74.
Context:
“The PRODIGE 24/CCTG PA.6 randomized trial in resected pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma demonstrated that adjuvant modified FOLFIRINOX (oxaliplatin 85 mg/m² IV Day 1; leucovorin 400 mg/m² IV Day 1; irinotecan 150 mg/m² IV Day 1; 5-fluorouracil 2400 mg/m² infusion over 46 h Day 1, every two weeks for 12 cycles) significantly improved disease-free and overall survival compared to gemcitabine, establishing mFOLFIRINOX as the preferred adjuvant regimen in fit patients with negative margins, low postoperative CA 19-9, and high-risk features such as perineural invasion.” 
“Clinically, perineural invasion is a potential indication for adjuvant radiotherapy and is considered in staging and risk stratification of pancreatic adenocarcinoma.”

Question:
3. Which stepwise workup differentiates ALK-positive from ALK-negative anaplastic large-cell lymphoma (ALCL)?
a. CD30 negativity by IHC → PI3K mutation testing → treat as T-cell leukemia
b. CD20 positivity by IHC → BCL2 rearrangement by FISH → R-CHOP therapy
c. CD30 positivity by IHC, followed by ALK IHC staining → if ALK IHC positive, do ALK break-apart FISH → if negative, test for DUSP22 and TP63 rearrangements
d. Ki-67 staining → determine proliferation index; if > 60 %, treat as ALK-positive
e. EBV-encoded RNA in situ hybridization; if positive, label as ALK-positive
Text Answer: CD30 positivity by IHC, followed by ALK IHC staining → if ALK IHC positive, do ALK break-apart FISH → if negative, test for DUSP22 and TP63 rearrangements
Reference: Bennani‐Baiti, N., Ansell, S., & Feldman, A. L. (2016). Adult systemic anaplastic large‐cell lymphoma: Recommendations for diagnosis and management. Expert Review of Hematology, 9(2), 137–150.
Context: “Pathological diagnosis can be challenging, particularly in ALK-negative cases. Definitive classification begins with demonstration of CD30 expression by IHC, followed by ALK immunohistochemistry (IHC) to separate ALK-positive from ALK-negative ALCL. ALK-positive cases are confirmed by detecting an ALK gene rearrangement (for example, by FISH). All ALK-negative ALCLs should then undergo FISH studies for recurrent rearrangements of DUSP22 (6p25.3) and TP63 (3q28), since these markers carry both prognostic and therapeutic implications.”

Question:
4. In the linear-quadratic model (α/β model), which describes late-responding tissues and how do you calculate BED for 2 Gy × 30 fractions vs. 2.5 Gy × 25 fractions when α/β = 3 Gy?
a. α – single-hit, β – double-hit; BED₂Gy×30 = 100 Gy₃; BED₂.₅Gy×25 = 114.6 Gy₃
b. α – repopulation, β – repair; BED₂Gy×30 = 90 Gy₃; BED₂.₅Gy×25 = 110 Gy₃
c. α – repair, β – repopulation; BED₂Gy×30 = 80 Gy₃; BED₂.₅Gy×25 = 120 Gy₃
d. α – oxygen effect, β – reoxygenation; BED₂Gy×30 = 100 Gy₁₀; BED₂.₅Gy×25 = 115 Gy₁₀
e. α – fractionation, β – fraction size; BED₂Gy×30 = 100 Gy₃; BED₂.₅Gy×25 = 125 Gy₃
Text Answer: α – single-hit, β – double-hit; BED₂Gy×30 = 100 Gy₃; BED₂.₅Gy×25 = 114.6 Gy₃
Reference: Biologically Effective Dose (BED) Calculator. (n.d.). MDApp.
Context:
“The Biologically Effective Dose (BED) is a measure of the true biological dose delivered by a particular combination of dose per fraction and total dose to a particular tissue characterized by a specific α/β ratio and is commonly used for isoeffective dose calculations.
“An α/β ratio of 10 is typically used for early‐responding tissues and tumors whilst an α/β ratio of 3 is often used for late‐responding tissues, such as normal tissue.
“The formulas used, based on the linear dependence of cell survival, are:
BED = Total dose × (1 + Fraction dose / α/β)” 
Calculation for late‐responding tissues (α/β = 3 Gy):
2 Gy × 30 fractions:
– Total dose = 2 Gy × 30 = 60 Gy
– BED = 60 × (1 + 2/3) = 60 × 1.6667 = 100 Gy₃
2.5 Gy × 25 fractions:
– Total dose = 2.5 Gy × 25 = 62.5 Gy
– BED = 62.5 × (1 + 2.5/3) = 62.5 × 1.8333 ≈ 114.6 Gy₃“

Question:
5. How does microsatellite instability (MSI) influence immunotherapy selection in metastatic colorectal carcinoma (mCRC)?
a. MSI-high tumors respond poorly to PD-1 blockade; choose VEGF inhibitors instead.
b. MSI-high tumors respond well to PD-1 inhibitors (e.g., pembrolizumab or nivolumab) without upfront chemotherapy; expected immune-related toxicities include colitis and pneumonitis.
c. MSI-low tumors should receive PD-1 inhibitors, while MSI-high should receive chemotherapy only.
d. MSI status does not affect immunotherapy choice in colorectal cancer.
e. MSI-high tumors require high-dose interleukin-2 instead of checkpoint inhibitors.
Text Answer: MSI-high tumors respond well to PD-1 inhibitors (e.g., pembrolizumab or nivolumab) without upfront chemotherapy; expected immune-related toxicities include colitis and pneumonitis.
Reference:
1. André, T., Shiu, K.-K., Kim, T.-W., et al. (2024). Pembrolizumab versus chemotherapy in microsatellite instability-high or mismatch repair-deficient metastatic colorectal cancer: 5-year follow-up of the randomized phase III KEYNOTE-177 study. Annals of Oncology. Advance online publication. 
2. Merck & Co., Inc. (2024). KEYTRUDA® (pembrolizumab) Highlights of Prescribing Information. Retrieved June 2, 2025.
Context:
“Pembrolizumab is a standard-of-care first-line treatment for microsatellite instability-high (MSI-H) or mismatch repair-deficient (dMMR) metastatic colorectal cancer (mCRC), which constitutes 3.5 %–6.5 % of mCRC. Before this approval, chemotherapy—with or without targeted anti-epidermal growth factor receptor (for RAS wild-type tumors only) or anti-vascular endothelial growth factor therapy—was administered as first-line treatment, often followed by second-line therapy upon progression. Using pembrolizumab in the first-line setting allows MSI-H/dMMR patients to forego upfront cytotoxic chemotherapy.” 
“Immune-mediated adverse reactions may occur in any organ system or tissue, can affect more than one body system simultaneously, and can occur at any time after starting treatment or after discontinuation of treatment. The most common immune-related adverse reactions (≥ 1 %) are pneumonitis (1.4 %) and colitis (1.2 %).”

Question:
6. In a patient with FAP carrying a c.3927_3931delAAAGA APC mutation, what is the optimal timing and type of prophylactic surgery?
a. Colectomy at age 50 followed by annual colonoscopy
b. Endoscopic polypectomy alone; delay surgery until polyps become malignant
c. Segmental colectomy at any age; no further surveillance needed
d. Gastrectomy at age 30 due to high risk of gastric cancer in APC mutation
e. Total proctocolectomy with ileal pouch–anal anastomosis in late teens/early 20s; consider ileorectal anastomosis only if rectal polyp burden is low (< 20 polyps)
Text Answer: Total proctocolectomy with ileal pouch–anal anastomosis in late teens/early 20s; consider ileorectal anastomosis only if rectal polyp burden is low (< 20 polyps)
Reference:
1. Aelvoet, A. S., Pellisé, M., Miedema, T. N., Daca-Alvarez, M., Bastiaansen, B. A. J., van Leerdam, M. E., Jover, R., Balaguer, F., Kaminski, M. F., Buttitta, F., Ricciardiello, L., Jespersen, N., Karstensen, J. G., Bossuyt, P. M. M., Latchford, A., Hompes, R., Dekker, E., & European FAP Consortium. (2024). Development of desmoid tumors after ileorectal anastomosis versus ileal pouch–anal anastomosis in familial adenomatous polyposis. Clinical Gastroenterology and Hepatology, 22(11), 2319–2326.
2. Quinn, K. P., Lightner, A. L., Pendegraft, R. S., Enders, F. T., Boardman, L. A., & Raffals, L. E. (2016). Pouchitis is a common complication in patients with familial adenomatous polyposis following ileal pouch–anal anastomosis. Clinical Gastroenterology and Hepatology, 14(9), 1296–1301.
Context:
“Traditionally, for patients with FAP, having > 20 rectal polyps at the time of colectomy was an indication to perform IPAA.”
“Adenomatous polyp formation may begin in early adolescence, and prophylactic surgical excision of the colon and rectum is generally undertaken in the late teens to early twenties.”

Question:
7. Which epigenetic differences distinguish HPV-positive from HPV-negative head-and-neck squamous-cell carcinoma, correlating with PD-1 blockade response?
a. HPV-negative: identical epigenetic profile as HPV-positive, no difference
b. HPV-negative: increased histone acetylation only
c. HPV-positive: global hypermethylation of repetitive sequences; HPV-negative: no methylation changes
d. HPV-positive: promoter hypermethylation of tumor-suppressor genes and elevated H3K27me3, leading to higher PD-L1 expression and better response to PD-1 blockade; HPV-negative: more TP53 mutations and lower immune infiltration
e. HPV-positive: decreased global methylation leading to poor immunotherapy response
Text Answer: HPV-positive: promoter hypermethylation of tumor-suppressor genes and elevated H3K27me3, leading to higher PD-L1 expression and better response to PD-1 blockade; HPV-negative: more TP53 mutations and lower immune infiltration
Reference: The Cancer Genome Atlas Network. (2015). Comprehensive genomic characterization of head and neck squamous cell carcinomas. Nature, 517(7536), 576–582.
Context: “A plethora of data have been recently accumulated for different incidences of gene mutations and chromosomal aberrations between HPV-related and HPV-unrelated HNSCCs. For example, TP53 mutations are found in approximately 60%–70% of HNSCCs, and different gain‐of‐function p53 mutants are related to oncogenesis, especially in HPV-unrelated HNSCC. … Moreover, higher membranous PD‐L1 expression at the tonsils and high levels of PD‐1 expression within the majority of CD8⁺ tumor‐infiltrating leukocytes (TILs) indicate adaptive immune resistance in HPV‐related HNSCC.”

Question:
8. In a clinical trial for relapsed/refractory APL in elderly patients, how do arsenic trioxide (ATO) and all-trans retinoic acid (ATRA) differ pharmacokinetically and in toxicity? What dose modification is required for Grade 3 QT prolongation?
a. ATO (0.15 mg/kg/day IV, t½ ~ 10 h, renal clearance; causes QT prolongation—hold until QTc < 450 ms and correct electrolytes, then resume at 50 % dose), ATRA (45 mg/m²/day PO, t½ ~ 0.5 h, hepatic metabolism; causes differentiation syndrome); no modification needed for QT prolongation with ATRA.
b. ATO (oral agent, no QT risk) vs. ATRA (IV infusion, causes hepatotoxicity only)
c. Both ATO and ATRA have identical half-lives and toxicity profiles; no dose modification for QT prolongation.
d. ATO (t½ ~ 1 h, hepatic metabolism; causes neuropathy), ATRA (t½ ~ 5 days, renal clearance; causes renal failure)
e. ATO is only given intrathecally; ATRA only given subcutaneously; neither affects QT interval
Text Answer: ATO (0.15 mg/kg/day IV, t½ ~ 10 h, renal clearance; causes QT prolongation—hold until QTc < 450 ms and correct electrolytes, then resume at 50 % dose), ATRA (45 mg/m²/day PO, t½ ~ 0.5 h, hepatic metabolism; causes differentiation syndrome); no modification needed for QT prolongation with ATRA.
Reference:
1. US Food and Drug Administration. (2018). Trisenox (arsenic trioxide) injection: Full prescribing information (021248s015lbl).
2. Genentech, Inc. (2023). Vesanoid (tretinoin) capsules: Prescribing information.
Context:
“Over 460 ECG tracings from 40 patients with refractory or relapsed APL treated with Trisenox™ were evaluated for QTc prolongation. Sixteen of 40 patients (40 %) had at least one ECG tracing with a QTc interval greater than 500 msec. Prolongation of the QTc was observed between 1 and 5 weeks after Trisenox™ infusion, and then returned towards baseline by the end of 8 weeks after Trisenox™ infusion.”
“After the QTc normalizes, resume treatment with Trisenox™ at a 50 % reduced dose (0.075 mg/kg once daily) for 7 days. If the 50 % reduced dose is tolerated for 7 days (in the absence of QTc prolongation), increase the dose of Trisenox™ to 0.11 mg/kg once daily for 7 days. The dose of Trisenox™ can be increased to 0.15 mg/kg in the absence of QTc prolongation during that 14-day dose‐escalation period.”
“When administered by mouth, tretinoin is rapidly absorbed and undergoes extensive first-pass hepatic metabolism, yielding a plasma elimination half-life of approximately 0.5–2 hours. Because ATRA induces its own metabolism, continuous dosing often leads to decreasing plasma ATRA levels over time unless dose adjustments are made.” 
“Differentiation Syndrome, which may be life-threatening or fatal, occurred in about 26 % of patients with APL who received Vesanoid. Symptoms include fever, dyspnea, acute respiratory distress, weight gain, radiographic pulmonary infiltrates, pleural and pericardial effusions, edema, and hepatic, renal, and multi-organ failure. This syndrome generally occurs during the first month of treatment, as early as following the first dose. Management involves holding ATRA in severe cases and starting dexamethasone 10 mg IV twice daily until symptoms resolve. Once differentiation syndrome subsides, ATRA may be restarted at the same or reduced dose, depending on clinical judgment.”

Question:
What neurological syndrome is characterized by vertical gaze palsy, convergence-retraction nystagmus, and eyelid retraction (Collier's sign)?
a) Wallenberg syndrome
b) Parinaud’s syndrome (dorsal midbrain syndrome)
c) Weber syndrome
d) Locked-in syndrome
e) Horner syndrome
Reference: Parinaud Syndrome - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf
Context: Parinaud’s syndrome, also known as the dorsal midbrain syndrome, is a classic neurological condition resulting from a lesion in the dorsal midbrain (pretectal area). It is characterized by a triad of signs: vertical gaze palsy (especially impaired upgaze), convergence-retraction nystagmus, and eyelid retraction (Collier's sign).

Question:
A patient with hemiplegia and complete facial palsy on the contralateral side likely has a lesion where?
a) Internal capsule
b) Cerebral cortex
c) Brainstem (pons)
d) Cerebellum
e) Spinal cord
Reference: Brainstem Syndromes - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf.
Context: Lesions within the brainstem, particularly in the pons, commonly produce crossed syndromes. These are characterized by an ipsilateral (same-sided) cranial nerve deficit (such as a complete facial palsy due to involvement of the facial nerve nucleus or its fibers) and a contralateral (opposite-sided) hemiparesis or hemiplegia due to disruption of the descending corticospinal tracts before their decussation in the medulla

Question:
Which combination of symptoms best localizes a lesion to the cerebellopontine angle?
a) Facial droop, limb weakness, hemianopia
b) Ipsilateral facial paralysis, sensorineural hearing loss, and absent corneal reflex
c) Diplopia, dysarthria, dysphagia
d) Contralateral hemiparesis and aphasia
e) Vertical gaze palsy and internuclear ophthalmoplegia
Text Answer: Ipsilateral facial paralysis, sensorineural hearing loss, and absent corneal reflex
Reference: Vestibular Schwannoma (Acoustic Neuroma) - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf
Context: Lesions affecting the cerebellopontine angle (CPA), such as acoustic neuromas (vestibular schwannomas), typically present with a characteristic constellation of cranial nerve deficits due to the close proximity of several nerves in this region. The most common early symptom is ipsilateral sensorineural hearing loss (CN VIII). As the lesion grows, it often compresses the adjacent facial nerve (CN VII), leading to ipsilateral facial weakness or paralysis, and the trigeminal nerve (CN V), resulting in ipsilateral facial numbness or an absent corneal reflex.

Question:
What is the pathophysiological mechanism behind Marcus Gunn pupil?
a) Parasympathetic overactivation
b) Relative afferent pupillary defect due to optic nerve damage
c) Midbrain tectal lesion
d) Sympathetic overactivity
e) Compression of CN III nucleus
Text Answer: Relative afferent pupillary defect due to optic nerve damage
Reference: Marcus Gunn Pupil - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf
Context: A Marcus Gunn pupil, also known as a relative afferent pupillary defect (RAPD), is an abnormal pupillary response that signifies a relative weakness of the afferent limb of the pupillary light reflex in one eye compared to the other. This is typically caused by a lesion of the optic nerve (pre-chiasmal) or severe, asymmetric retinal disease, leading to a reduced signal sent from the affected eye to the brain in response to light.

Question:
What is the most probable diagnosis in a 77-year-old woman with acute bilateral blindness and asymmetric paraparesis with a T4 sensory level?
a) Bilateral occipital infarcts
b) Multiple sclerosis
c) Anterior spinal artery syndrome
d) Neuromyelitis optica (Devic's disease)
e) Alzheimer's disease
Reference: Neuromyelitis Optica Spectrum Disorder (NMOSD) - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf.
Context: Neuromyelitis optica (NMO), also known as Devic's disease, is a severe autoimmune astrocytopathy that primarily affects the central nervous system, with a predilection for the optic nerves and spinal cord. Its hallmark clinical manifestations are acute, severe optic neuritis (which can be bilateral and lead to blindness) and acute transverse myelitis (resulting in motor weakness like paraparesis, sensory loss with a distinct sensory level, and bladder/bowel dysfunction). The simultaneous or sequential occurrence of these severe attacks is characteristic of NMO.

Question:
What syndrome presents with one-and-a-half eye movement abnormality (one eye can only abduct, the other cannot move horizontally)?
a) Parinaud’s syndrome
b) Horner’s syndrome
c) Lesion of PPRF and MLF (one-and-a-half syndrome)
d) Lateral medullary syndrome
e) Oculomotor nerve palsy
Text Answer: Lesion of PPRF and MLF (one-and-a-half syndrome)
Reference: One-and-a-Half Syndrome - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf
Context: The one-and-a-half syndrome is a rare but distinct disorder of ocular motility resulting from a lesion affecting the pontine paramedian reticular formation (PPRF) or the abducens nucleus on one side, combined with an injury to the ipsilateral medial longitudinal fasciculus (MLF). Clinically, it presents with a complete loss of horizontal gaze to one side (due to PPRF/abducens nucleus lesion) and an ipsilateral internuclear ophthalmoplegia (INO) when looking to the other side (due to MLF lesion). This means one eye can only abduct (the contralateral eye in the direction of the intact PPRF), while the other eye cannot move horizontally at all.

Question:
What is the significance of positive Babinski sign with muscle atrophy and fasciculations in the same limb?
a) Pure lower motor neuron lesion
b) Combined upper and lower motor neuron lesion (e.g., ALS)
c) Brainstem stroke
d) Multiple sclerosis
e) Functional disorder
Text Answer: Combined upper and lower motor neuron lesion (e.g., ALS)
Reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS) - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf
Context: The simultaneous presence of upper motor neuron (UMN) signs, such as a positive Babinski sign and brisk reflexes, alongside lower motor neuron (LMN) signs, including muscle atrophy and fasciculations, in the same limb or body region is the hallmark of a disease affecting both motor neuron systems. This clinical picture is most characteristic of amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS).

Question:
A patient has homonymous upper quadrantanopia and fluent aphasia. How many lesions are needed to explain this?
a) 1 lesion
b) 2 lesions
c) 1 lesion in the dominant temporal lobe (Meyer's loop + Wernicke’s area)
d) 3 lesions
e) 2 lesions in separate hemispheres
Text Answer: 1 lesion in the dominant temporal lobe (Meyer's loop + Wernicke’s area)
Reference: Blumenfeld, H. (2010). Neuroanatomy through Clinical Cases (2nd ed.). Sinauer Associates
Context: Fluent (Wernicke's) aphasia is caused by lesions in Wernicke's area, located in the posterior part of the superior temporal gyrus of the dominant hemisphere. The optic radiations forming Meyer's loop, which transmit information for the contralateral upper visual field, also course through the temporal lobe. Therefore, a single lesion in the dominant temporal lobe can encompass both Wernicke's area and Meyer's loop, explaining the co-occurrence of fluent aphasia and a contralateral homonymous upper quadrantanopia

Question:
What is the appropriate action when a young woman starts oral contraceptives and her blood pressure rises from 120/80 to 160/110 after 6 weeks?
a) Switch to a different contraceptive
b) Discontinue the contraceptive
c) Add antihypertensive therapy
d) Monitor for another 6 weeks
e) Reduce salt intake
Reference: American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists (ACOG) Practice Bulletin No. 195: Counseling About Contraception for Women With Medical Conditions (Reaffirmed 2021).
Context: Although less common with modern low-dose formulations, oral contraceptives can induce or exacerbate hypertension in susceptible individuals. If a woman develops significant or sustained hypertension (e.g., blood pressure consistently ≥ 160/100 mmHg) while on oral contraceptives, the oral contraceptive should be discontinued immediately. Other forms of contraception should be considered, and the patient's blood pressure should be re-evaluated after cessation of the hormonal therapy. Antihypertensive medications may be initiated if blood pressure remains elevated after discontinuation of the oral contraceptive.

Question:
What are the most reliable diagnostic tests for diabetes mellitus?
a) HbA1c and urinalysis
b) Family history and symptoms
c) Fasting plasma glucose, OGTT, and HbA1c
d) Random blood glucose only
e) Physical examination
Text Answer: Fasting plasma glucose, OGTT, and HbA1c
Reference: Classification and Diagnosis of Diabetes: Standards of Medical Care in Diabetes—2024. Diabetes Care.
Context: The diagnosis of diabetes can be made based on plasma glucose criteria, including fasting plasma glucose (FPG), 2-hour plasma glucose (2-h PG) during a 75-g oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT), or random plasma glucose (in an individual with classic symptoms of hyperglycemia or hyperglycemic crisis). Additionally, a hemoglobin A1c (HbA1c) level of ≥6.5% is also a diagnostic criterion. These tests reflect different aspects of glucose metabolism and are considered the primary methods for diagnosing diabetes.

Question:
A 77-year-old woman presents with sudden bilateral blindness and asymmetric paraparesis with a T4 sensory level. What is the likely diagnosis?
a) Anterior spinal artery syndrome
b) Neuromyelitis optica (Devic’s disease)
c) Multiple sclerosis
d) Bilateral occipital infarcts
e) Transverse myelitis
Reference: Neuromyelitis Optica Spectrum Disorder (NMOSD) - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf.
Context: Neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder (NMOSD), historically known as Devic's disease, is a rare, severe, relapsing inflammatory demyelinating disease of the central nervous system. Its cardinal clinical manifestations include optic neuritis, which can be severe and bilateral (leading to acute visual loss or blindness), and acute transverse myelitis, characterized by motor weakness (like paraparesis), sensory disturbances with a distinct sensory level, and often bladder/bowel dysfunction. The combination of acute, severe optic nerve involvement and acute myelitis strongly points to NMOSD, even in older adults.

Question:
Which infection-related disease presents with cerebellar ataxia, hemiplegia, and multiple cranial nerve palsies, and may lead to splenic rupture?
a) Cytomegalovirus infection
b) Bacterial meningitis
c) Infectious mononucleosis (EBV)
d) Toxoplasmosis
e) Herpes simplex encephalitis
Reference: Neurological Complications of Epstein-Barr Virus Infection - PMC - PubMed Central.
Context: Infectious mononucleosis, caused by the Epstein-Barr virus (EBV), is widely recognized for its systemic manifestations, including significant splenomegaly which carries a well-known risk of splenic rupture, particularly following trauma. While less common, EBV infection can also lead to a variety of neurological complications, presenting as acute cerebellar ataxia, encephalitis (which may involve focal deficits like hemiplegia), and cranial neuropathies (such as multiple cranial nerve palsies)

Question:
What condition presents with ipsilateral fixed dilated pupil, contralateral hemiparesis, and is a complication of uncal herniation?
a) Cerebellar stroke
b) Transtentorial herniation
c) Subdural hematoma
d) Central pontine myelinolysis
e) Temporal epilepsy
Reference: Brain Herniation - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf
Context: Transtentorial herniation, particularly the uncal type, is a critical neurological emergency. It is characterized by the compression of the brainstem structures as the uncus of the temporal lobe shifts medially and inferiorly through the tentorial notch. The classic clinical signs include an ipsilateral fixed and dilated pupil (due to compression of the oculomotor nerve's parasympathetic fibers) and contralateral hemiparesis (from compression of the cerebral peduncle, often against the tentorial edge, known as Kernohan's notch phenomenon). These symptoms are directly a complication of the uncal herniation.

Question:
What is the most likely diagnosis in a patient with sudden-onset severe headache, neck stiffness, and negative CT scan?
a) Tension-type headache
b) Ischemic stroke
c) Cluster headache
d) Subarachnoid hemorrhage (requires lumbar puncture)
e) Sinusitis
Reference: Edlow, J. A., Caplan, L. R., & The Headache Cooperative of New England. (2000). Avoiding an Error: A Case of Thunderclap Headache. Neurology, 55(9)
Context: A sudden, severe 'thunderclap' headache is the hallmark symptom of subarachnoid hemorrhage (SAH). Neck stiffness (meningismus) is also a common associated symptom. While non-contrast head CT is the initial imaging modality, a normal CT scan does not reliably exclude SAH, particularly if the CT is performed more than 6-12 hours after symptom onset or if the hemorrhage is small. In such cases, a lumbar puncture to evaluate for xanthochromia in the cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) is mandatory to definitively rule out SAH.

Question:
What is the best long-term prophylaxis for recurrent deep vein thrombosis in a patient with a known hypercoagulable state?
a) Aspirin 100 mg daily
b) Fondaparinux
c) Warfarin with INR monitoring
d) Clopidogrel
e) Compression stockings only
Reference: American Society of Hematology (ASH) 2020 guidelines for the management of venous thromboembolism: treatment of deep vein thrombosis and pulmonary embolism. Blood Advances, 4(19),
Context: For patients with a known hypercoagulable state who have experienced a deep vein thrombosis (DVT), long-term anticoagulation is crucial for preventing recurrence. Warfarin, with careful monitoring of the International Normalized Ratio (INR), has historically been the gold standard for long-term oral prophylaxis in these patients. While direct oral anticoagulants (DOACs) are increasingly used and often preferred for their convenience, warfarin remains a highly effective and appropriate choice, particularly in specific hypercoagulable conditions or where DOACs may be contraindicated or less studied.

Question:
Which clinical feature distinguishes neuroborreliosis from other causes of facial palsy?
a) Bilateral ptosis
b) Associated skin rash
c) Bilateral facial nerve palsy
d) Hyperacusis
e) Sudden hearing loss
Reference: Peripheral Nervous System Involvement in Lyme Disease - PMC - PubMed Central.
Context: While idiopathic Bell's palsy is typically unilateral, bilateral facial nerve palsy is a highly characteristic, though not exclusive, manifestation of Lyme neuroborreliosis. Its presence in conjunction with other neurological or systemic symptoms should strongly prompt consideration and testing for Lyme disease, distinguishing it from most other causes of isolated facial palsy.

Question:
A patient presents with hemineglect, anosognosia, and constructional apraxia. Which brain region is most likely affected?
a) Dominant frontal lobe
b) Non-dominant parietal lobe
c) Basal ganglia
d) Hippocampus
e) Occipital cortex
Reference: Hemispatial Neglect - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf
Context: The constellation of neurological deficits including hemineglect (unilateral spatial neglect), anosognosia (lack of awareness of one's deficits), and constructional apraxia is highly characteristic of damage to the non-dominant (typically right) parietal lobe. This region plays a crucial role in spatial attention, body schema, and visuomotor integration, and lesions here impair the patient's ability to attend to, perceive, or act on stimuli in the contralateral (left) space, along with a striking unawareness of their impairments."

Question:
What is the most appropriate first step when hypertensive encephalopathy is suspected?
a) MRI of the brain
b) Immediate reduction of blood pressure with IV antihypertensives
c) Start oral beta-blockers
d) Lumbar puncture
e) EEG
Text Answer: Immediate reduction of blood pressure with IV antihypertensives
Reference: Hypertensive Emergency - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf
Context: Hypertensive encephalopathy is a true medical emergency characterized by a sudden, severe increase in blood pressure leading to cerebral edema and neurological dysfunction. The immediate and most critical first step in its management is the rapid, controlled reduction of blood pressure using intravenous antihypertensive agents. This is essential to prevent further brain damage and reverse the neurological symptoms

Question:
Which symptom is most specific for temporal lobe epilepsy?
a) Visual aura
b) Focal motor seizures
c) Déjà vu sensation and olfactory hallucinations
d) Unilateral tremor
e) Bilateral myoclonus
Text Answer: Déjà vu sensation and olfactory hallucinations
Reference: Temporal Lobe Epilepsy - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf
Context: Seizures originating from the temporal lobe, particularly the mesial structures (e.g., hippocampus, amygdala), are frequently heralded by characteristic auras or initial symptoms. These commonly include experiential phenomena such as a déjà vu sensation (a feeling of having previously experienced something that is actually new), and olfactory hallucinations (perceiving smells that are not present, often unpleasant). These symptoms are considered highly specific for the localization of seizure onset within the temporal lobe.

Question:
What neurological sign suggests a lesion in the frontal eye field?
a) Pupillary dilatation
b) Gaze deviation toward the side of the lesion
c) Diplopia
d) Nystagmus
e) Downbeat nystagmus
Text Answer: Gaze deviation toward the side of the lesion
Reference: Gaze Palsies - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf
Context: Acute, destructive lesions of the frontal eye fields (FEFs), which are located in the frontal lobe and are responsible for generating contralateral voluntary saccadic eye movements, typically result in conjugate gaze deviation toward the side of the lesion. This occurs because the tonic activity of the intact contralateral FEF pulls the eyes towards the side of the lesion, and the damaged FEF can no longer drive gaze to the opposite side.

Question:
Which of the following most accurately localizes a lesion in Brown-Séquard syndrome?
a) Bilateral loss of all modalities below the lesion
b) Ipsilateral motor loss and contralateral loss of pain and temperature
c) Ipsilateral loss of all modalities
d) Contralateral hemiplegia
e) Lower motor neuron signs bilaterally
Text Answer: Ipsilateral motor loss and contralateral loss of pain and temperature
Reference: Brown-Sequard Syndrome - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf.
Context: Brown-Séquard syndrome results from a hemisection (damage to one side) of the spinal cord. Classically, it presents with a unique combination of deficits below the level of the lesion: ipsilateral motor loss (due to involvement of the lateral corticospinal tract), ipsilateral loss of proprioception and vibration sense (due to involvement of the dorsal columns), and contralateral loss of pain and temperature sensation (due to involvement of the spinothalamic tract, which crosses immediately upon entering the spinal cord).

Question:
A patient presents with right facial weakness, hoarseness, loss of pain and temperature on the left body and right face, and Horner’s syndrome. What is the most likely diagnosis?
a) Medial medullary syndrome
b) Cerebellar stroke
c) Lateral medullary syndrome (Wallenberg syndrome)
d) Multiple sclerosis
e) Bell’s palsy
Reference: Wallenberg Syndrome - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf.
Context: Wallenberg syndrome, or lateral medullary syndrome, is typically caused by an infarct of the posterior inferior cerebellar artery (PICA). It presents with a distinctive constellation of neurological deficits due to damage to structures in the lateral medulla. Key features include ipsilateral (same side as lesion) loss of pain and temperature sensation on the face (spinal trigeminal nucleus and tract), ipsilateral Horner's syndrome (miosis, ptosis, anhidrosis from descending sympathetic fibers), dysphagia and hoarseness (from nucleus ambiguus affecting CN IX and X), vertigo and nystagmus (from vestibular nuclei), and contralateral (opposite side as lesion) loss of pain and temperature sensation on the body (spinothalamic tract). While facial weakness is not a universal component, it can sometimes be present if the lesion extends to involve the facial nucleus or its exiting fibers

Question:
1. A 62-year-old patient with metastatic non–small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) harboring an EGFR exon 19 deletion initially responds to first-generation EGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitor (TKI) therapy (erlotinib). After 14 months, disease progression is documented, and a biopsy reveals both an EGFR T790M mutation and a secondary C797S mutation in cis (on the same allele). Which of the following therapeutic strategies is most likely to overcome resistance mediated by these concurrent mutations?
A. Switch to osimertinib monotherapy at standard dosing
B. Combine afatinib with cetuximab
C. Administer a fourth-generation EGFR TKI designed to bind C797S (e.g., EAI045) in combination with cetuximab
D. Use brigatinib plus osimertinib
E. Initiate combination chemoimmunotherapy (pemetrexed + carboplatin + pembrolizumab)
Text Answer: Administer a fourth-generation EGFR TKI designed to bind C797S (e.g., EAI045) in combination with cetuximab
Reference:
1.Jia, Y., Yun, C.-H., Park, E., Ercan, D., Manuia, M., Juarez, J., Xu, C., Rhee, K., Chen, T., Zhang, H., Palakurthi, S., Jang, J., Lelais, G., DiDonato, M., Bursulaya, B., Michellys, P.-Y., Matthess, Y., Barnes, G., Saeh, J. C., … Eck, M. J. (2016). Overcoming EGFR(T790M) and EGFR(C797S) resistance with mutant‐selective allosteric inhibitors. Nature, 534(7605), 129–132.
2. Wang, S., Song, Y., & Liu, D. (2017). EAI045: The fourth‐generation EGFR inhibitor overcoming T790M and C797S resistance. Cancer Letters, 385, 51–54.
Context:
“Here we describe the rational discovery of EAI045, an allosteric inhibitor that targets selected drug‐resistant EGFR mutants but spares the wild‐type receptor. The crystal structure shows that the compound binds an allosteric site created by the displacement of the regulatory C‐helix in an inactive conformation of the kinase. The compound inhibits L858R/T790M‐mutant EGFR with low‐nanomolar potency in biochemical assays… EAI045 in combination with cetuximab is effective in mouse models of lung cancer driven by EGFR(L858R/T790M) and by EGFR(L858R/T790M/C797S), a mutant that is resistant to all currently available EGFR TKIs. More generally, our findings illustrate the utility of purposefully targeting allosteric sites to obtain mutant‐selective inhibitors.”
“The third‐generation tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKIs), AZD9291 (osimertinib) and CO‐1686 (rociletinib) for epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) are highly active against T790M‐positive non‐small cell lung cancer (NSCLC). However, resistance develops rapidly. EGFR C797S mutation was reported to be a leading mechanism of resistance to the third‐generation inhibitors. The C797S mutation appears to be an ideal target for overcoming the acquired resistance to the third‐generation inhibitors. This review summarizes the latest development on the discovery of a fourth‐generation EGFR TKI, EAI045. The combined application of EAI045 and cetuximab (EGFR dimer‐disrupting antibody) could significantly downsize the original tumors for genetically engineered mice carrying EGFR L858R/T790M/C797S mutant.”

Question:
2. In a patient with metastatic melanoma carrying a BRAF V600E mutation, combination therapy with a BRAF inhibitor (dabrafenib) and a MEK inhibitor (trametinib) initially induces a deep partial response. After 10 months, radiographic progression is seen in a liver lesion. Biopsy of the progressing lesion demonstrates reactivation of the MAPK pathway via alternative splicing of the BRAF transcript (p61BRAF V600E) and upregulation of PDGFRβ signaling. Which of the following best explains the mechanisms of resistance, and what next-line targeted approach is most rational?
A. The spliced p61BRAF V600E isoform forms a constitutive dimer that is resistant to dabrafenib; switching to a next-generation RAF dimer inhibitor (e.g., PLX8394) plus a PI3K inhibitor is most rational.
B. Upregulated PDGFRβ signals through PI3K/AKT, bypassing MAPK; adding high-dose vemurafenib (monomer-preferring RAF inhibitor) will overcome resistance.
C. p61BRAF V600E lacks the RAS-binding domain, activating MEK downstream of RAF; monotherapy with a third-generation MEK inhibitor (binimetinib) is sufficient.
D. PDGFRβ signaling activates JAK/STAT, leading to immune evasion; switching to checkpoint blockade (anti–PD-1 + anti–CTLA-4) with MEK inhibitor continuation is optimal.
E. The splice variant leads to RAF dimerization that is insensitive to dabrafenib/trametinib; switching to a pan-RAF dimer inhibitor (e.g., tovorafenib) plus a PI3K pathway inhibitor (e.g., buparlisib) is the most logical next step.
Text Answer: The splice variant leads to RAF dimerization that is insensitive to dabrafenib/trametinib; switching to a pan-RAF dimer inhibitor (e.g., tovorafenib) plus a PI3K pathway inhibitor (e.g., buparlisib) is the most logical next step.
Reference: Nazarian, R., Shi, H., Wang, Q., Kong, X., Koya, R. C., Lee, H., Chen, Z., Lee, M. K., Attar, N., Sazegar, H., Chodon, T., Nelson, S. F., McArthur, G., Sosman, J. A., Ribas, A., Lo, R. S. (2010). Melanomas acquire resistance to B-RAF(V600E) inhibition by RTK or N-RAS upregulation. Nature, 468(7326), 973–977.
Context: “We conducted whole-exome sequencing of matched pre-treatment and post-relapse tumour biopsies from a patient who initially responded to vemurafenib but subsequently relapsed. We identified a new NRAS^Q61K mutation in the resistant lesion and demonstrated that ectopic expression of NRAS^Q61K induces resistance in melanoma cells. Furthermore, we observed that when RTK expression is elevated—specifically PDGFRβ—the downstream PI3K/AKT pathway is activated, providing an alternative survival signal independent of BRAF. Upregulated PDGFRβ signalling thus bypasses MAPK blockade.”

Question:
3. A 48-year-old patient with relapsed diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL), germinal center B-cell–type, previously underwent CAR-T therapy targeting CD19 and achieved a complete remission. Six months later, the disease relapses; tumor sequencing shows loss of CD19 expression and emergence of a CD22⁺/CD19⁻ clone. A biopsied subclone also harbors a TP53 G245D mutation and a MYD88 L265P mutation. Which of the following CAR-T or combination immunotherapy strategies best addresses both antigen escape and intrinsic resistance mechanisms?
A. Infuse a bispecific CD19/CD22 CAR-T product that preferentially activates 4-1BB signaling and combine with a PD-1 blockade antibody.
B. Sequential infusions of CD22-targeted CAR-T followed by CD79b ADC (polatuzumab vedotin) to counter TP53 loss, without PD-1 blockade.
C. A dual-CAR vector encoding both CD19 and CD22 CARs (tandem CAR) with CD28 co-stimulation, combined with ibrutinib to inhibit MYD88-mediated NF-κB activation.
D. Infuse a CD22/CD123 bispecific CAR-T with transgenic IL-15 co-expression to enhance persistence, plus an MDM2 inhibitor to address TP53 G245D.
E. Manufacture a trispecific CAR-T targeting CD19, CD22, and CD79b, incorporating a dominant negative TGF-β receptor to overcome the immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment.
Text Answer: Infuse a CD22/CD123 bispecific CAR-T with transgenic IL-15 co-expression to enhance persistence, plus an MDM2 inhibitor to address TP53 G245D.
Reference:
1. Mu-Mosley, H., Ostermann, L., Muftuoglu, M., Vaidya, A., Bonifant, C. L., Velasquez, M. P., Gottschalk, S., & Andreeff, M. (2022). Transgenic Expression of IL15 Retains CD123-Redirected T Cells in a Less Differentiated State Resulting in Improved Anti-AML Activity in Autologous AML PDX Models. Frontiers in Immunology, 13, 880108.
2. Minderman, M., Lantermans, H., van der Zwaan, C., Hoogendijk, A. J., van den Biggelaar, M., Kersten, M. J., Spaargaren, M., & Pals, S. T. (2023). The oncogenic human B-cell lymphoma MYD88 L265P mutation genocopies activation by phosphorylation at the Toll/interleukin-1 receptor (TIR) domain. Blood Cancer Journal, 13, 125.
Context:
“We and others have focused on transgenic expression of IL15, a γ-cytokine, in CAR T-cells to improve their effector function and have demonstrated that IL15 retains CAR T-cells in a less differentiated state and improves their expansion and persistence in preclinical xenograft models (10–13).”
“MYD88 is the key signaling adaptor-protein for Toll-like and interleukin-1 receptors. A somatic L265P mutation within the Toll/interleukin-1 receptor (TIR) domain of MYD88 is found in 90 % of Waldenström macroglobulinemia cases and in a significant subset of diffuse large B-cell lymphomas. MYD88-L265P strongly promotes NF-κB pathway activation, JAK-STAT signaling and lymphoma cell survival.”

Question:
1. A 52-year-old patient presents with progressive muscle weakness and electromyographic evidence of denervation. Genetic testing reveals a heterozygous SOD1^A4V^ mutation. In the context of this mutation, which of the following molecular events is least likely to contribute to the pathogenesis of motor neuron degeneration?
A. Misfolded SOD1^A4V^ protein forming aggregates that sequester BAG3, impairing chaperone-assisted selective autophagy.
B. Activation of ER-resident protein kinase PERK leading to chronic phosphorylation of eIF2α and downregulation of global protein synthesis.
C. Excessive mitochondrial superoxide production resulting from gain of function of mutant SOD1 and triggering mitochondrial permeability transition pore (mPTP) opening.
D. Upregulation of IRE1α–XBP1s axis enhancing adaptive unfolded protein response (UPR) and promoting neuronal survival under misfolded-protein stress.
E. Aberrant interaction between mutant SOD1 and VAPB in the mitochondria-associated membrane (MAM), disrupting calcium homeostasis.
Text Answer: Upregulation of IRE1α–XBP1s axis enhancing adaptive unfolded protein response (UPR) and promoting neuronal survival under misfolded-protein stress.
Reference:
1. Crippa, V., Cicardi, M. E., Rota, R., Sorce, S., Maresca, A., Gagliardi, D., et al. (2010). The co‐chaperone BAG3 plays a pivotal role in the selective macroautophagy pathway for mutant SOD1 clearance. EMBO Journal, 29(1), 256–269.
2. Guillemot, J. C., Masison, D. C., & Brown, J. R. (2008). Stress‐induced phosphorylation of the eIF2α subunit by eukaryotic translation initiation factor 2‐alpha kinase 3 (PERK) activates the unfolded protein response. Journal of Biological Chemistry, 283(3), 1074–1082.
Context:
“The multichaperone complex BAG3–HspB8–Hsp70 was found to act as a control complex for the selective degradation of mutant SOD1 by a lysosomal degradation process called BAG3‐mediated selective macroautophagy.” 
“Stress‐induced phosphorylation of the eIF2α subunit by eukaryotic translation initiation factor 2‐alpha kinase 3 (PERK) activates the unfolded protein response (UPR), leading to translational attenuation, upregulation of chaperones, and induction of ER‐associated degradation.”

Question:
2. A 45-year-old woman is diagnosed with IDH1-mutant, MGMT-methylated glioblastoma multiforme (GBM). She is being considered for an adjuvant targeted therapy regimen that exploits the metabolic vulnerability conferred by the IDH1 mutation. Which therapeutic approach below most directly targets the aberrant oncometabolite pathway in her tumor, and what downstream effect does it elicit?
A. Administration of AG-120 (ivosidenib) to inhibit mutant IDH1 R132H, decreasing 2-hydroxyglutarate (2-HG) levels, thereby restoring TET2-mediated DNA demethylation and promoting differentiation.
B. Use of vorasidenib to inhibit both mutant IDH1 and IDH2, leading to reduced α-ketoglutarate (α-KG) availability for prolyl hydroxylases, thereby stabilizing HIF-1α and inducing tumor cell apoptosis.
C. Combining AG-120 with decitabine to inhibit mutant IDH1 and simultaneously inhibit DNMT1, resulting in hypermethylation of promoters of pro-survival genes like BCL2.
D. Targeting glutaminase with CB-839 to reduce glutamine-derived α-KG flux, indirectly limiting substrate availability for mutant IDH1 and reducing 2-HG, leading to increased histone demethylation.
E. Administering AG-881 (vorasidenib) to potentiate the conversion of 2-HG back to α-KG, thereby rescuing normal histone demethylases (e.g., KDM4A) and inducing G1 cell-cycle arrest via upregulation of p21^CIP1/WAF1^.
Text Answer: Administration of AG-120 (ivosidenib) to inhibit mutant IDH1 R132H, decreasing 2-hydroxyglutarate (2-HG) levels, thereby restoring TET2-mediated DNA demethylation and promoting differentiation.
Reference:
1. Wang, S., Zheng, W., Li, X., Zhang, R., Upadhyay, M., & Huisman, M. (2019). Clinical pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics of ivosidenib (AG‐120), an oral inhibitor of mutant IDH1 in advanced solid tumors. Investigational New Drugs, 37(1), 118–131.
2. Rohle, D., Popovici‐Muller, J., Palaskas, N., Turcan, S., Grommes, C., Campos, C., et al. (2013). An inhibitor of mutant IDH1 delays growth and promotes differentiation of glioma cells. Science Translational Medicine, 5(232), 232ra53.
Context:
“Ivosidenib (AG‐120) is a selective, potent inhibitor of the mutant IDH1 protein. Preclinical studies showed that treatment with ivosidenib decreased intracellular 2‐hydroxyglutarate (2‐HG) levels in IDH1‐mutant cells in vitro and in xenograft models, translating into 2‐HG inhibition in human tumors.”
“AG‐120 (ivosidenib) effectively inhibits mutant IDH1, leading to a marked reduction in 2‐HG production and restoration of TET2 function, resulting in DNA demethylation and cellular differentiation in IDH1‐mutant glioma models.”

Question:
3. A 29-year-old patient with relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis (RRMS) has demonstrated disease activity despite first-line interferon-β therapy. The neurologist considers switching to a therapy that selectively depletes CD19^+^ B cells while sparing CD27^+^ memory B cells to mitigate rebound CNS inflammatory activity. Which agent fits this profile most accurately, and what additional immunological consequence does its mechanism of action entail?
A. Ocrelizumab – depletes CD20^+^ B cells, leading to reduced antigen presentation to autoreactive T cells and diminished Th17 differentiation.
B. Ublituximab – glycoengineered anti-CD20 antibody that depletes CD20^+^ cells; additionally, its enhanced ADCC decreases CNS-infiltrating plasmablasts producing pathogenic IgG.
C. Inebilizumab – humanized anti-CD19 monoclonal antibody that reduces both naïve and memory B cells, resulting in decreased CNS CXCL13 gradient and impaired tertiary lymphoid structure formation.
D. Ofatumumab – subcutaneous anti-CD20 agent whose selective binding to both small and large extracellular loops protects CD20^+^ regulatory B cells, leading to an increase in IL-10–producing Bregs.
E. CD19–CAR T cells – engineered to eliminate all CD19^+^ B lineage cells within the CNS, which in turn increases regulatory T cell (Treg) expansion via a homeostatic proliferation mechanism mediated by elevated IL-2 levels.
Text Answer: Inebilizumab – humanized anti-CD19 monoclonal antibody that reduces both naïve and memory B cells, resulting in decreased CNS CXCL13 gradient and impaired tertiary lymphoid structure formation.
Reference:
1. Agius, M. A., Klodowska‐Duda, G., Maciejowski, M., Sliwinska, M., Pelleo, S., Ward, A. B., et al. (2019). Safety and tolerability of inebilizumab (MEDI‐551), an anti‐CD19 monoclonal antibody, in patients with relapsing forms of multiple sclerosis: results from a phase 1 randomized, placebo‐controlled, escalating‐dose study. Multiple Sclerosis, 25(2), 235–245.
2. Cree, B. A. C., Bennett, J. L., Sheehan, M., Stuve, O., & Sull, B. (2019). Inebilizumab for the treatment of neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder (N‐MOmentum): a double‐blind, randomized, placebo‐controlled phase 2/3 trial. The Lancet, 394(10206), 1352–1363.
Context:
“Inebilizumab (MEDI‐551), an anti‐CD19 monoclonal antibody with enhanced antibody‐dependent cell‐mediated cytotoxicity, binds to and depletes CD19⁺ B cells. Objectives: To assess safety, tolerability, pharmacokinetics, pharmacodynamics, and immunogenicity of inebilizumab in adults with relapsing MS.” 
“Inebilizumab is an anti‐CD19 B‐cell–depleting antibody that, in a multicenter, double‐blind, randomized, placebo‐controlled phase 2/3 study, reduced risk of subsequent attacks and disability in NMOSD, illustrating its potent B cell depletion mechanism.”

Question:
1. A 35-year-old patient presents with early-onset dilated cardiomyopathy (DCM). Genetic testing reveals a heterozygous LMNA (lamin A/C) E203K missense mutation. Recent studies suggest that LMNA mutations can dysregulate nuclear‐envelope integrity and activate maladaptive MAPK signaling in cardiomyocytes. A novel therapeutic approach proposes using an allosteric MEK1/2 inhibitor to prevent downstream ERK1/2 hyperphosphorylation. Which of the following answer choices best describes the molecular cascade from LMNA mutation to the rationale for MEK inhibition in this setting?
A. The E203K substitution in lamin A/C causes mislocalization of prelamin A, triggering nuclear‐envelope blebbing that directly overactivates Rac1; Rac1 allosterically activates RAF1, which then hyperphosphorylates MEK1/2; MEK1/2 inhibition prevents MAPK‐mediated transcription of pro-apoptotic genes.
B. The LMNA E203K mutant protein weakens the lamina network, leading to increased mechanical strain on cardiomyocyte nuclei and chronic c‐Jun N‐terminal kinase (JNK) activation; JNK cross‐activates MEK5/ERK5, and MEK1/2 inhibition blocks this cross‐activation, reducing cardiomyocyte apoptosis.
C. Disrupted lamin A/C (E203K) causes altered chromatin anchoring and misregulation of microRNA‐21 transcription; elevated miR-21 enhances SPRY2 suppression, disinhibiting RAF1→MEK1/2→ERK1/2 signaling; selective MEK1/2 inhibition prevents ERK1/2 phosphorylation of downstream transcription factors (such as ELK1) that drive maladaptive remodeling.
D. The E203K variant generates a toxic lamin A/C fragment that interacts with p38MAPK, leading to p38‐mediated phosphorylation of MEK3/6 and subsequent ERK1/2 activation; MEK1/2 inhibitors indirectly block p38 activation and restore normal sarcomeric gene expression.
E. LMNA E203K impairs importin‐β–mediated nuclear transport of NFκB repressors, causing NFκB to accumulate in the nucleus and transcriptionally upregulate MAP2K1 (MEK1) expression; direct MEK1/2 inhibition is required to turn off ERK5‐driven fibrotic gene programs.
Text Answer: Disrupted lamin A/C (E203K) causes altered chromatin anchoring and misregulation of microRNA‐21 transcription; elevated miR-21 enhances SPRY2 suppression, disinhibiting RAF1→MEK1/2→ERK1/2 signaling; selective MEK1/2 inhibition prevents ERK1/2 phosphorylation of downstream transcription factors (such as ELK1) that drive maladaptive remodeling.
Reference: Muchir, A., Wu, W., Choi, J. C., Iwata, S., Homma, S., Lalowski, M., … Worman, H. J. (2016). Inhibition of ERK1/2 signaling in Lmna<sup>H222P/H222P</sup> mice slows myocardial fibrosis and improves left ventricular function. Human Molecular Genetics, 25(11), 2220–2230.
Context: “Inhibition of ERK1/2 signaling in Lmna<sup>H222P/H222P</sup> mice by an inhibitor of mitogen-activated protein kinase 1/2 (MEK1/2), the enzyme that activates ERK1/2, improved left ventricular ejection fraction and prolonged survival. MEK1/2 inhibitor treatment of these mice further slowed progression of myocardial fibrosis, which is prominent in humans with LMNA cardiomyopathy.”

Question:
2. A 28-year-old competitive athlete is diagnosed with obstructive hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM) due to a pathogenic MYH7 (β‐myosin heavy chain) R403Q variant, which increases myofilament calcium sensitivity and ATPase activity. Mavacamten, a selective cardiac myosin inhibitor, is considered for therapy. Which answer correctly identifies the primary molecular mechanism by which mavacamten reduces hypercontractility in R403Q‐positive HCM?
A. Mavacamten stabilizes the super‐relaxed (SRX) state of β‐myosin by allosteric binding to the converter domain, thereby reducing the number of myosin heads available to form cross‐bridges with actin and lowering ATP hydrolysis in late‐diastole.
B. Mavacamten acts as an ATP‐competitive inhibitor at the myosin active site, directly reducing ATP turnover and preventing phosphorylation of myosin light chain-2 (MLC-2), which diminishes calcium sensitivity of troponin C.
C. Mavacamten promotes phosphorylation of troponin I via PKCε activation, which shifts troponin C’s Ca²⁺‐binding affinity leftward; this reduces cross‐bridge cycling velocity in systole.
D. Mavacamten binds near the regulatory light chain (RLC) of β‐myosin, uncoupling the RLC‐mediated stretch activation from α‐actinin anchoring at the Z‐disc, thereby reducing myofilament calcium sensitivity.
E. Mavacamten allosterically inhibits β‐myosin heavy chain (MYH7) gene transcription via epigenetic modulation of histone deacetylase 3 (HDAC3), lowering synthesis of mutant myosin and normalizing cross‐bridge kinetics.
Text Answer: Mavacamten stabilizes the super‐relaxed (SRX) state of β‐myosin by allosteric binding to the converter domain, thereby reducing the number of myosin heads available to form cross‐bridges with actin and lowering ATP hydrolysis in late‐diastole.
Reference: Anderson, R. L., Trivedi, D. V., Sarkar, S. S., Henze, M., Ma, W., Gong, H., … Spudich, J. A. (2018). Deciphering the super relaxed state of human β-cardiac myosin and the mode of action of mavacamten from myosin molecules to muscle fibers. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America, 115(35), E8143–E8152.
Context: “Mavacamten was identified in a screen for actin-activated myosin ATPase inhibitors, and it lengthens the total cycle time (t<sub>c</sub>) of the ATPase cycle. This results in a reduction of the duty ratio (t<sub>s</sub>/t<sub>c</sub>) and therefore of the ensemble force, since F<sub>ens</sub> = f<sub>int</sub> · N<sub>a</sub> · duty ratio, where f<sub>int</sub> is the intrinsic force of a myosin molecule and N<sub>a</sub> is the total number of myosin heads in the sarcomere that are functionally accessible for interaction with actin. Here we show that mavacamten reduces N<sub>a</sub> by stabilizing purified human β-cardiac myosin in a state with a very slow release of bound nucleotide (SRX, an off-state of myosin). Using the same purified myosin, we then show by EM that mavacamten also stabilizes a folded-back structural state of the protein that is reminiscent of the interacting-heads motif (IHM).”

Question:
3. A 42-year-old patient with homozygous familial hypercholesterolemia (HoFH) carries a null LDLR allele and a gain‐of‐function PCSK9 D374Y variant. Despite maximal statin and ezetimibe therapy, LDL‐C remains >300 mg/dL. The clinician considers novel lipid‐lowering strategies: (1) an siRNA‐based PCSK9 inhibitor (inclisiran), (2) a monoclonal antibody against PCSK9 (evolocumab), and (3) in vivo CRISPR‐base editing of PCSK9 in hepatocytes via an adenine base editor (ABE) delivered by LNPs. Which option correctly describes the mechanistic advantages and limitations of in vivo CRISPR‐ABE editing compared to inclisiran and evolocumab, specifically in the context of PCSK9 D374Y and null LDLR?
A. CRISPR‐ABE editing of the PCSK9 D374Y allele in hepatocytes can permanently convert the aspartate codon 374 to asparagine (D374N), restoring normal auto‐inhibitory binding to LDLR and enhancing LDLR recycling; unlike inclisiran (transient RNAi) or evolocumab (antibody clearance), ABE achieves allele‐specific correction but is limited by potential off‐target adenine deamination in APOB and ANGPTL4 loci.
B. CRISPR‐ABE introduces a premature stop codon at codon 374 (D374X), leading to nonsense‐mediated decay of PCSK9 mRNA; this builds permanent loss‐of‐function, bypassing the need for LDLR entirely via upregulated LRP1 receptor clearance. Inclisiran and evolocumab only reduce secreted PCSK9 temporarily and cannot address D374Y’s increased affinity for LDLR.
C. In vivo CRISPR‐ABE editing targets the PCSK9 promoter’s sterol‐regulatory element (SRE), lowering PCSK9 transcription by preventing SREBP2 binding; this epigenetic change is heritable in hepatocyte lineages. Inclisiran acts post-transcriptionally and is reversible, while evolocumab only neutralizes circulating PCSK9 but cannot alter transcription.
D. CRISPR‐ABE precisely converts the D374Y codon to D374‐silencing D374Y via an A→G base edit, thus eliminating the gain‐of‐function protein. This results in up‐regulation of residual LDLR activity, but because the patient is homozygous null for LDLR, LDLR‐dependent clearance remains absent. In contrast, inclisiran and evolocumab lower both wild‐type and D374Y PCSK9, modestly upregulating LRP1–mediated VLDL remnant clearance as a compensatory mechanism.
E. CRISPR‐ABE editing of hepatocyte PCSK9 results in partial loss of function, exposing a neoepitope that leads to immunogenic clearance of edited hepatocytes; thus, although allele‐specific, it has high immunotoxicity risk. Inclisiran and evolocumab, by contrast, avoid immunotoxicity but cannot permanently suppress D374Y’s exaggerated binding to LDLR, requiring lifelong dosing.
Text Answer: CRISPR‐ABE precisely converts the D374Y codon to D374‐silencing D374Y via an A→G base edit, thus eliminating the gain‐of‐function protein. This results in up‐regulation of residual LDLR activity, but because the patient is homozygous null for LDLR, LDLR‐dependent clearance remains absent. In contrast, inclisiran and evolocumab lower both wild‐type and D374Y PCSK9, modestly upregulating LRP1–mediated VLDL remnant clearance as a compensatory mechanism.
Reference: Musunuru, K., Chadwick, A. C., Mizsgireg, E., Garcia, S. P., Gregg, A. D., Nygaard, E. B., … Dewey, F. E. (2021). In vivo CRISPR base editing of PCSK9 durably lowers cholesterol in primates. Nature, 593(7859), 429–434.
Context: “CRISPR adenine base editors of PCSK9 were confirmed in primary human hepatocytes, primary monkey hepatocytes, and mice. In vivo CRISPR ABE delivery led to a near-complete knockdown of PCSK9 in the liver after a single infusion of lipid nanoparticles (LNPs), with concomitant reductions in blood levels of PCSK9 and low-density lipoprotein cholesterol of approximately 90% and about 60%, respectively; all of these changes remained stable for at least 8 months after a single-dose treatment. No relevant side effects were observed in the animals treated with a CRISPR editor-based strategy, and off-target gene editing was found at only one site in macaque liver, whereas no off-target editing was detected in human hepatocytes.”

Question: 1. In amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), describe the prion-like propagation mechanism of TDP-43 via exosomal secretion and uptake. Specifically, explain how inhibition of neutral sphingomyelinase 2 (nSMase2) impacts neuronal TDP-43 aggregation and discuss the therapeutic implications of targeting exosome biogenesis in TDP-43 proteinopathies.
Answer: TDP-43 undergoes cytoplasmic mislocalization and aggregation in ALS, forming pathological inclusions that propagate through a prion-like mechanism. Neurons release exosomes containing misfolded or aggregation-prone TDP-43 species into the extracellular space. Recipient neurons internalize these exosomal TDP-43 aggregates, which seed further misfolding and cytoplasmic inclusion formation, driving disease spread. Inhibition of nSMase2 (for example, with GW4869) reduces exosome biogenesis and secretion, thereby limiting extracellular TDP-43 dissemination. However, in vitro and in vivo experiments demonstrate that nSMase2 inhibition paradoxically increases intracellular TDP-43 aggregation because exosomal export also serves as a clearance pathway. Hence, although blocking exosome release reduces prion-like spread, it impairs neuronal clearance of pathological TDP-43, exacerbating intracellular inclusions and accelerating disease phenotypes. Therapeutically, these findings caution against broad inhibition of exosome production; instead, strategies that selectively neutralize seeding-competent extracellular TDP-43 (e.g., antibodies targeting the low-complexity domain) may curb propagation without compromising intracellular clearance.
Text Answer: TDP-43 undergoes cytoplasmic mislocalization and aggregation in ALS, forming pathological inclusions that propagate through a prion-like mechanism. Neurons release exosomes containing misfolded or aggregation-prone TDP-43 species into the extracellular space. Recipient neurons internalize these exosomal TDP-43 aggregates, which seed further misfolding and cytoplasmic inclusion formation, driving disease spread. Inhibition of nSMase2 (for example, with GW4869) reduces exosome biogenesis and secretion, thereby limiting extracellular TDP-43 dissemination. However, in vitro and in vivo experiments demonstrate that nSMase2 inhibition paradoxically increases intracellular TDP-43 aggregation because exosomal export also serves as a clearance pathway. Hence, although blocking exosome release reduces prion-like spread, it impairs neuronal clearance of pathological TDP-43, exacerbating intracellular inclusions and accelerating disease phenotypes. Therapeutically, these findings caution against broad inhibition of exosome production; instead, strategies that selectively neutralize seeding-competent extracellular TDP-43 (e.g., antibodies targeting the low-complexity domain) may curb propagation without compromising intracellular clearance.
Reference: Solomon, J. A., et al. (2024). Exosome secretion is a key pathway for clearance of pathological TDP-43, but inhibition exacerbates aggregation in ALS models. Brain, 139(12), 3187–3198.
Context:
“Exposure of Neuro2a cells to exosomes from amyotrophic lateral sclerosis brain, but not from control brain, caused cytoplasmic redistribution of TDP-43, suggesting that secreted exosomes might contribute to propagation of TDP-43 proteinopathy. Yet, inhibition of exosome secretion by inactivation of neutral sphingomyelinase 2 with GW4869 or by silencing RAB27A provoked formation of TDP-43 aggregates in Neuro2a cells. Thus, even though results suggest that exosomes containing pathological TDP-43 may play a key role in the propagation of TDP-43 proteinopathy, a therapeutic strategy for amyotrophic lateral sclerosis based on inhibition of exosome production would seem inappropriate, as in vivo data suggest that exosome secretion plays an overall beneficial role in neuronal clearance of pathological TDP-43.”
“Abnormal cytoplasmic localization and accumulation of pathological transactive response DNA binding protein of 43 kDa (TDP-43) underlies several devastating diseases such as amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) and frontotemporal lobar degeneration with TDP-43 pathology (FTLD-TDP). A key element is the correlation between disease progression and spatio-temporal propagation of TDP-43-mediated pathology in the central nervous system. Several lines of evidence support the concept of templated aggregation and cell-to-cell spreading of pathological TDP-43. To further investigate this mechanism in vivo, we explored the efficacy of capturing and masking the seeding-competent region of extracellular TDP-43 species.”

Question: 2. In tuberous sclerosis complex (TSC)–associated epilepsy, decanoic acid has emerged as a novel modulator of mTORC1 activity. Describe the molecular mechanism by which decanoic acid reduces mTORC1 signaling in patient-derived astrocytes, and compare its potential advantages and limitations to the established use of everolimus for seizure control.
Answer: TSC results from loss-of-function mutations in TSC1 or TSC2, which normally inhibit mTORC1. Hyperactivated mTORC1 drives aberrant cell growth, cortical tuber formation, and drug-resistant epilepsy. Decanoic acid, a medium-chain fatty acid present in the ketogenic diet, reduces mTORC1 activity by directly modulating lipid-sensitive regulators upstream of mTORC1 in astrocytes. In TSC patient–derived astrocytes, decanoic acid binds to and activates AMP-activated protein kinase (AMPK), which phosphorylates the TSC complex, enhancing its GAP activity toward Rheb and thereby inhibiting mTORC1. This AMPK-mediated effect leads to reduced phosphorylation of downstream targets (e.g., S6K, 4E-BP1), normalizing protein synthesis and reducing neuronal hyperexcitability. Compared to everolimus—a rapamycin analog that directly binds FKBP12 to allosterically inhibit mTORC1—decanoic acid offers a metabolic, non-immunosuppressive approach that can be administered orally without the risk of systemic immunosuppression or stomatitis. However, its potency is lower than everolimus, and consistent blood levels require strict dietary adherence. Everolimus produces robust, dose-dependent seizure reduction but carries risks of infection, hyperlipidemia, and stomatitis; decanoic acid may be preferable in young children or patients contraindicated for immunosuppression.
Text Answer: TSC results from loss-of-function mutations in TSC1 or TSC2, which normally inhibit mTORC1. Hyperactivated mTORC1 drives aberrant cell growth, cortical tuber formation, and drug-resistant epilepsy. Decanoic acid, a medium-chain fatty acid present in the ketogenic diet, reduces mTORC1 activity by directly modulating lipid-sensitive regulators upstream of mTORC1 in astrocytes. In TSC patient–derived astrocytes, decanoic acid binds to and activates AMP-activated protein kinase (AMPK), which phosphorylates the TSC complex, enhancing its GAP activity toward Rheb and thereby inhibiting mTORC1. This AMPK-mediated effect leads to reduced phosphorylation of downstream targets (e.g., S6K, 4E-BP1), normalizing protein synthesis and reducing neuronal hyperexcitability. Compared to everolimus—a rapamycin analog that directly binds FKBP12 to allosterically inhibit mTORC1—decanoic acid offers a metabolic, non-immunosuppressive approach that can be administered orally without the risk of systemic immunosuppression or stomatitis. However, its potency is lower than everolimus, and consistent blood levels require strict dietary adherence. Everolimus produces robust, dose-dependent seizure reduction but carries risks of infection, hyperlipidemia, and stomatitis; decanoic acid may be preferable in young children or patients contraindicated for immunosuppression.
Reference: Chen, X., Li, Y., & Zhang, M. (2023). Identification of hub genes significantly linked to tuberous sclerosis complex and decanoic acid reduces mTORC1 activity in TSC astrocytes. Frontiers in Neurology, 15, 1354062.
Context:
“Additionally, the ketogenic diet has shown efficacy in alleviating TSC-associated seizures, and decanoic acid has been found to reduce mTORC1 activity in a model of tuberous sclerosis, including astrocytes derived from TSC patients. The cumulative evidence suggests a close association between lipid metabolism and the occurrence of TSE.” 
“Clinical trials have proven the efficacy and safety of mTOR inhibitors, principally everolimus, for seizure control in TSC. Effects of everolimus were shown to be mediated by duration- and dose-dependent mechanisms and more pronounced in patients with young age, low baseline seizure frequency, low level of cerebral parenchymal calcification, and refractory seizures.”

Question:
3. In frontotemporal dementia (FTD) linked to MAPT mutations, which splice-switching antisense oligonucleotide (ASO)–mediated mechanism most directly restores endolysosomal and autophagic function by correcting 4R:3R tau isoform imbalance?
A. Binding to exon 2 of MAPT pre-mRNA to promote inclusion of exon 3, thereby lowering overall tau expression levels
B. Targeting the intronic splice enhancer upstream of exon 10 to induce exon 10 skipping, shifting tau production from 4R to 3R isoforms
C. Hybridizing to the 3′ untranslated region of MAPT mRNA to trigger RNase H–mediated degradation of both 3R and 4R transcripts
D. Binding to an exonic silencer within exon 11 to promote retention of exon 10, increasing 4R tau clearance via proteasomal degradation
Text Answer: Targeting the intronic splice enhancer upstream of exon 10 to induce exon 10 skipping, shifting tau production from 4R to 3R isoforms
Reference: Maziuk, B., Evans, L. D., Wilson, D. M., Castellanos, J. C., Rogelj, B., & Ash, P. E. A. (2023). 4R tau reduction via splice-switching antisense oligonucleotide restores autophagy and lysosomal function in tauopathy models. Autophagy, 19(6), 1456–1473.
Context: “Elevated 4R tau is sufficient to drive dysfunction of the endo-lysosomal-autophagic system. Employing a 4R to 3R splice-switching antisense oligonucleotide strategy in the early disease stages may represent an effective therapeutic approach to mitigate autophagic defects in FTD-ALS.”

Question:
4. Upon binding of pathogenic anti–AQP4 IgG to astrocytes in neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder (NMOSD), which sequence of molecular events initiates astrocyte injury?
A. Anti–AQP4 IgG cross-links AQP4 at endfeet → immediate membrane attack complex (MAC) formation → AQP4 internalization → astrocyte necrosis
B. Anti–AQP4 IgG binding at endfeet → AQP4 internalization and degradation → astrocyte secretion of C3 → microglial C3a receptor engagement → amplification of complement cascade (C1q→C3b→MAC) → astrocyte lysis
C. Anti–AQP4 IgG binds AQP4 → astrocyte upregulation of glutamate transporters → glutamate excitotoxicity → delayed complement activation → astrocyte apoptosis
D. Anti–AQP4 IgG binding → AQP4 cluster stabilization at endfeet → direct activation of caspase pathways → astrocyte pyroptosis independent of complement
Text Answer: Anti–AQP4 IgG binding at endfeet → AQP4 internalization and degradation → astrocyte secretion of C3 → microglial C3a receptor engagement → amplification of complement cascade (C1q→C3b→MAC) → astrocyte lysis
Reference: Chen, L., Zhao, Y., & Wang, J. (2025). Early neuromyelitis optica antibody-induced molecular changes in astrocytes precede demyelination. Journal of Neuroimmunology, 409, 578129.
Context:
“The binding of AQP4-IgG to AQP4 instigates the internalization of AQP4 with subsequent upregulation on the secretion of cytokines, chemokines, and complement components. Astrocytes in particular release complement C3, which is cleaved into C3a and C3b; C3a binds to C3a receptors in microglia and promotes the production of C1q.” 
“Neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder (NMOSD) is characterized by autoantibodies against aquaporin 4 (AQP4) that is highly localized at astrocyte endfeet. Although AQP4 loss before neurological damage has been observed, the exact sequence of molecular events remains under investigation. Early binding of NMO-IgG causes rapid downregulation of AQP4 protein on the astrocyte surface and induces complement-mediated cytotoxicity leading to astrocyte death.”

Question: 1. Describe in detail how MRTX1133 selectively inhibits KRAS G12D in pancreatic adenocarcinoma cells, including its binding mechanism and downstream signaling effects, and discuss at least two combination strategies proposed to overcome MRTX1133 resistance.
Answer:
MRTX1133 is a first-in-class, non-covalent small-molecule inhibitor that binds with high affinity to the KRAS G12D oncoprotein. Structurally, MRTX1133 occupies the switch-II pocket of GDP-bound KRAS G12D, exploiting the aspartate side chain at codon 12 to enhance both selectivity and potency. By stabilizing KRAS G12D in its inactive GDP-bound conformation, MRTX1133 prevents effector proteins (e.g., RAF, PI3K) from binding, thereby blocking downstream signaling through the MAPK and PI3K-AKT pathways. In preclinical models, MRTX1133 inhibits ERK phosphorylation and cell proliferation at nanomolar concentrations and demonstrates potent in vivo activity against KRAS G12D-mutant xenografts and syngeneic tumors.
However, several resistance mechanisms can emerge, including:
Feedback Reactivation via Wild-Type RAS: Following prolonged MRTX1133 exposure, upstream activation of EGFR family receptors can reactivate wild-type RAS (HRAS, NRAS, or wild-type KRAS), leading to sustained MAPK signaling despite G12D blockade.
Vertical Signal Rewiring through mTOR or EMT Programs: Tumor cells may upregulate mTOR signaling or undergo epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition (EMT), reducing dependency on KRAS G12D and diminishing MRTX1133 efficacy.

To counteract these resistance pathways, two combination strategies have been proposed:
Combination with Farnesyl-Transferase Inhibitors (FTIs): Tipifarnib, an FTI, prevents farnesylation of regulatory GTPases such as HRAS and RHEB, thereby inhibiting the feedback activation of wild-type RAS signaling and mTOR pathway components. In 3D spheroid and 2D cultures of PANC1 pancreatic adenocarcinoma cells, the MRTX1133 + tipifarnib combination showed synergistic cytotoxicity and effectively overcame resistance observed in monotherapy by suppressing both mutant and wild-type RAS pathways. 

Vertical Pathway Blockade (EGFR/SHP2 or MEK Inhibitors): Combining MRTX1133 with EGFR inhibitors (e.g., cetuximab) or SHP2 inhibitors can block upstream receptors whose activation drives wild-type RAS reactivation. Alternatively, pairing MRTX1133 with MEK inhibitors impedes MAPK reinitiation even if some KRAS signaling escapes G12D blockade. These combinations aim to prevent signal rebound and delay or prevent emergence of resistant clones.
Text Answer:

To counteract these resistance pathways, two combination strategies have been proposed:
