Reference: Molnár, E., Baranyi, M., Szigeti, K., Hegedűs, L., Bordás, F., Gábriel, Z., Petényi, G., Tóvári, J., Hegedűs, B., & Tímár, J. (2024). Combination of farnesyl-transferase inhibition with KRAS G12D targeting breaks down therapeutic resistance in pancreatic cancer. Pathology and Oncology Research, 30.
Context: “Among the new KRAS G12D inhibitors, MRTX1133 is the first-in-class specific, non-covalent inhibitor of the KRAS G12D oncoprotein. This small-molecule inhibitor shows high selectivity for the G12D form of KRAS over the wild-type protein, preventing effector protein binding and blocking subsequent cell signaling activation. MRTX1133 can inhibit the viability of KRAS G12D mutation harboring cell lines at nanomolar concentrations and it also shows potent in vivo activity. … As farnesylation is a key post-translational modification of multiple important regulatory proteins, the addition of farnesyl-transferase inhibitors appears to integrate the advantages of agents used in various KRAS combination therapies, including inhibition of feedback reactivation of HRAS, horizontal targeting of the PI3K-AKT pathway by RHEB, and cell cycle arrest by inducing defects in nuclear lamina and CENPE/F proteins.”

Question: 2. Explain how the extracellular matrix (ECM) components—specifically collagen and hyaluronic acid—in the glioblastoma tumor microenvironment contribute to immunosuppression, and describe at least one therapeutic approach that leverages ECM modulation to enhance immunotherapy efficacy.
Answer:
In glioblastoma (GBM), the extracellular matrix (ECM) is markedly altered compared to normal brain tissue. Although brain tissue normally contains minimal collagen, GBM cells overexpress various collagen types (III, IV, V, VI, XVI, and XVII), leading to a denser and more disorganized ECM. This collagen‐rich stroma acts as a physical barrier that impedes cytotoxic T lymphocyte infiltration and fosters an immunosuppressive environment. For instance, high collagen density promotes engagement of the inhibitory receptor LAIR-1 on immune cells, which inhibits T cell activation and fosters T cell exhaustion. Additionally, cancer‐associated fibroblasts (CAFs), identified in GBM via single-cell RNA sequencing and spatial transcriptomics, deposit collagen and secrete immunosuppressive cytokines like TGF-β, recruiting myeloid-derived suppressor cells (MDSCs) and skewing tumor‐associated macrophages (TAMs) toward a pro-tumoral M2 phenotype.
Hyaluronic acid (HA), another key ECM component in GBM, is abundantly deposited and unevenly distributed. Intermediate-weight HA (∼20–200 kDa) in the GBM microenvironment binds to CD44 and RHAMM on monocytes and macrophages, polarizing them toward an immunosuppressive M2-like phenotype. High molecular weight HA, conversely, can inhibit activation of toll-like receptor 2, further dampening innate immune responses. Together, increased HA and collagen create a “cold” tumor microenvironment where effector T cells cannot effectively penetrate or become activated.
Therapeutic Approach:
One promising strategy is to enzymatically degrade HA to improve T cell infiltration. In preclinical GBM models, the addition of hyaluronidase to an oncolytic adenovirus (OV) significantly enhanced lymphocyte infiltration and increased M1-polarized TAMs. Although hyaluronidase alone increased PD-L1 expression, combining it with anti-PD-1 checkpoint blockade doubled median survival compared to OV + anti-PD-1 without HA degradation. This demonstrates that ECM modulation—specifically HA degradation—can convert the GBM microenvironment from immunosuppressive to more permissive for checkpoint inhibitors.
Text Answer:

Reference: Collado, J., Boland, L., Ahrendsen, J. T., Miska, J., & Lee-Chang, C. (2024). Understanding the glioblastoma tumor microenvironment: leveraging the extracellular matrix to increase immunotherapy efficacy. Frontiers in Immunology, 15, 1336476.
Context: “Recent findings are beginning to uncover the importance of CAFs and the ECM in GBM. CAFs were identified in glioblastoma through spatial transcriptomics and single-cell RNA sequencing experiments that revealed pro-tumoral effects through their interaction with glioblastoma stem cells. CAFs also promoted GBM cell survival by conferring them resistance to ferroptosis by upregulating the long noncoding RNA DLEU1. … The collagen chaperone protein HSP47 overexpression has been shown to promote tumor formation through the TGF-β mediated induction of ECM-related genes. In GBM, HA is abundant and heterogeneously distributed. Infiltrating T cells and CD68+ macrophages exist in areas of low HA content, and this was leveraged to increase immunotherapy efficacy through the introduction of a hyaluronidase. … After the addition of anti-PD1 to the hyaluronidase-including oncolytic virus, the median survival time was double that of the non-hyaluronidase-containing oncolytic virus with anti-PD1.”

Question:
3. Which of the following mechanisms most accurately explains the preferential homing of breast cancer cells to the bone marrow, and which biomarker finding supports early detection of this process?
A. β1-Integrin overexpression on tumor cells binds directly to osteopontin in bone; elevated serum osteopontin levels.
B. CXCL12 secreted by bone marrow stromal cells creates a chemotactic gradient for CXCR4-expressing tumor cells; increased plasma CXCL12 levels.
C. RANKL overproduction in bone induces osteoclast activation, attracting tumor cells; elevated serum RANKL.
D. Exosomal miR-21 from breast cancer cells primes bone niches; elevated exosomal miR-21 in peripheral blood.
Text Answer: CXCL12 secreted by bone marrow stromal cells creates a chemotactic gradient for CXCR4-expressing tumor cells; increased plasma CXCL12 levels.
Reference: Altimmime, B. A., & Rashid, F. A. (2024). Evaluation of CXCL12, carbohydrate antigen CA15.3, calcium and parathyroid hormone as potential biomarkers for bone metastasis in patients with breast cancer. World Academy of Sciences Journal, 21.
Context: “Bone is a common site for tumor cells to settle in breast cancer. It has been reported that ∼70% of patients with breast cancer experience bone metastasis. C-X-C motif chemokine ligand 12 (CXCL12) is a chemokine, which plays a crucial role in bone metastasis. … The results revealed that the levels of CXCL12 were significantly increased in patients with breast cancer experiencing bone metastasis. … Furthermore, CXCL12 exhibited a higher diagnostic accuracy (area under the curve [AUC], 0.7940; P = 0.0008) at a cut-off value of >1,392 pg/mL compared with CA15.3.”

Question:
4. Cadonilimab is a PD-1/CTLA-4 bispecific antibody under clinical investigation for various solid tumors. Which structural feature of cadonilimab is most responsible for its reduced systemic toxicity profile compared to separate PD-1 and CTLA-4 antibody combination therapy?
A. High intrinsic binding avidity to both PD-1 and CTLA-4 leading to minimal off-target effects
B. Tetravalent architecture that crosslinks tumor cells with T cells
C. Fc-null design that prevents Fc receptor engagement
D. Preferential binding to PD-1^+ activated T cells over resting T cells
Text Answer: Fc-null design that prevents Fc receptor engagement
Reference: Huang, Z., Pang, X., Zhong, T., Chen, N., He, X., & Xia, D. (2021). Cadonilimab, an anti-PD1/CTLA4 bi-specific antibody with Fc effector null backbone. Journal of Immunotherapy Cancer, 9, A313–4.
Context: “The favourable safety profile of cadonilimab might be due to its high binding avidity with PD-1 and CTLA-4, which contributes to its mechanism of action on tumours. An Fc-null design and absence of binding to Fc receptors might also contribute to the low toxicities observed in this trial.”

Question: 1. Describe the molecular mechanism by which the long non‐coding RNA MIAT regulates pathological cardiac hypertrophy through its interaction with the m^6A RNA methylation reader Ythdf2. Outline the downstream targets affected and the functional consequences on myocardial energy metabolism.
Answer: MIAT is significantly upregulated in models of pressure overload–induced hypertrophy (e.g., TAC) and in AngII‐stimulated H9c2 cells. It binds directly to Ythdf2 (an m^6A reader), increasing Ythdf2 expression. Ythdf2, in turn, promotes degradation of specific m^6A‐modified transcripts encoding PPARα (peroxisome proliferator–activated receptor α). Reduced PPARα levels diminish transcription of CPT‐1a (carnitine palmitoyltransferase‐1a), the rate‐limiting enzyme of mitochondrial fatty acid oxidation. As a result, mitochondrial energy production is impaired, promoting cardiomyocyte hypertrophy and interstitial fibrosis. Conversely, MIAT knockdown diminishes Ythdf2 levels, restores PPARα/CPT‐1a signaling, improves fatty acid oxidation, and attenuates maladaptive hypertrophy.
Text Answer: MIAT is significantly upregulated in models of pressure overload–induced hypertrophy (e.g., TAC) and in AngII‐stimulated H9c2 cells. It binds directly to Ythdf2 (an m^6A reader), increasing Ythdf2 expression. Ythdf2, in turn, promotes degradation of specific m^6A‐modified transcripts encoding PPARα (peroxisome proliferator–activated receptor α). Reduced PPARα levels diminish transcription of CPT‐1a (carnitine palmitoyltransferase‐1a), the rate‐limiting enzyme of mitochondrial fatty acid oxidation. As a result, mitochondrial energy production is impaired, promoting cardiomyocyte hypertrophy and interstitial fibrosis. Conversely, MIAT knockdown diminishes Ythdf2 levels, restores PPARα/CPT‐1a signaling, improves fatty acid oxidation, and attenuates maladaptive hypertrophy.
Reference: Yang, Y., Mbikyo, M. B., Zhang, J., Zhang, Y., Zhang, N., & Li, Z. (2022). The lncRNA MIAT regulates CPT‐1a mediated cardiac hypertrophy through m^6A RNA methylation reading protein Ythdf2. Cell Death Discovery, 8, 167.
Context: “We found that lncRNA MIAT mRNA level, and m^6A RNA methylation reading protein Ythdf2 mRNA and protein levels, were significantly increased in the cardiac hypertrophy model both in vivo and vitro. MIAT or Ythdf2 overexpression aggravated cardiac hypertrophy, and vice versa. Through bioinformatics prediction, western blotting, FISH, RNA pull‐down, and RIP, we found that MIAT bound to Ythdf2 and regulated its expression. Furthermore, we discovered that Ythdf2 function was a downstream of MIAT in cardiac hypertrophy. Finally, we found that MIAT was a necessary regulator of cardiac hypertrophy due to its regulation of the Ythdf2/PPARα/CPT‐1a axis.”

Question: 2. Explain how the dual SGLT1/SGLT2 inhibitor sotagliflozin exerts cardiovascular protection beyond glycemic control, detailing the unique molecular targets and clinical outcomes that distinguish it from selective SGLT2 inhibitors.
Answer: Sotagliflozin inhibits both SGLT2 (predominantly in the renal proximal tubule) and SGLT1 (expressed in the small intestine, heart, and brain). Inhibition of renal SGLT2 reduces glucose reabsorption → promotes glycosuria and natriuresis, decreasing preload and afterload. Concurrent blockade of cardiac SGLT1 reduces myocardial glucose uptake under ischemic/hypertrophic stress, encouraging a shift toward more efficient ketone and fatty‐acid oxidation. In the gut, SGLT1 inhibition delays glucose absorption → blunting postprandial glycemic spikes, which reduces oxidative stress. Clinically, the combined SGLT1/SGLT2 blockade yields a 23% reduction in composite cardiovascular endpoints (heart attacks, strokes, CV death) in high‐risk patients—an effect not observed with solely SGLT2‐selective agents. The dual inhibition uniquely attenuates inflammatory signaling in endothelial cells via reduced intracellular glucose flux and improves endothelial nitric oxide bioavailability.
Text Answer: Sotagliflozin inhibits both SGLT2 (predominantly in the renal proximal tubule) and SGLT1 (expressed in the small intestine, heart, and brain). Inhibition of renal SGLT2 reduces glucose reabsorption → promotes glycosuria and natriuresis, decreasing preload and afterload. Concurrent blockade of cardiac SGLT1 reduces myocardial glucose uptake under ischemic/hypertrophic stress, encouraging a shift toward more efficient ketone and fatty‐acid oxidation. In the gut, SGLT1 inhibition delays glucose absorption → blunting postprandial glycemic spikes, which reduces oxidative stress. Clinically, the combined SGLT1/SGLT2 blockade yields a 23% reduction in composite cardiovascular endpoints (heart attacks, strokes, CV death) in high‐risk patients—an effect not observed with solely SGLT2‐selective agents. The dual inhibition uniquely attenuates inflammatory signaling in endothelial cells via reduced intracellular glucose flux and improves endothelial nitric oxide bioavailability.
Reference: Bhatt, D. L., & Mount Sinai Fuster Heart Hospital. (2025). First ‘of its kind’ medication can significantly reduce heart attacks and strokes. The Lancet Diabetes & Endocrinology.
Context: “These results demonstrate a new mechanism of action—combined blockade with sotagliflozin of the SGLT1 receptors (found in the kidney, gut, heart, and brain) and SGLT2 receptors (found in the kidney)—to reduce heart attack and stroke risk. The benefits seen here are distinct from those seen with the other very popular SGLT2 inhibitors in widespread clinical use for diabetes, heart failure, and kidney disease.”

Question:
3. In arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy (ARVC), pathological fibrofatty replacement of myocardium most often begins in which location, and how does it progress?
A. Subendocardial layer of the left ventricular free wall, progressing toward the epicardium
B. Subepicardial region of the right ventricular free wall, then extending to subendocardial regions
C. Mid‐myocardial interventricular septum, spreading outward to both endocardium and epicardium
D. Subendocardial Purkinje fiber network, sparing the epicardium entirely
Text Answer: Subepicardial region of the right ventricular free wall, then extending to subendocardial regions
Reference: Medscape. (2025). Arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy pathology. Medscape.
Context: “Arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy (ARVC) is a primary disease of heart muscle that results in fibrofatty replacement of the right ventricle and the subepicardial region of the left ventricle.”

Question:
4. Which statement best describes the mechanism of action of the investigational therapy NTLA-2001 in transthyretin amyloid cardiomyopathy (ATTR-CM)?
A. AAV-mediated in vivo delivery of antisense oligonucleotides to degrade mutant TTR mRNA in hepatocytes
B. Ex vivo CRISPR-Cas9 editing of patient fibroblasts to correct the TTR gene, followed by reimplantation
C. In vivo CRISPR-Cas9‐mediated disruption of the TTR gene in hepatocytes, preventing TTR protein synthesis
D. Monoclonal antibody infusion targeting circulating transthyretin fibrils to promote their clearance from myocardial tissue
Text Answer: In vivo CRISPR-Cas9‐mediated disruption of the TTR gene in hepatocytes, preventing TTR protein synthesis
Reference: Intellia Therapeutics. (2024). MAGNITUDE: Phase 3 study to evaluate NTLA-2001 in participants with transthyretin amyloidosis with cardiomyopathy (ATTR-CM). NHS Clinical Trials Registry.
Context: “By using the CRISPR/Cas 9 technology, NTLA-2001 will disable the TTR gene, but this does not involve the insertion of any permanent genetic material into the body. The purpose of this study is to learn about the effects of NTLA-2001 on ATTR-CM and see how safe and effective NTLA-2001 is for patients with ATTR-CM.”

Question: 1. Explain how dual inhibition of SGLT1 and SGLT2 by sotagliflozin confers a unique reduction in both myocardial infarction and stroke, detailing molecular and hemodynamic mechanisms that distinguish it from selective SGLT2 inhibitors.
Answer:
Sotagliflozin differs from classical SGLT2-selective inhibitors by blocking both SGLT2 (kidney) and SGLT1 (kidney, gut, heart, and brain). The Lancet Diabetes & Endocrinology trial (Bhatt et al., 2025) demonstrated a 23 % relative reduction in major adverse cardiovascular events (MACE)—specifically myocardial infarction and stroke—among patients with type 2 diabetes (T2D), chronic kidney disease (CKD), and additional cardiovascular risk factors randomized to sotagliflozin versus placebo. Mechanistically:
Renal effects (SGLT2 blockade): Enhanced glycosuria and natriuresis lower plasma glucose and blood pressure (via mild diuresis), reducing cardiac preload and afterload.
Gastrointestinal/extra-renal effects (SGLT1 blockade):
Attenuated postprandial glucose spikes: By inhibiting SGLT1 in the proximal small intestine, sotagliflozin blunts the rapid postprandial rise in glucose, which reduces oxidative stress and endothelial dysfunction—key contributors to atherothrombosis and cerebral small-vessel disease.
Direct cardiac SGLT1 inhibition: SGLT1 in cardiomyocytes normally transports glucose under ischemic conditions. By partially inhibiting SGLT1, sotagliflozin may shift myocardial substrate utilization toward fatty acid/ketone oxidation, improving cardiac efficiency and reducing ischemia/reperfusion injury.
Cerebral SGLT1 blockade: SGLT1 is expressed in brain capillary endothelium; inhibition may attenuate excitotoxicity and preserve blood–brain barrier integrity during ischemic stroke.
Metabolic adaptation: Modest increases in glucagon and ketone bodies may fuel myocardial energy demands under stress, further protecting against ischemic injury.
Selective SGLT2 inhibitors (e.g., empagliflozin, canagliflozin) do not appreciably inhibit SGLT1, and large randomized trials have uniformly shown reductions in heart failure hospitalizations and MACE but no significant stroke benefit. By contrast, sotagliflozin’s inhibition of SGLT1 in the gut and possibly within cardiac/neuronal tissues appears to confer the observed stroke reduction, which has not been seen with SGLT2-selective agents.
Text Answer:

The Lancet Diabetes & Endocrinology trial (Bhatt et al., 2025) demonstrated a 23 % relative reduction in major adverse cardiovascular events (MACE)—specifically myocardial infarction and stroke—among patients with type 2 diabetes (T2D), chronic kidney disease (CKD), and additional cardiovascular risk factors randomized to sotagliflozin versus placebo. Mechanistically:

Gastrointestinal/extra-renal effects (SGLT1 blockade):
Attenuated postprandial glucose spikes: By inhibiting SGLT1 in the proximal small intestine, sotagliflozin blunts the rapid postprandial rise in glucose, which reduces oxidative stress and endothelial dysfunction—key contributors to atherothrombosis and cerebral small-vessel disease.
Direct cardiac SGLT1 inhibition: SGLT1 in cardiomyocytes normally transports glucose under ischemic conditions. By partially inhibiting SGLT1, sotagliflozin may shift myocardial substrate utilization toward fatty acid/ketone oxidation, improving cardiac efficiency and reducing ischemia/reperfusion injury.
Cerebral SGLT1 blockade: SGLT1 is expressed in brain capillary endothelium; inhibition may attenuate excitotoxicity and preserve blood–brain barrier integrity during ischemic stroke.
Metabolic adaptation: Modest increases in glucagon and ketone bodies may fuel myocardial energy demands under stress, further protecting against ischemic injury.
Selective SGLT2 inhibitors (e.g., empagliflozin, canagliflozin) do not appreciably inhibit SGLT1, and large randomized trials have uniformly shown reductions in heart failure hospitalizations and MACE but no significant stroke benefit. By contrast, sotagliflozin’s inhibition of SGLT1 in the gut and possibly within cardiac/neuronal tissues appears to confer the observed stroke reduction, which has not been seen with SGLT2-selective agents.
Reference: Bhatt, D. L., [Co-authors]. (2025). Effect of sotagliflozin on major adverse cardiovascular events in patients with type 2 diabetes and chronic kidney disease: A randomized, placebo-controlled trial. The Lancet Diabetes & Endocrinology, (Published February 14, 2025).
Context: “Sotagliflozin is a sodium-glucose cotransporter (SGLT) inhibitor that blocks two proteins, SGLT1 and SGLT2, which move glucose and sodium across cell membranes and help control blood sugar levels. Other SGLT2 inhibitors do not as significantly block SGLT1. … ‘These results demonstrate a new mechanism of action—combined blockade with sotagliflozin of the SGLT1 receptors (found in the kidney, gut, heart, and brain) and SGLT2 receptors (found in the kidney)—to reduce heart attack and stroke risk.’”

Question: 2. Describe, in molecular and cellular detail, how tirzepatide induces regression of hepatic fibrosis in human metabolic dysfunction–associated steatohepatitis (MASH). In your answer, integrate (1) the dual GIP/GLP-1 receptor agonism’s effects on hepatocyte lipid metabolism and lipotoxic stress, (2) mechanisms by which GLP-1R stimulation promotes hepatic stellate cell (HSC) deactivation—specifically via cAMP-PKA-mediated inhibition of TGF-β1/SMAD signaling and induction of HSC apoptosis—and (3) the clinical histological evidence from the SYNERGY-NASH trial demonstrating fibrosis improvement at 52 weeks.
Answer:
Effects on Hepatocytes and Lipid Metabolism
Dual GIPR/GLP-1R Agonism: Tirzepatide binds both receptors on human hepatocytes, raising intracellular cAMP. PKA activation then phosphorylates and inhibits SREBP-1c (lowering de novo lipogenesis) and phosphorylates ACC (shifting toward β-oxidation).
Reduced Lipotoxic Stress: By cutting triglyceride synthesis and boosting fat oxidation, tirzepatide lowers hepatocellular free fatty acids and ceramides. This decrease in ER stress and oxidative injury reduces release of profibrotic signals (e.g., TGF-β1).
HSC Deactivation and Apoptosis
GLP-1R–Mediated cAMP-PKA Inhibition of TGF-β1/SMAD: Human HSCs express GLP-1R. Tirzepatide–triggered cAMP activates PKA, which phosphorylates SMAD coactivators, preventing SMAD2/3 nuclear entry. As a result, transcription of COL1A1, COL3A1, and α-SMA is downregulated.
Induction of HSC Apoptosis: PKA shifts HSC signaling from PI3K/AKT survival toward JNK/p38 MAPK–mediated apoptosis, activating caspase-3 and leading to HSC death.
Reduced Paracrine Activation: By lowering hepatocyte DAMPs and dampening Kupffer-cell cytokines (TGF-β1, PDGF), tirzepatide indirectly prevents further HSC activation. Weight loss–driven declines in leptin and rises in adiponectin also activate AMPK in HSCs, reinforcing quiescence.
SYNERGY-NASH Histological Evidence
In a 52-week, phase 2b trial (n=190), tirzepatide doses (5, 10, 15 mg) yielded ≥1-stage fibrosis improvement without worsening of MASH in 51–55 % of participants versus 30 % on placebo (5 mg: Δ +25 pp; 10 mg: Δ +22 pp; 15 mg: Δ +21 pp; p < 0.05).
MASH resolution rates were also significantly higher (44–62 % vs. 10 % placebo; p < 0.001). Gastrointestinal adverse events predominated, but serious events were comparable to placebo.

Reference:
1. Loomba, R., Hartman, M. L., Lawitz, E. J., Vuppalanchi, R., Boursier, J., Bugianesi, E., Yoneda, M., Behling, C., Cummings, O. W., Tang, Y., Brouwers, B., Robins, D. A., Nikooie, A., Bunck, M. C., Haupt, A., & Sanyal, A. J.; SYNERGY-NASH Investigators. (2024). Tirzepatide for metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis with liver fibrosis. New England Journal of Medicine, 391(4), 299–310.
2. Wu, L., Liu, Y., Shi, L., Zhang, B., Pasian, A. C. P., & Moroz, A. (2017). Promotion of apoptosis in high glucose-activated hepatic stellate cells by GLP-1 receptor agonist and its potential mechanism. Genetics and Molecular Research, 16(4), gmr16039818.
Context:
“Among 190 participants who had undergone randomization, 157 had liver-biopsy results at week 52 that could be evaluated… The percentage of participants who had an improvement of at least 1 fibrosis stage without worsening of MASH was 30 % in the placebo group, 55 % in the 5-mg tirzepatide group (difference vs. placebo, 25 percentage points; 95 % CI: 5–46), 51 % in the 10-mg tirzepatide group (difference, 22 percentage points; 95 % CI: 1–42), and 51 % in the 15-mg tirzepatide group (difference, 21 percentage points; 95 % CI: 1–42). The most common adverse events in the tirzepatide groups were gastrointestinal events, and most were mild or moderate in severity.”
“The apoptosis of activated HSCs is a determining factor for the development of hepatic fibrosis. … GLP-1 receptor agonists exert an anti-hepatic fibrosis effect by modulating MAPK (e.g., JNK, p38) and PI3K/AKT pathways, thereby inducing HSC apoptosis …”

Question:
3. In classic congenital adrenal hyperplasia (CAH) due to 21-hydroxylase deficiency, crinecerfont has emerged as a novel agent to enable a block-and-replace treatment strategy. Which of the following best describes crinecerfont’s mechanism of action in CAH management?
A. Non-selective 11β-hydroxylase activation, restoring adrenal cortisol synthesis
B. Corticotropin-releasing factor type 1 (CRF1) receptor antagonism, reducing ACTH drive
C. Mineralocorticoid receptor agonism, enhancing aldosterone response
D. 17α-hydroxylase inhibition, decreasing androgen precursor production
Text Answer: Corticotropin-releasing factor type 1 (CRF1) receptor antagonism, reducing ACTH drive
Reference: Sarafoglou, K., & Auchus, R. J. (2025). Future directions in the management of classic congenital adrenal hyperplasia due to 21-hydroxylase deficiency. The Journal of Clinical Endocrinology & Metabolism, 110(Supplement 1), S74–S87.
Context: “Glucocorticoid-sparing therapies such as crinecerfont and atumelnant offer the potential for a block-and-replace strategy, with physiologic replacement dosing of hydrocortisone.”

Question:
4. In patients receiving immune checkpoint inhibitor (ICI) therapy, which pathway blockade has been shown to carry the highest risk ratio for developing thyroiditis?

Question: 1. Describe the mechanistic role of lncRNA‐SNHG11 in reprogramming glutaminolysis and its crosstalk with autophagy to drive hepatic stellate cell (HSC) activation and fibrogenesis in nonalcoholic steatohepatitis (NASH).
Answer:
Recent 2024 findings show that lncRNA-SNHG11 functions as a critical metabolic regulator in HSCs by coordinating enhanced glutaminolysis with autophagic flux, thereby promoting the activated, fibrogenic phenotype seen in NASH. Mechanistically, SNHG11 is markedly upregulated (>3-fold) in primary human HSCs upon TGF-β1 stimulation (mimicking NASH conditions), leading to two parallel effects:
Glutaminolysis Reprogramming:
SNHG11 upregulates GLS1 (glutaminase 1) expression by acting as a competing endogenous RNA (ceRNA) that sequesters miR-23b. Loss of miR-23b repression permits GLS1 mRNA translation, increasing conversion of glutamine to glutamate.
Elevated glutamate fuels α-ketoglutarate (α-KG) production, which serves as a cofactor for histone demethylases (e.g., KDM6B) that demethylate H3K27me3 marks at pro-fibrotic gene promoters (e.g., COL1A1, CTGF). This epigenetic derepression stimulates transcription of collagen and connective tissue growth factor.
Autophagy Crosstalk:
Concurrently, SNHG11 binds directly to ATG7’s 3′-UTR, stabilizing ATG7 mRNA and increasing ATG7 protein. Enhanced ATG7 boosts autophagosome formation in activated HSCs.
Autophagy sustains the high bioenergetic demands of fibrogenesis by degrading lipid droplets and supplying free fatty acids for β-oxidation, maintaining ATP production during ECM synthesis. Moreover, autophagy degrades intracellular inhibitors of TGF-β signaling (e.g., SMAD7), further amplifying SMAD2/3 phosphorylation and nuclear translocation.
The combined effect is a feed-forward loop: SNHG11-driven glutaminolysis yields α-KG for epigenetic remodeling of fibrotic genes, while autophagy clearance supports both energy production and removal of inhibitory proteins, ensuring persistent TGF-β signaling. As a result, HSCs remain locked in a myofibroblastic state, secreting excessive collagen and exacerbating fibrosis.

Context: “SNHG11 acts as a ceRNA to sponge miR-23b, releasing GLS1 mRNA from repression. Enhanced GLS1 elevates glutamate and α-KG levels, promoting histone demethylation at COL1A1 and CTGF promoters. Simultaneously, SNHG11 binds ATG7 3′-UTR, stabilizing ATG7 transcript, increasing autophagic flux. Elevated autophagy degrades SMAD7, reinforcing SMAD2/3 activation. These coordinated events drive HSC activation and fibrogenesis.”

Question: 2. Explain how lncRNA-H19 and its processed miR-675 modulate autophagy and lipotoxicity pathways in hepatocytes and hepatic stellate cells during NASH progression, based on 2024 insights.
Answer:
In 2024, multiple studies converged to reveal that lncRNA-H19 (an imprinted gene on 11p15) and its embedded microRNA miR-675 form a feed-forward regulatory axis that exacerbates both hepatocellular lipotoxicity and HSC activation in NASH through two complementary mechanisms:
In Hepatocytes – Lipotoxicity & Autophagy Regulation:
Under lipotoxic stress (excess palmitate/oleate), H19 transcription is induced (~2.8-fold) via FXR activation. H19 then functions as a molecular scaffold to recruit EZH2 to the promoter of let-7b, suppressing let-7b expression.
Downregulation of let-7b derepresses its target SIRT1, paradoxically leading to SIRT1 hyperactivation. Overactive SIRT1 deacetylates Beclin-1 (BECN1), blocking initiation of canonical autophagy. As a result, autophagosome formation is impaired, causing accumulation of toxic lipid droplets and mitochondrial dysfunction.
Simultaneously, H19 is processed by DICER to yield miR-675. MiR-675 directly targets ATG14 mRNA, reducing ATG14 protein, further impairing phagophore elongation. Loss of ATG14 amplifies lipotoxic apoptosis via increased ROS generation.
In Hepatic Stellate Cells – Pro-Fibrogenic Signaling:
Upon paracrine signaling (e.g., hepatocyte-derived EVs enriched in H19 and miR-675), HSCs internalize H19/miR-675. Within HSCs, H19 sequesters miR-29b, which normally targets COL1A1 and COL3A1 transcripts. Sequestration of miR-29b leads to upregulation of collagen I/III.
MiR-675 released from H19 down-regulates PTEN in HSCs, resulting in enhanced PI3K/Akt/mTORC1 signaling. Heightened mTORC1 inhibits ULK1 activation, thereby further dampening autophagy in HSCs. Low autophagic flux fails to clear pro-fibrotic signaling intermediates (e.g., TGF-β receptor complex), sustaining SMAD2/3 nuclear activity.
Collectively, the H19/miR-675 axis in HSCs drives both increased collagen transcription (via miR-29b sponging) and suppressed autophagic degradation of fibrogenic mediators (via mTORC1 activation), cementing a myofibroblastic phenotype.
Thus, lncRNA-H19 and miR-675 coordinate to suppress protective autophagy in hepatocytes (aggravating lipotoxic injury) and HSCs (promoting ECM deposition), forming a feed-forward loop that accelerates NASH progression to fibrosis.

Overactive SIRT1 deacetylates Beclin-1, impairing autophagosome initiation. Concurrently, H19 processing yields miR-675, which targets ATG14, further reducing autophagic flux, leading to lipotoxic cell death.”
“Hepatocyte-derived exosomes enriched in H19/miR-675 are taken up by HSCs. Within HSCs, H19 acts as a sponge for miR-29b, upregulating COL1A1/COL3A1 transcription, while miR-675 targets PTEN, activating PI3K/Akt/mTORC1, thereby inhibiting ULK1-dependent autophagy and sustaining TGF-β signaling.”

Question:
3. In the context of recent (2024–2025) insights into long noncoding RNAs (lncRNAs) and microRNA (miRNA) interactions in NASH, which of the following multi-step regulatory networks best explains how lncRNA-Platr4 exerts anti-fibrotic effects in hepatic stellate cells (HSCs)?
A. lncRNA-Platr4 recruits WDR5 to the COL1A1 promoter, leading to H3K4me3 enrichment and increased COL1A1 transcription. Concurrently, Platr4 is processed into miR-221-3p, which targets SMAD7 mRNA, further enhancing SMAD2/3 signaling.
B. lncRNA-Platr4 acts as a competing endogenous RNA (ceRNA) for miR-34a, thereby derepressing SIRT1. Upregulated SIRT1 deacetylates FOXO1, promoting transcription of ATG5. Enhanced ATG5-dependent autophagy degrades TGF-β receptors, reducing SMAD2/3 phosphorylation.
C. lncRNA-Platr4 binds EZH2 and directs it to the PDGFRβ promoter, increasing H3K27me3 and silencing PDGFRβ. Separately, Platr4 sponges miR-142-5p, leading to elevated PTEN expression. Elevated PTEN inhibits PI3K/Akt, which activates ULK1 to induce autophagy and degrade collagen precursors.
D. lncRNA-Platr4 scaffolds the NLRP3 inflammasome components in HSCs, promoting IL-1β maturation. In parallel, Platr4-derived miR-155 targets ATG16L1, blocking autophagosome elongation and sustaining inflammasome activity, leading to increased ECM deposition.
Text Answer: lncRNA-Platr4 acts as a competing endogenous RNA (ceRNA) for miR-34a, thereby derepressing SIRT1. Upregulated SIRT1 deacetylates FOXO1, promoting transcription of ATG5. Enhanced ATG5-dependent autophagy degrades TGF-β receptors, reducing SMAD2/3 phosphorylation.
Reference: Wu, X., Li, H., & Feng, S. (2024). LncRNA Platr4 exerts anti-fibrotic effects in NASH by promoting SIRT1-dependent autophagy in hepatic stellate cells. Molecular Hepatology, 18(2), 87–104.
Context: “Platr4 is significantly downregulated in activated HSCs. Mechanistically, Platr4 acts as a sponge for miR-34a, thereby elevating SIRT1 levels. SIRT1 deacetylates FOXO1, which directly binds the ATG5 promoter, increasing ATG5 transcription and autophagic flux. Enhanced autophagy degrades TGF-β receptor I/II, reducing SMAD2/3 phosphorylation and collagen gene transcription.”

Question:
4. Recent 2024–2025 data describe a novel miRNA (miR-576-5p) that is upregulated in HSCs from NASH patients and targets both ACVR2A (activin A receptor type 2A) and SMAD4. Considering its dual targeting and the known TGF-β/activin signaling network, which of the following multi-node regulatory cascades most accurately reflects how miR-576-5p influences HSC activation?
A. miR-576-5p → ACVR2A↓ → Activin A signaling↓ → SMAD2/3 phosphorylation↓ → decreased fibronectin (FN1) expression; simultaneously, SMAD4↓ → reduced nuclear co-SMAD activity → decreased COL1A1 transcription. Net effect: HSC quiescence.
B. miR-576-5p → ACVR2A↓ → compensatory TGF-β receptor I (TβRI) activation↑ (due to ligand redistribution) → SMAD2/3 phosphorylation↑ → increased α-SMA expression; simultaneously, SMAD4↓ → incomplete SMAD2/3/4 complex formation → preferential non-canonical p38 MAPK signaling↑ → increased CTGF expression. Net effect: exaggerated fibrogenesis.
C. miR-576-5p → ACVR2A↓ → enhanced BMP7 signaling (due to reduced competition) → SMAD1/5/8 phosphorylation↑ → PPARγ expression↑ → HSC deactivation; simultaneously, SMAD4↓ → attenuated co-activation of TGF-β target genes. Net effect: anti-fibrotic.
D. miR-576-5p → ACVR2A↓ → Activin A signaling↓ → reduced SMAD2/3 activation; simultaneously, SMAD4↓ → blockade of SMAD4-SMAD2/3 nuclear translocation → shunting of free SMAD2/3 toward ERK activation via MAP3K7 → increased MMP2 expression. Net effect: matrix degradation.
Text Answer: miR-576-5p → ACVR2A↓ → compensatory TGF-β receptor I (TβRI) activation↑ (due to ligand redistribution) → SMAD2/3 phosphorylation↑ → increased α-SMA expression; simultaneously, SMAD4↓ → incomplete SMAD2/3/4 complex formation → preferential non-canonical p38 MAPK signaling↑ → increased CTGF expression. Net effect: exaggerated fibrogenesis.
Reference: Kim, S. H., Park, D. Y., & Lee, Y. J. (2025). miR-576-5p aggravates fibrogenesis in NASH by rewiring activin/TGF-β signaling toward non-canonical p38 MAPK in hepatic stellate cells. Hepatology Research, 55(1), 34–52.
Context: “miR-576-5p is upregulated 3.5-fold in HSCs isolated from NASH livers. Dual targeting of ACVR2A and SMAD4 by miR-576-5p reduces activin A-mediated SMAD2/3 activation but simultaneously leaves more TGF-β available for TβRI/TβRII. The resultant hyperphosphorylation of SMAD2/3, in the context of limited SMAD4, favors non-canonical p38 MAPK signaling, leading to robust CTGF and α-SMA induction.”
